# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BASK Transmitter — Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the architecture of a BASK transmitter (Product Modulator / Switching Modulator approach).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Binary Data Source\n0 and 1 bits"] --> B["NRZ Encoder\nUnipolar Pulse Train"]
    B --> C["Multiplier Block\nBalanced Modulator"]
    D["Local Oscillator\nCarrier A_c cos 2pi f_c t"] --> C
    C --> E["Bandpass Filter\nCenter f_c Bandwidth 2R_b"]
    E --> F["Channel Output\nBASK Signal s t"]
    
    G["Alternative Path"] -.-> H["Switching Modulator\nFET or PIN switch"]
    B --> H
    D --> H
    H --> F
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style E fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    style F fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000
    style H fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40,color:#000
```

**Operation:**
- The **multiplier path** (top) generates DSB-SC-like BASK with a small residual carrier.
- The **switching modulator path** (bottom) literally switches the carrier ON/OFF using an electronic switch (FET, transistor, or PIN diode) — this produces true OOK with cleaner OFF state.

## 4.2 BASK Receiver — Coherent Demodulator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Received Signal\nr t = s t + n t"] --> B["Bandpass Filter\nCenter f_c"]
    B --> C["Multiplier\nCoherent Detector"]
    D["Local Oscillator\nSynchronous Carrier\ncos 2pi f_c t"] --> C
    C --> E["Lowpass Filter\nIntegrator over T_b"]
    E --> F["Sampler\nAt t = nT_b"]
    F --> G["Decision Device\nThreshold lambda"]
    G --> H["Output Binary\n0 or 1"]
    
    I["Synchronization\nCarrier Recovery PLL"] -.-> D
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style E fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    style F fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000
    style G fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40,color:#000
    style H fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style I fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#263238,color:#000
```

**Key Components:**
- **BPF:** Limits noise bandwidth before detection.
- **Coherent multiplier:** Recovers the baseband by mixing with a locally generated carrier synchronized in frequency **and phase**.
- **LPF (Integrator):** Averages the demodulated signal over one bit period to maximize SNR.
- **Decision device:** Compares the sampled output to a threshold `\lambda` and outputs 0 or 1.

## 4.3 BASK Receiver — Non-Coherent (Envelope Detector)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Received Signal\nr t = s t + n t"] --> B["Bandpass Filter\nCenter f_c"]
    B --> C["Envelope Detector\nDiode + RC Lowpass"]
    C --> D["Sampler\nAt t = nT_b"]
    D --> E["Decision Device\nThreshold lambda"]
    E --> F["Output Binary\n0 or 1"]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style D fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    style E fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000
    style F fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40,color:#000
```

**Operation:** The envelope detector extracts the **amplitude envelope** of the modulated signal. During bit '1', a non-zero DC level appears; during bit '0', only noise remains. The decision device discriminates based on this DC level.

> [!TIP]
> **Why non-coherent?** Envelope detection avoids the need for a **synchronized local oscillator** (no PLL required). This makes the receiver simple, cheap, and ideal for OOK remote-control applications. However, the BER is worse (3 dB penalty).

## 4.4 BASK Waveform — Time Domain Sketch (Logical Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Binary\n1 0 1 1 0 0 1"] --> B["NRZ Pulse\n+1 for 1, 0 for 0"]
    B --> C["Carrier\nA_c cos 2pi f_c t"]
    A --> C
    C --> D["BASK Output\nBursts of carrier for 1, off for 0"]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style D fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visualize the time-domain waveform:** For input `1011001`, the BASK output is:
> - Bit 1: Several cycles of `\cos(2\pi f_c t)` at amplitude `A_c`
> - Bit 0: Zero amplitude (flat line)
> - Bit 1: Carrier bursts again
> - ... and so on.
>
> The **envelope** of the BASK signal is a **unipolar NRZ waveform** identical to the original data — this is the key property used by envelope detectors.

## 4.5 Decision Flow Chart — BASK Detection Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start\nReceive sample r at t=nT_b"] --> B{"Sample exceeds\nthreshold lambda?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Decide BIT = 1"]
    B -- "No" --> D["Decide BIT = 0"]
    C --> E["Output to next stage\nIncrement n by 1"]
    D --> E
    E --> F{"More bits\nto receive?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> A
    F -- "No" --> G["End\nFrame complete"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions in KTU 2024 ESE typically test **definitions, working principles, advantages/disadvantages, and small derivations**. Aim for 2–3 sentences + a small diagram or formula.

---

### **Question A1 [3 Marks]** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**"Define Binary Amplitude Shift Keying (BASK). Why is it also called On-Off Keying (OOK)?"**

**Model Answer:**

**BASK (Binary Amplitude Shift Keying)** is a digital modulation technique in which the amplitude of a sinusoidal carrier is switched between two discrete levels according to the binary data, while the frequency and phase remain constant. Mathematically, the modulated signal is:

$$s(t) = b(t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad b(t) \in \{0, 1\}$$

It is called **On-Off Keying (OOK)** because one binary state (say '1') corresponds to the carrier being **ON** (amplitude `A_c`) and the other state ('0') corresponds to the carrier being **OFF** (amplitude = 0). Hence, the carrier is literally keyed (switched) on and off by the binary data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note (1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the OOK explanation, 1 mark for the diagram or example).**

---

### **Question A2 [3 Marks]** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**"List any three advantages and two disadvantages of BASK."**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Simplicity of implementation** — A simple electronic switch or multiplier is sufficient; no complex signal processing required.
2. **Lowest transmitter cost** — Ideal for low-budget applications like RFID and remote controls.
3. **Easy non-coherent detection** — Envelope detector works perfectly; no need for carrier synchronization.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Poor spectral efficiency** — Bandwidth is twice the bit rate (`B_T = 2R_b`); `\eta = 0.5` bps/Hz.
2. **Highly susceptible to noise and fading** — Since information is in the amplitude, any amplitude distortion directly causes errors. The BER is worse than BPSK by **3 dB**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Always answer both sub-parts in a structured way with headings, formulas, and a final conclusion.

---

### **QUESTION A [14 MARKS]** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the generation of BASK signal using a **switching modulator** with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for the BASK signal mathematically.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **transmission bandwidth** and **power spectral density** of BASK. Sketch the spectrum.

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question A**

#### Part (a): BASK Generation by Switching Modulator (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram Description:**

The switching modulator uses an electronic switch (FET, BJT, or PIN diode) controlled by the binary data. When the data bit is '1', the switch is **closed**, allowing the carrier to pass through to the output. When the data bit is '0', the switch is **open**, blocking the carrier.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Binary Data\n0 or 1"] --> B["Switch Control\nLogic Circuit"]
    C["Carrier Oscillator\nA_c cos 2pi f_c t"] --> D["Electronic Switch\nFET or PIN Diode"]
    B --> D
    D --> E["BASK Output\ns t"]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style C fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style E fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000
```

**Mathematical Derivation:**

Let the binary NRZ data be `b(t) = \sum_n a_n p(t - nT_b)` where `a_n \in \{0, 1\}` and `p(t)` is a unit-amplitude rectangular pulse of width `T_b`.

The carrier is `c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)`.

The BASK signal at the output of the switch is:

$$s(t) = b(t) \cdot c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a_n p(t - nT_b)$$

For the k-th bit interval `kT_b \leq t < (k+1)T_b`:

$$s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{if } a_k = 1 \\ 0 & \text{if } a_k = 0 \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Point:** The switching modulator produces a **true OOK signal** with a perfect zero state during bit '0' (no carrier leakage), which is cleaner than the product modulator's output.

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Block diagram: **2 marks**
- Binary-to-switching logic explanation: **2 marks**
- Mathematical derivation: **3 marks**

---

#### Part (b): Bandwidth and PSD of BASK (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Baseband NRZ Spectrum**

The baseband unipolar NRZ signal `b(t)` has the following PSD:

$$S_b(f) = \frac{1}{4} \delta(f) + \frac{T_b}{4} \text{sinc}^2(f T_b)$$

The first null occurs at `f = \pm 1/T_b = \pm R_b`.

**Step 2: Modulation Theorem (Frequency Shifting)**

Multiplication by the carrier shifts the spectrum to `\pm f_c`:

$$S_{BASK}(f) = \frac{A_c^2}{4} \left[ S_b(f - f_c) + S_b(f + f_c) \right]$$

**Step 3: Explicit Form of BASK PSD**

$$S_{BASK}(f) = \frac{A_c^2}{16} \left[ \delta(f - f_c) + \delta(f + f_c) \right] + \frac{A_c^2 T_b}{16} \left[ \text{sinc}^2\left[(f-f_c)T_b\right] + \text{sinc}^2\left[(f+f_c)T_b\right] \right]$$

**Step 4: Transmission Bandwidth**

The main lobe of the BASK spectrum extends from `f_c - R_b` to `f_c + R_b`. Therefore:

$$\boxed{B_T = 2 R_b}$$

**Step 5: Spectrum Sketch**

```
         |BASK Spectrum (single-sided representation)|
         
              Continuous lobes (sinc²)
              decay as 1/f²
              ↓       ↓         ↓
         __  /  \    / \       / \      __
        /  \/    \  /   \     /   \    /  \
───────/────\────\/─────\───/─────\──/────\───→  f
   -f_c-R_b   \ /       \ /       \/     f_c+R_b
              |
              | (small discrete line at ±f_c)
              |
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
> - Baseband NRZ PSD formula: **1 mark**
> - Modulation theorem application: **2 marks**
> - Final BASK PSD expression: **2 marks**
> - Bandwidth calculation + spectrum sketch: **2 marks**

---

### **QUESTION B [14 MARKS]** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the **coherent detection** of BASK signal. Derive the expression for the **probability of error (BER)** assuming AWGN channel.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A BASK system transmits data at `R_b = 1 \text{ Mbps}` over an AWGN channel with PSD `N_0 = 10^{-9} \text{ W/Hz}`. The carrier amplitude is `A_c = 10 \text{ mV}`. Calculate:
   1. Bit duration `T_b`
   2. Bit energy `E_b`
   3. The **probability of bit error** using coherent detection.
   4. The **probability of bit error** using non-coherent (envelope) detection.

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question B**

#### Part (a): Coherent Detection of BASK and BER Derivation (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (Coherent Detector):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Received r t = s t + n t"] --> B["BPF\nCenter f_c BW = 2R_b"]
    B --> C["Multiplier\nCoherent Mixer"]
    D["Local Oscillator\ncos 2pi f_c t phase locked"] --> C
    C --> E["Integrator\nAveraging over T_b"]
    E --> F["Sampler\nat t = nT_b"]
    F --> G["Threshold Comparator\nThreshold = A_c T_b / 4"]
    G --> H["Binary Output\n0 or 1"]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style E fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    style F fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000
    style G fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40,color:#000
    style H fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
```

**BER Derivation:**

Assume equiprobable bits. For binary '1', the received signal during `T_b` is:

$$r(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + n(t)$$

The correlator computes:

$$y = \int_0^{T_b} r(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \, dt = \int_0^{T_b} \left[ A_c \cos^2(2\pi f_c t) + n(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) \right] dt$$

The first term evaluates to `A_c T_b / 2`. The second term is a Gaussian random variable with mean 0 and variance `N_0 T_b / 4`.

Decision rule: Compare `y` with threshold `\lambda = A_c T_b / 4`. Declare '1' if `y > \lambda`, else '0'.

Probability of error (when '1' is sent, noise pushes `y` below `\lambda`):

$$P(e \vert 1) = P\!\left( n < -\frac{A_c T_b}{4} \right) = Q\!\left( \frac{A_c T_b / 4}{\sqrt{N_0 T_b / 4}} \right) = Q\!\left( \sqrt{\frac{A_c^2 T_b}{4 N_0}} \right)$$

Since `E_b = A_c^2 T_b / 4`:

$$\boxed{P_e = Q\!\left( \sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}} \right)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning #1:** Students often confuse `E_b` and `E_s`. For BASK, since only one bit is sent per symbol, `E_s = E_b`. Do not add an extra factor of 2. Also, the `Q(\cdot)` argument is `\sqrt{E_b/N_0}` for BASK — this is **3 dB worse than BPSK** which has `Q(\sqrt{2 E_b/N_0})`. Many students write BPSK's expression by mistake.

---

#### Part (b): Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- `R_b = 1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^6 \text{ bps}`
- `N_0 = 10^{-9} \text{ W/Hz}`
- `A_c = 10 \text{ mV} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 10^{-2} \text{ V}`

**1. Bit Duration:**

$$T_b = \frac{1}{R_b} = \frac{1}{10^6} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1 \, \mu\text{s}$$

**2. Bit Energy:**

$$E_b = \frac{A_c^2 T_b}{4} = \frac{(10^{-2})^2 \times 10^{-6}}{4} = \frac{10^{-10}}{4} = 2.5 \times 10^{-11} \text{ J}$$

**3. Coherent BER:**

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-11}}{10^{-9}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-2} = 0.025$$

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{0.025}\right) = Q(0.1581)$$

Using the approximation `Q(x) \approx 0.5 - x/\sqrt{2\pi}` for small `x`:

$$P_e \approx 0.5 - \frac{0.1581}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \approx 0.5 - 0.0632 \approx 0.437$$

> **More accurate:** From Q-function tables, `Q(0.158) \approx 0.4372`.

So `P_e \approx 4.37 \times 10^{-1}`. This is a **very high error rate** because the SNR is very low (`E_b/N_0 = 0.025` corresponds to `-16 \text{ dB}`).

**4. Non-Coherent BER:**

$$P_e = \frac{1}{2} e^{-E_b / 2 N_0} = \frac{1}{2} e^{-0.025 / 2} = \frac{1}{2} e^{-0.0125}$$

$$P_e = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.9876 \approx 0.494$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning #2:** In non-coherent detection, the formula is `P_e = (1/2) e^{-E_b/2N_0}` — students frequently write `e^{-E_b/N_0}` (missing the factor of 2 in the denominator). Also, be careful with units: `A_c` is in **Volts** (not mV) and `N_0` is in **W/Hz** (not dBm). Convert first, calculate later.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
> - `T_b` calculation: **1 mark**
> - `E_b` calculation: **2 marks**
> - Coherent `P_e`: **2 marks**
> - Non-coherent `P_e`: **2 marks**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this checklist the night before your KTU ESE exam!**

### ✅ Concept Mastery
- [ ] BASK = Amplitude modulation by a binary signal; carrier is switched ON/OFF.
- [ ] BASK is also called **OOK (On-Off Keying)** — remember this synonym.
- [ ] The mathematical model: `s(t) = b(t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)` with `b(t) \in \{0, 1\}`.
- [ ] **Frequency and phase are constant** — only amplitude changes.

### ✅ Key Formulas (Memorize These!)
- [ ] **Transmission Bandwidth:** `B_T = 2 R_b`
- [ ] **Spectral Efficiency:** `\eta = 0.5` bps/Hz (lowest among common binary schemes)
- [ ] **Bit Energy:** `E_b = A_c^2 T_b / 4`
- [ ] **Average Power:** `P_t = A_c^2 / 4`
- [ ] **Coherent BER:** `P_e = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})`
- [ ] **Non-Coherent BER:** `P_e = (1/2) e^{-E_b/2N_0}`

### ✅ Spectral Properties
- [ ] BASK spectrum has **upper and lower sidebands** centered at `f_c`.
- [ ] The first null in the spectrum occurs at `f_c \pm R_b`.
- [ ] Side lobes decay as `\text{sinc}^2` (1/f²).
- [ ] There is a **small residual carrier component** at `\pm f_c` (because unipolar NRZ has a DC offset).

### ✅ Modulator Types
- [ ] **Product modulator** (multiplier): Generates BASK with a small residual carrier.
- [ ] **Switching modulator** (FET/PIN switch): Generates clean OOK with perfect zero state.

### ✅ Demodulator Types
- [ ] **Coherent detector** (synchronous multiplication + LPF): Lower BER, requires PLL.
- [ ] **Non-coherent detector** (envelope detector): Simpler, ~3 dB worse BER.

### ✅ Advantages
- [ ] Simplest transmitter (just a switch + oscillator).
- [ ] Lowest cost; ideal for low-data-rate applications.
- [ ] Envelope detection possible — no carrier synchronization needed.

### ✅ Disadvantages
- [ ] Poor spectral efficiency (0.5 bps/Hz).
- [ ] Highly susceptible to amplitude noise and fading.
- [ ] 3 dB worse than BPSK in power efficiency.

### ✅ Common Applications
- [ ] RFID tags, NFC, remote controls (garage doors, car key fobs).
- [ ] Optical OOK fiber links (early PON systems).
- [ ] Morse code (CW) radio transmission.
- [ ] 433 MHz / 315 MHz ISM band devices.
- [ ] Industrial telemetry at low data rates.

### ✅ KTU Exam Pitfalls to Avoid
- [ ] ⚠️ Do not confuse BASK BER with BPSK BER — BASK is **3 dB worse**.
- [ ] ⚠️ For non-coherent detection, the exponent is `-E_b/(2N_0)`, **not** `-E_b/N_0`.
- [ ] ⚠️ Always convert `A_c` to **Volts** and `N_0` to **W/Hz** before computing `E_b/N_0`.
- [ ] ⚠️ Bandwidth of BASK is `2 R_b`, not `R_b`.
- [ ] ⚠️ When drawing the BASK spectrum, remember to show BOTH the upper and lower sidebands (positive and negative frequencies).
- [ ] ⚠️ Spectral efficiency is bit rate / bandwidth, **not** the other way around.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BFSK
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT416 - PRINCIPLES OF COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** BFSK

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2: BINARY FREQUENCY SHIFT KEYING (BFSK)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **BFSK (Binary Frequency Shift Keying)** is a form of binary digital modulation in which the **frequency** of a sinusoidal carrier wave is discretely varied between two preset constant values ($f_1$ and $f_0$) in accordance with the binary information bits being transmitted, while the amplitude and phase of the carrier are held constant.

Mathematically, a BFSK signal is expressed as:

$$
s(t) = 
\begin{cases}
\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_1 t + \phi_1), & \text{for binary } \mathbf{1} \\
\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_0 t + \phi_0), & \text{for binary } \mathbf{0}
\end{cases}
$$

where:
- $E_b$ = Energy per bit (in Joules)
- $T_b$ = Bit duration (in seconds)
- $f_1$ = **Mark frequency** (carrier frequency for bit `1`)
- $f_0$ = **Space frequency** (carrier frequency for bit `0`)
- $\phi_1, \phi_0$ = Initial phases of the two oscillators

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** BFSK is a continuous-phase (CPFSK-class) modulation. The transitions between $f_1$ and $f_0$ are abrupt in basic BFSK but phase-continuous in the more robust variant, **CPFSK (Continuous Phase FSK)**.

---

### 🌐 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Whistles" Model

Imagine you are a soldier in a battlefield trying to send a Morse-like signal to a teammate across the field:

- You have **two whistles**: one **high-pitched** and one **low-pitched**.
- You blow the **high whistle** to send a binary `1`.
- You blow the **low whistle** to send a binary `0`.
- Your teammate, wearing noise-isolated earphones, listens and notes down the sequence.

In this analogy:
- The **whistle** is your *carrier wave*.
- The **pitch** of the whistle represents the **frequency** of the carrier.
- The **act of choosing which whistle to blow** is the *modulation* process.
- The **listener's brain filtering the two pitches** is the *demodulation* process.

This is **exactly how BFSK works** — the transmitter is essentially a "frequency switch" toggling between two oscillators!

> [!TIP] **Why use two frequencies instead of two amplitudes (like ASK)?**  
> Amplitude is highly susceptible to noise and channel fading. **Frequency**, on the other hand, is a *relative* parameter and remains more robust against amplitude noise. This makes FSK superior to ASK for noisy channels (e.g., low-cost RF remote controls, pagers, walkie-talkies).

---

### 🧭 Classification of BFSK

| Variant | Description | Carrier Phase Relation | Detection Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coherent BFSK** | Receiver uses a locally generated reference synchronized to the transmitter | $\phi_1$ and $\phi_0$ are tracked | Synchronous / Correlative detection |
| **Non-Coherent BFSK** | No phase reference needed at the receiver | Phases can be random and independent | Envelope detection (simpler, cheaper) |
| **CPFSK** | Continuous Phase FSK — phase does not jump during bit transitions | Phase is continuous | Either coherent or non-coherent |

> [!NOTE] **Standard Reference Values (from ITU & Bell Systems):**  
> - **Bell 202 Modem:** Mark = **1200 Hz**, Space = **2200 Hz** (FSK at 1200 bps).  
> - **Bell 103 Modem:** Mark = **1270 Hz**, Space = **1070 Hz** (FSK at 300 bps).  
> - **FM Broadcast (WBFM):** Frequency deviation $\Delta f = \pm 75$ kHz.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔍 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The generation, transmission, and detection of a BFSK signal can be broken into the following logical steps:

#### **Step 1: Bit-to-Frequency Mapping**
The binary data stream is divided into individual bits of duration $T_b$. Each bit controls an electronic switch:
- Bit `1` → routes carrier oscillator running at $f_1$ to the output.
- Bit `0` → routes carrier oscillator running at $f_0$ to the output.

> **Why two separate oscillators?**  
> A single VCO (Voltage-Controlled Oscillator) tuned by the data can also produce FSK, but it is harder to maintain a precise frequency separation. The two-oscillator (or direct-digital-synthesis) method is preferred in textbook KTU analysis.

#### **Step 2: Frequency Deviation ($\Delta f$) and Modulation Index ($h$)**
The **frequency deviation** is the *half-separation* between the two carrier frequencies:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{\vert f_1 - f_0 \vert}{2}
$$

The **modulation index** of an FSK signal is defined as:

$$
h = \frac{f_1 - f_0}{R_b} = (f_1 - f_0)\,T_b = 2\,\Delta f\,T_b
$$

> **How to interpret $h$:**  
> - $h = 0.5$ → **Minimum-shift keying (MSK)** — a special, spectrally efficient form of CPFSK.  
> - $h = 1$ → Sunde's FSK (orthogonal BFSK).  
> - $h \gg 1$ → wideband FSK.

#### **Step 3: Orthogonality Condition (Critical for Detection)**
For the receiver to be able to **perfectly distinguish** $f_1$ from $f_0$ without inter-symbol interference, the two signals must be **mutually orthogonal** over the bit interval $[0, T_b]$:

$$
\int_0^{T_b} \cos(2\pi f_1 t)\,\cos(2\pi f_0 t)\,dt = 0
$$

Solving this integral yields the **orthogonality condition**:

$$
f_1 - f_0 = \frac{n}{T_b}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots
$$

The **minimum** frequency separation for orthogonality is therefore:

$$
\boxed{\; \vert f_1 - f_0 \vert_{\min} = \frac{1}{T_b} \;}
$$

#### **Step 4: Detection at the Receiver**
Two main strategies:
1. **Coherent Detection:** Use two correlators (matched filters), one tuned to $\cos(2\pi f_1 t)$ and the other to $\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$. Whichever correlator gives the larger output determines the received bit.
2. **Non-Coherent Detection:** Use two **bandpass filters** centered at $f_1$ and $f_0$, followed by **envelope detectors**. The larger envelope wins. *(Simpler and more common in low-cost applications.)*

---

### 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT] **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR KTU 2024 EXAMS**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BFSK signal (bit `1`) | $s_1(t) = \sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_1 t)$ | Volts |
| 2 | BFSK signal (bit `0`) | $s_0(t) = \sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ | Volts |
| 3 | Frequency deviation | $\Delta f = \frac{\vert f_1 - f_0\vert}{2}$ | Hz |
| 4 | Modulation index | $h = (f_1 - f_0)T_b = 2\Delta f T_b$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Minimum orthogonal separation | $\vert f_1 - f_0\vert_{\min} = \dfrac{1}{T_b}$ | Hz (coherent) |
| 6 | Non-coherent min separation | $\vert f_1 - f_0\vert_{\min} = \dfrac{1}{2T_b}$ | Hz |
| 7 | Transmission bandwidth (Carson's rule) | $B_T = 2(\Delta f + f_b)$ | Hz |
| 8 | Power Spectral Density (PSD) | $S_{BFSK}(f) = \dfrac{E_b}{4T_b}\left[\text{sinc}^2((f-f_1)T_b) + \text{sinc}^2((f-f_0)T_b)\right]$ | W/Hz |
| 9 | BER (Coherent BFSK) | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | BER (Non-Coherent BFSK) | $P_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Spectral efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{R_b}{B_T}$ | bits/s/Hz |
| 12 | SNR per bit | $\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}$ | Dimensionless (dB for log) |

> [!NOTE] **Important Tip:** The $Q$-function used in BER is defined as $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_x^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2}\,du$. In KTU exams, $Q(x)$ values are usually given or approximated. Recall: $Q(0) = 0.5$, $Q(1) = 0.1587$, $Q(2) = 0.0228$, $Q(3) = 0.00135$.

---

### 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility

BFSK is the workhorse of low-to-medium data-rate digital communication:

1. **Paging Systems (POCSAG, FLEX):** Use 2-FSK or 4-FSK at 512 bps – 4.8 kbps.
2. **Two-way Radio Communications:** Walkie-talkies, marine VHF, and aircraft communications use NB-FSK.
3. **AMR (Automatic Meter Reading):** Smart electricity/water meters transmit consumption data via low-power FSK.
4. **Garage Door Openers & Car Key Fobs (ISM 433 MHz):** Simple, low-cost FSK transmitters.
5. **DTMF Signalling:** Uses a pair of FSK-like tones to represent digits (Touch-Tone phones).
6. **Caller ID (Bell 202 standard):** 1200 bps FSK modulation between bursts.

> **Why engineers choose BFSK over BPSK for these applications:**  
> Simpler circuitry (no carrier recovery PLL needed in non-coherent mode), excellent performance in **frequency-selective fading channels**, and tolerance to oscillator drift.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🧮 Derivation 1: Orthogonality Condition for BFSK Signals

**Goal:** Show that the minimum frequency separation for which $\int_0^{T_b} s_1(t)\,s_0(t)\,dt = 0$ is $f_1 - f_0 = 1/T_b$.

**Step 1 — Write the inner product integral.**

$$
I = \int_0^{T_b} s_1(t)\,s_0(t)\,dt
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the BFSK signal expressions** (assuming zero phase for simplicity and normalization $\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}$ cancels out):

$$
I = \int_0^{T_b} \cos(2\pi f_1 t)\,\cos(2\pi f_0 t)\,dt
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the trigonometric identity** $2\cos A \cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$:

$$
I = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^{T_b} \left[ \cos(2\pi(f_1 - f_0)t) + \cos(2\pi(f_1 + f_0)t) \right] dt
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate term by term:**

$$
I = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \frac{\sin(2\pi(f_1 - f_0)T_b)}{2\pi(f_1 - f_0)} + \frac{\sin(2\pi(f_1 + f_0)T_b)}{2\pi(f_1 + f_0)} \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that for $f_1, f_0 \gg 1/T_b$** (typical RF case), the second term is negligible. The first term equals zero whenever:

$$
\sin(2\pi(f_1 - f_0)T_b) = 0 \implies 2\pi(f_1 - f_0)T_b = n\pi
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify for the minimum** ($n = 1$):

$$
\boxed{\; f_1 - f_0 = \frac{1}{2T_b}\ \text{(non-coherent)} \quad \text{or} \quad f_1 - f_0 = \frac{1}{T_b}\ \text{(coherent, min orthogonal)} \;}
$$

> **Note:** The factor of 2 difference arises because non-coherent detection (envelope) can still resolve frequencies separated by half a cycle within a bit duration, while coherent detection requires a full cycle.

---

### 🧮 Derivation 2: Transmission Bandwidth of BFSK (Carson's Rule)

**Goal:** Derive the bandwidth occupied by a BFSK signal.

**Step 1 — Identify the peak frequency deviation:**

$$
\Delta f = \frac{\vert f_1 - f_0\vert}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Carson's rule for FM-type signals:**

$$
B_T = 2(\Delta f + W) = 2\Delta f + 2W
$$

where $W$ is the **highest baseband modulating frequency**. For a binary NRZ data stream, $W = f_b/2$ (the fundamental frequency of the bit pattern), but conservatively, we often use $W = f_b$.

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
B_T = 2\Delta f + 2 f_b = (f_1 - f_0) + 2 f_b
$$

**Step 4 — Special case (minimum orthogonal BFSK):** Substituting $f_1 - f_0 = 1/T_b = f_b$:

$$
\boxed{\; B_T = f_b + 2f_b = 3 f_b = \frac{3}{T_b}\ \text{(approx.)} \;}
$$

But a more common KTU answer is:

$$
\boxed{\; B_T = 2(\Delta f + f_b) \;}
$$

---

### 🧮 Derivation 3: Probability of Error (BER) for Coherent BFSK

**Setup:** Two equiprobable orthogonal signals $s_1(t), s_0(t)$ corrupted by AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$. The receiver computes two decision variables $r_1$ and $r_0$ (the correlator outputs) and decides $\hat{b} = 1$ if $r_1 > r_0$, else $\hat{b} = 0$.

**Step 1 — Express the decision variable.** Under $H_1$ (bit `1` sent), the difference is:

$$
D = r_1 - r_0 = \sqrt{E_b} + n_1 - n_0
$$

where $n_1, n_0$ are independent zero-mean Gaussian RVs each with variance $N_0/2$.

**Step 2 — Compute the mean and variance of $D$ under $H_1$:**

$$
\mathbb{E}[D \mid H_1] = \sqrt{E_b}
$$

$$
\text{Var}[D \mid H_1] = \text{Var}[n_1] + \text{Var}[n_0] = \frac{N_0}{2} + \frac{N_0}{2} = N_0
$$

**Step 3 — An error occurs** if $D < 0$. Standardize the Gaussian:

$$
P_e = P(D < 0 \mid H_1) = P\!\left( \frac{D - \sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0}} < -\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the left-tail of a unit normal:**

$$
\boxed{\; P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right) \;}
$$

---

### 🧮 Derivation 4: Probability of Error (BER) for Non-Coherent BFSK

**Setup:** Use envelope detectors on two bandpass filters centered at $f_1$ and $f_0$. The envelopes $R_1, R_0$ follow **Rician** distribution (under $H_1$) and **Rayleigh** distribution (under $H_0$).

**Step 1 — Probability of error given $H_1$:** The conditional error is the probability that the envelope at $f_0$ exceeds that at $f_1$:

$$
P(e \mid H_1) = P(R_0 > R_1 \mid H_1) = \frac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b}{2N_0}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — By symmetry**, $P(e \mid H_0)$ is identical.

**Step 3 — Total probability of error** (assuming equiprobable bits):

$$
P_e = P(H_1) P(e \mid H_1) + P(H_0) P(e \mid H_0) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/2N_0}\right] + \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/2N_0}\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Final simplified result:**

$$
\boxed{\; P_e = \frac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b}{2N_0}\right) \;}
$$

---

### 📋 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Coherent vs Non-Coherent BFSK

| Feature | Coherent BFSK | Non-Coherent BFSK |
|---|---|---|
| Reference carrier needed? | ✅ Yes (PLL / Costas loop) | ❌ No |
| Complexity | Higher | Lower |
| Performance at low $E_b/N_0$ | Superior | ~3 dB worse |
| BER expression | $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ | $\frac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/(2N_0)}$ |
| Phase tracking required? | Yes | No |
| Typical application | Satellite comms, deep-space | Pagers, RKE, low-cost RF links |
| Min. frequency separation | $1/T_b$ | $1/(2T_b)$ |
| Susceptibility to random phase shifts | High | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🎯 Diagram 1: BFSK Transmitter (Two-Oscillator Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn["Binary Data Input d(t)"] --> nodeInv["NOT Gate Inverter"]
    nodeIn --> nodeMuxA["AND Gate 1"]
    nodeInv --> nodeMuxB["AND Gate 2"]
    nodeOsc1["Oscillator f1 Mark Frequency"] --> nodeMuxA
    nodeOsc0["Oscillator f0 Space Frequency"] --> nodeMuxB
    nodeMuxA --> nodeSum["Summing Amplifier Adder"]
    nodeMuxB --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodePA["Power Amplifier"]
    nodePA --> nodeAnt["Transmit Antenna"]
    nodeAnt --> nodeOut["Channel AWGN n(t)"]
```

**Operation Logic:**  
- When bit `1` arrives, AND-1 opens → oscillator at $f_1$ passes to adder.  
- When bit `0` arrives, the inverter activates AND-2 → oscillator at $f_0$ passes.  
- The output is summed, amplified, and radiated.

---

### 🎯 Diagram 2: Coherent BFSK Receiver (Correlator-based)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeRx["Received Signal r(t) = s(t) plus n(t)"] --> nodeCor1["Multiplier 1 with Local cos 2 pi f1 t"]
    nodeRx --> nodeCor0["Multiplier 2 with Local cos 2 pi f0 t"]
    nodeLO1["Local Oscillator 1 at f1 Synchronized"] --> nodeCor1
    nodeLO0["Local Oscillator 2 at f0 Synchronized"] --> nodeCor0
    nodeCor1 --> nodeInt1["Integrator 0 to Tb"]
    nodeCor0 --> nodeInt0["Integrator 0 to Tb"]
    nodeInt1 --> nodeSamp1["Sampler at t = Tb"]
    nodeInt0 --> nodeSamp0["Sampler at t = Tb"]
    nodeSamp1 --> nodeComp["Comparator Decision Device"]
    nodeSamp0 --> nodeComp
    nodeComp --> nodeOut["Recovered Binary Output"]
```

**Operation Logic:**  
- The received signal is correlated against two locally generated references.  
- The integrator accumulates energy over one bit period.  
- Whichever correlator yields the larger sample value wins — the comparator outputs the corresponding bit.

---

### 🎯 Diagram 3: Non-Coherent BFSK Receiver (Envelope Detector)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeRx["Received Signal r(t)"] --> nodeBPF1["Bandpass Filter 1 Centered at f1"]
    nodeRx --> nodeBPF0["Bandpass Filter 0 Centered at f0"]
    nodeBPF1 --> nodeEnv1["Envelope Detector 1 Diode plus RC"]
    nodeBPF0 --> nodeEnv0["Envelope Detector 0 Diode plus RC"]
    nodeEnv1 --> nodeSamp1["Sampler at t = Tb"]
    nodeEnv0 --> nodeSamp0["Sampler at t = Tb"]
    nodeSamp1 --> nodeComp["Comparator"]
    nodeSamp0 --> nodeComp
    nodeComp --> nodeOut["Decoded Bit Output"]
```

**Operation Logic:**  
- Two narrow BPFs isolate the energy around $f_1$ and $f_0$.  
- Envelope detectors extract the magnitude of each band.  
- The comparator picks the larger envelope to decide the bit.  
- **No carrier synchronization is needed**, hence the name *non-coherent*.

---

### 🎯 Diagram 4: BFSK Signal Generation — Time-Domain Waveform Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeBit["Binary Input: 1 0 1 1 0 0 1"]
    nodeBit --> nodeMap["Frequency Mapper"]
    nodeMap --> nodeWave1["Segment 1: f1 frequency for 1"]
    nodeMap --> nodeWave0["Segment 2: f0 frequency for 0"]
    nodeMap --> nodeWave1b["Segment 3: f1 frequency for 1"]
    nodeMap --> nodeWave1c["Segment 4: f1 frequency for 1"]
    nodeMap --> nodeWave0b["Segment 5: f0 frequency for 0"]
    nodeMap --> nodeWave0c["Segment 6: f0 frequency for 0"]
    nodeMap --> nodeWave1d["Segment 7: f1 frequency for 1"]
    nodeWave1 --> nodeCombine["Concatenated BFSK Waveform s(t)"]
    nodeWave0 --> nodeCombine
    nodeWave1b --> nodeCombine
    nodeWave1c --> nodeCombine
    nodeWave0b --> nodeCombine
    nodeWave0c --> nodeCombine
    nodeWave1d --> nodeCombine
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each bit interval $T_b$ carries a single-frequency sinusoid. The frequency switches abruptly (or phase-continuously, in CPFSK) at bit boundaries.

---

### 🎯 Diagram 5: BFSK Power Spectral Density (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeCenter["Frequency Axis f"]
    nodeF0["Sinc Squared Peak at f0"]
    nodeF1["Sinc Squared Peak at f1"]
    nodeF0 --> nodeSep["Separation equal to f1 minus f0 equal to n by Tb"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeSep
    nodeSep --> nodeBW["Total Bandwidth B_T = 2 times Delta f plus 2 times f_b"]
```

> **Key insight:** The PSD of BFSK has two main lobes (one per carrier frequency). The two lobes are separated by $f_1 - f_0$, and each has a width of approximately $2 f_b$. The total bandwidth is therefore $B_T \approx (f_1 - f_0) + 2 f_b$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
*Define Binary Frequency Shift Keying (BFSK). With a neat block diagram, explain the generation of BFSK signal using the switching method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE] **Definition (1 mark):**  
> BFSK is a digital modulation technique in which the instantaneous frequency of the carrier is switched between two discrete values $f_1$ and $f_0$ in accordance with the binary data bits, while keeping the amplitude constant.

> **Generation (1 mark):**  
> In the switching method, the binary data controls an electronic switch that selects one of two oscillators. When the input is `1`, oscillator at $f_1$ is connected to the output; when the input is `0`, oscillator at $f_0$ is connected.

> **Block Diagram Description (1 mark):**  
> The transmitter consists of two carrier oscillators (at $f_1$ and $f_0$), an inverter, two AND gates, a summing amplifier, and a power amplifier feeding the antenna. The data bit directly drives one AND gate while its complement (via inverter) drives the other. The OR/Add operation produces the BFSK waveform.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**  
*State the orthogonality condition for BFSK signals. Why is this condition important in digital communication?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Condition (2 marks):**  
> Two BFSK signals $s_1(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_1 t)$ and $s_0(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ are mutually orthogonal over the bit duration $T_b$ if:  
> $$\int_0^{T_b} s_1(t)\,s_0(t)\,dt = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad f_1 - f_0 = \frac{n}{T_b}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3,\ldots$$  
> The minimum separation is $f_1 - f_0 = 1/T_b$ (coherent) or $1/(2T_b)$ (non-coherent).

> **Importance (1 mark):**  
> Orthogonality ensures that the receiver can perfectly distinguish between the two signals using correlators or matched filters, leading to **zero inter-symbol interference** and **optimal (minimum) probability of error** in an AWGN channel. Non-orthogonal signals would cause correlation leakage and increase BER.

---

### 📝 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**

**Q.** *(a)* Derive the expression for the probability of error of coherent BFSK in an AWGN channel. Plot the BER vs $E_b/N_0$ curve and comment. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A BFSK system uses mark frequency $f_1 = 1.5$ MHz and space frequency $f_0 = 1.0$ MHz. The bit rate is $R_b = 200$ kbps. Determine:  
   (i) Frequency deviation $\Delta f$  
   (ii) Modulation index $h$  
   (iii) Transmission bandwidth $B_T$ using Carson's rule  
   (iv) Is this system orthogonal? Justify. *(7 marks)*

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Coherent BFSK BER:**

> The transmitted BFSK signals are:  
> $$s_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_1 t), \quad s_0(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_0 t), \quad 0 \le t \le T_b$$

> The received signal is $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$, where $n(t)$ is AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$.

> The optimal receiver uses two correlators with reference signals $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_1 t)$ and $\phi_0(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$.

> **Decision variables:**  
> $$r_1 = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt, \quad r_0 = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi_0(t)\,dt$$

> The receiver decides `1` if $r_1 > r_0$, else `0`.

> **Under $H_1$ (bit `1` sent):**  
> $$r_1 = \sqrt{E_b} + n_1, \quad r_0 = n_0$$
> where $n_1, n_0$ are independent Gaussian RVs with $\mathbb{E}[n_i] = 0$ and $\text{Var}[n_i] = N_0/2$.

> **Difference:** $D = r_1 - r_0 = \sqrt{E_b} + n_1 - n_0$  
> Mean: $\mathbb{E}[D] = \sqrt{E_b}$  
> Variance: $\text{Var}[D] = N_0/2 + N_0/2 = N_0$

> **Error occurs if $D < 0$:**  
> $$P_e = P\!\left(\frac{D - \sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0}} < -\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

> **Final Result:** $\boxed{P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)}$

> **Plot and Comment (1 mark for plot, 1 mark for comment):**  
> The BER decreases monotonically as $E_b/N_0$ (in dB) increases. At $E_b/N_0 = 0$ dB, $P_e \approx 0.0786$. To achieve $P_e = 10^{-5}$, we need $E_b/N_0 \approx 9.6$ dB. The curve is symmetric in log-scale and falls off rapidly with increasing SNR.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem:**

**Given:** $f_1 = 1.5$ MHz, $f_0 = 1.0$ MHz, $R_b = 200$ kbps $\Rightarrow T_b = 1/R_b = 5\,\mu s$

**(i) Frequency deviation:**

$$
\Delta f = \frac{\vert f_1 - f_0 \vert}{2} = \frac{\vert 1.5 - 1.0 \vert \,\text{MHz}}{2} = \frac{0.5 \,\text{MHz}}{2} = 0.25 \,\text{MHz} = 250 \,\text{kHz}
$$

**(ii) Modulation index:**

$$
h = (f_1 - f_0)\,T_b = (0.5 \times 10^6)\,(5 \times 10^{-6}) = 2.5
$$

**(iii) Bandwidth (Carson's rule):**

$$
B_T = 2(\Delta f + f_b) = 2(250\,\text{kHz} + 200\,\text{kHz}) = 2 \times 450\,\text{kHz} = 900\,\text{kHz}
$$

**(iv) Orthogonality check:**

For orthogonal BFSK, minimum separation is $1/T_b = 1/(5\,\mu s) = 200$ kHz.

Actual separation $= f_1 - f_0 = 500$ kHz $= 2.5 \times (1/T_b)$.

Since $500$ kHz $= 2.5 \times 200$ kHz (which is an integer multiple of $1/T_b$), **the system IS orthogonal** — specifically, $n = 2.5$ ... wait, let us recheck.

**Correction for orthogonality:** For coherent orthogonal BFSK, we need $f_1 - f_0 = n/T_b$ for integer $n$.

Here, $f_1 - f_0 = 500$ kHz, and $1/T_b = 200$ kHz. So $f_1 - f_0 / (1/T_b) = 500/200 = 2.5$.

This is **not an integer**, so the system is **NOT orthogonal** for coherent detection at the minimum separation. However, since the actual separation $500$ kHz is much larger than the minimum $200$ kHz, the signals will be **near-orthogonal** with negligible correlation. The system will work in practice but strictly speaking is not exactly orthogonal.

> **Re-stated Answer:** The system is **not exactly orthogonal** for the minimum coherent condition (since 2.5 is not an integer multiple of $1/T_b$). However, because $f_1 - f_0 \gg 1/T_b$, the residual correlation is negligible and the system behaves as nearly orthogonal.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**Q.** *(a)* With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a non-coherent BFSK receiver. Derive the expression for its probability of error. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A non-coherent BFSK receiver operates with $E_b/N_0 = 8$ (linear scale). Compute the bit error probability. Compare the result with that of a coherent BFSK receiver at the same $E_b/N_0$. *(7 marks)*

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**Part (a) — Non-Coherent BFSK Receiver:**

> **Block Diagram (described in 3 marks):**  
> The non-coherent BFSK receiver consists of:  
> 1. **Two Bandpass Filters (BPF1, BPF0)** — centered at $f_1$ and $f_0$ with bandwidth $B \approx 2/T_b$ each.  
> 2. **Two Envelope Detectors (ED1, ED0)** — typically a diode detector followed by a low-pass filter.  
> 3. **Samplers** — sample the envelope at $t = T_b$.  
> 4. **Comparator** — selects the larger envelope to decide the bit.

> **Working Principle (2 marks):**  
> The incoming signal $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ is split into two parallel paths. BPF1 extracts the energy around $f_1$ and rejects the rest. BPF0 does the same for $f_0$. The envelope detectors convert the bandpass signal at each filter output into a baseband envelope. After sampling at the end of the bit period, the comparator decides `1` if envelope-1 > envelope-0, else `0`. **No carrier phase reference is required**, hence *non-coherent*.

> **Derivation of BER (2 marks):**  
> Under $H_1$, the envelope at the $f_1$ output follows a Rician distribution, and the envelope at the $f_0$ output follows a Rayleigh distribution. Computing $P(\text{env}_0 > \text{env}_1 \mid H_1)$ yields $\frac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/2N_0)$. By symmetry, the total error probability is:  
> $$\boxed{P_e = \frac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b}{2N_0}\right)}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Comparison:**

**Given:** $E_b/N_0 = 8$ (linear), so $10\log_{10}(8) = 9.03$ dB.

**Non-Coherent BFSK BER:**

$$
P_e^{\text{NC}} = \frac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{8}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{2}e^{-4} = \frac{1}{2}(0.01832) = 9.16 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Coherent BFSK BER:**

$$
P_e^{\text{C}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{8}\right) = Q(2.828)
$$

Using standard $Q$-function table: $Q(2.83) \approx 2.33 \times 10^{-3}$.

**Comparison:**

| $E_b/N_0$ | Coherent $P_e$ | Non-Coherent $P_e$ | Ratio (NC/C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8 (linear) | $2.33 \times 10^{-3}$ | $9.16 \times 10^{-3}$ | $\approx 3.93\times$ |

> **Comment (1 mark):**  
> At $E_b/N_0 = 8$ (linear), the non-coherent BFSK has a BER roughly **3.93 times higher** than the coherent version. Equivalently, coherent BFSK requires about **2–3 dB less SNR** to achieve the same BER as non-coherent BFSK. This SNR penalty is the **price paid for the simpler receiver circuitry** in non-coherent detection. For low-cost or mobile applications where complexity and power are constraints, the non-coherent design wins; for high-performance links, coherent detection is preferred.

---

> [!WARNING] **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to take the half-separation:** Many students incorrectly write $f_1 - f_0$ as the *frequency deviation*. The correct definition is $\Delta f = (f_1 - f_0)/2$. Loss of **1–2 marks** if you use the wrong definition.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up the two BER formulas:** Coherent = $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$; Non-coherent = $\frac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/2N_0)$. Examiners deliberately give questions where both are valid candidates. Write the **correct one with derivation context**.
> 
> 3. **Bandwidth formula confusion:** Carson's rule $B_T = 2(\Delta f + f_b)$ is for FM-like signals. Do not confuse with $B_T = 2f_b$ (BPSK bandwidth) or $B_T = f_b$ (baseband).
> 
> 4. **Orthogonality check error:** Always verify $f_1 - f_0 = n/T_b$ for **integer** $n$ when claiming orthogonality. Non-integer ratios give near-orthogonal, not strictly orthogonal, signals.
> 
> 5. **Modulation index mistake:** $h = 2\Delta f T_b$, NOT $\Delta f T_b$. Many students miss the factor of 2.
> 
> 6. **Phase continuity ignored:** Basic BFSK has phase discontinuities at bit transitions, causing spectral spreading. For CPFSK (continuous phase), the spectrum is much narrower. State this in viva/exam answers for full marks.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Definition:** BFSK = two-frequency carrier switching for binary bits `1` and `0`.
- [x] **Mark frequency ($f_1$)** for bit `1`; **Space frequency ($f_0$)** for bit `0`.
- [x] **Frequency deviation:** $\Delta f = (f_1 - f_0)/2$.
- [x] **Modulation index:** $h = 2\Delta f T_b = (f_1 - f_0)/R_b$.
- [x] **Orthogonality condition:** $f_1 - f_0 = n/T_b$, minimum $= 1/T_b$ (coherent) or $1/(2T_b)$ (non-coherent).
- [x] **Bandwidth (Carson's rule):** $B_T = 2(\Delta f + f_b)$.
- [x] **BER Coherent:** $P_e = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$.
- [x] **BER Non-Coherent:** $P_e = \frac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/2N_0)$.
- [x] **PSD:** Two sinc² lobes centered at $f_0$ and $f_1$, separation $= f_1 - f_0$.
- [x] **Receiver types:** Coherent (correlator/PLL) vs Non-coherent (envelope detector).
- [x] **Coherent BFSK outperforms non-coherent by ~2-3 dB at same BER.**
- [x] **Real-world examples:** Bell 202 modem (1200/2200 Hz), POCSAG pagers, RKE key fobs, DTMF.
- [x] **CPFSK** is the continuous-phase variant — narrower spectrum, used in GSM, Bluetooth (GFSK).
- [x] **Spectral efficiency:** Lower than BPSK but more robust to amplitude noise.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BPSK
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT416 - PRINCIPLES OF COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** BPSK

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK)** — also known as **Phase Reversal Keying (PRK)** or **2-PSK** — is a fundamental **digital passband modulation scheme** in which the phase of a constant-amplitude sinusoidal carrier is abruptly shifted between **two discrete values**, typically **0° and 180° (π radians)**, to represent the two binary symbols of the incoming bitstream, namely logic **'0'** and logic **'1'**. The amplitude and frequency of the carrier remain strictly invariant; only the phase carries the information.

Mathematically, the BPSK modulated signal is expressed as:

$$
s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{for binary '1'} \\ A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \pi) = -A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{for binary '0'} \end{cases}
$$

where $A_c$ is the carrier amplitude and $f_c$ is the carrier frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** BPSK is a **CO2-mapped (Module 2)** topic. Students must master the *signal representation*, *constellation diagram*, *bandwidth*, and most importantly, the **Bit Error Probability derivation** — these are recurring 14-mark questions in University ESE papers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Faces of a Coin" Intuition

Imagine a **flashlight** that an engineer in a dark room shines toward a receiver standing far away.

- When the engineer **holds the flashlight upright** → the receiver sees light coming from the **top (phase = 0°)**. This is **bit 1**.
- When the engineer **turns the flashlight upside-down (180° flip)** → the receiver sees light coming from the **bottom (phase = 180°)**. This is **bit 0**.

The flashlight brightness (amplitude) never changes. The signal's frequency never changes. **Only the direction (phase) flips.** The receiver, knowing exactly when to look, decides whether the wave is "up" or "down" and reconstructs the bit.

This is BPSK in plain English. Because the two states are **maximally separated in phase (180° apart)**, BPSK is the **most robust** of all binary modulation schemes against noise — at the cost of using the same bandwidth as BFSK but transmitting only 1 bit/symbol.

### 1.3 Key Physical and System Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Symbol | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Number of bits per symbol ($k$) | $\log_2(2) = 1$ | Determines M-ary nature |
| Number of symbols ($M$) | **2** | Binary modulation |
| Symbol duration ($T_s = T_b$) | $T_b$ | Bit rate = Symbol rate |
| Phase separation ($\Delta \phi$) | **180° = $\pi$ rad** | Maximum noise immunity |
| Bandwidth (null-to-null) | $2R_b$ | Equal to bit rate × 2 |
| Bandwidth efficiency ($\eta$) | **0.5 bits/sec/Hz** | Theoretical limit |
| Euclidean distance ($d$) | $2\sqrt{E_b}$ | Maximum for binary |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why BPSK over BFSK or ASK?** BPSK delivers the **lowest Bit Error Rate (BER)** for a given $E_b/N_0$ because the two signal vectors are placed on **opposite ends of the signal-space axis**, maximizing the distance between them. It is the workhorse of satellite telemetry, deep-space communication (NASA's Voyager probes), and Wi-Fi legacy 802.11b headers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Modulation Flow

The BPSK modulator (also called a *phase modulator* or *product modulator*) executes the following structured logic:

1. **Bitstream arrives** at the modulator with a bit period $T_b$ and bit energy $E_b = A_c^2 T_b / 2$.
2. **NRZ encoding** is applied. A bit `'1'` is mapped to a unipolar voltage level $+1$ volt (or $+A_c$), and bit `'0'` is mapped to $-1$ volt (or $-A_c$). This is the *baseband pulse-shaping stage*.
3. **Multiplication with carrier**: The NRZ signal $m(t) \in \{+1, -1\}$ is multiplied by the carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ using a **balanced modulator** (a double-balanced mixer).
4. **Carrier phase is inverted** when $m(t) = -1$ (i.e., bit `'0'`), producing the BPSK waveform.
5. The resulting waveform is bandpass-filtered, amplified by the RF power amplifier, and radiated via the antenna.

For the demodulation (coherent detection) path:

1. The **noisy received signal** $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ enters the receiver.
2. A **locally generated carrier** $\cos(2\pi f_c t + \hat{\phi})$ — where $\hat{\phi}$ is the receiver's phase estimate — is multiplied with $r(t)$ in a coherent detector.
3. The product is **low-pass filtered** to remove the $2f_c$ component.
4. The resulting baseband signal is **sampled** at the optimum instant $t = T_b$.
5. The sample is compared against threshold **0**: positive → bit `'1'`, negative → bit `'0'`.
6. A **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** or **Costas loop** is required at the receiver to achieve carrier synchronization.

### 2.2 Signal-Space (Geometric) Representation

The two BPSK signal vectors form a **one-dimensional constellation** lying on the in-phase ($I$) axis:

$$
s_1 = +\sqrt{E_b} \quad \text{(bit 1)}, \qquad s_0 = -\sqrt{E_b} \quad \text{(bit 0)}
$$

The **Euclidean distance** between the two points is:

$$
d = \sqrt{E_b} - (-\sqrt{E_b}) = 2\sqrt{E_b}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric Insight:** Because the constellation is 1-D with both points equidistant from the origin, BPSK is mathematically equivalent to **antipodal signaling**. The decision boundary is a single point — the **origin (0)**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | KTU Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BPSK signal (bit 1) | $s_1(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts | Reference phase |
| 2 | BPSK signal (bit 0) | $s_0(t) = -A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts | 180° shifted |
| 3 | General form | $s(t) = m(t) A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts | $m(t) \in \{-1, +1\}$ |
| 4 | Bit energy | $E_b = \frac{A_c^2 T_b}{2}$ | Joules | Fundamental relation |
| 5 | Constellation points | $s_1 = +\sqrt{E_b}, \; s_0 = -\sqrt{E_b}$ | $\sqrt{\text{Joules}}$ | Orthonormal basis |
| 6 | Euclidean distance | $d = 2\sqrt{E_b}$ | $\sqrt{\text{Joules}}$ | Maximum for binary |
| 7 | Null-to-null BW | $B_{NULL} = 2R_b$ | Hz | Two-sided lobe width |
| 8 | 90% power BW | $B_{90\%} \approx 1.6 R_b$ | Hz | Main lobe + side lobes |
| 9 | Bandwidth efficiency | $\eta = \frac{R_b}{B} = 0.5$ | bps/Hz | Same as BFSK |
| 10 | Probability of error | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Dimensionless | **Most asked formula** |
| 11 | Equivalent form | $P_e = \text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right) \big/ 2$ | Dimensionless | Complementary error function |
| 12 | Spectral density (PSD) | $S_{BPSK}(f) = \dfrac{E_b}{2}\!\left[\delta(f-f_c)+\delta(f+f_c)\right]$ | W/Hz | For rectangular pulses |
| 13 | Continuous PSD | $\dfrac{E_b}{2}\left[\text{sinc}^2\!\left(\frac{f-f_c}{R_b}\right) + \text{sinc}^2\!\left(\frac{f+f_c}{R_b}\right)\right]$ | W/Hz | Practical baseband |
| 14 | SNR per bit relation | $\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} = \dfrac{S}{N} \cdot \dfrac{B}{R_b}$ | Dimensionless | Link budget use |

> [!WARNING]
> **Crucial LaTeX note for tables:** Absolute-value or magnitude bars **must** be written as `\vert \cdot \vert` or `\lvert \cdot \rvert` — never as the raw `|` symbol — to avoid breaking the markdown table.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

BPSK is the modulation scheme of choice in:
- **Deep-space telemetry** (NASA's Voyager 1 & 2 use BPSK at 8.4 GHz and 2.3 GHz X-band/K-band links).
- **Satellite command uplinks** (INSAT, GPS coarse-acquisition (C/A) codes use BPSK).
- **WLAN legacy headers** (802.11b Barker spreading uses DBPSK = Differential BPSK).
- **RFID backscatter** and **low-power IoT** systems where robustness matters more than spectral efficiency.
- **Standardized test modems** in laboratories for calibrating AWGN channel emulators.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Probability of Bit Error ($P_e$) for BPSK under AWGN

**Given:** BPSK signals $s_1(t) = \sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t)$ and $s_0(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t)$ where $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is the orthonormal basis function. Channel is AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$. Receiver uses a **coherent correlator**.

**Find:** The average probability of bit error $P_e$.

---

**Step 1:** Define the received signal during $[0, T_b]$ when bit `'1'` is transmitted:

$$
r(t) = +\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t) + n(t)
$$

Apply the correlator with basis $\phi_1(t)$:

$$
r = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt
$$

This projects $r(t)$ onto the 1-D signal space.

**Step 2:** Substitute $r(t)$ into the integral:

$$
r = \int_0^{T_b} \left[+\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t) + n(t)\right]\phi_1(t)\,dt
$$

**Step 3:** Use **linearity of the integral** to split it into two pieces:

$$
r = \sqrt{E_b}\int_0^{T_b} \phi_1^2(t)\,dt \;+\; \int_0^{T_b} n(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt
$$

**Step 4:** The first integral equals **1** by the orthonormal property:

$$
\int_0^{T_b} \phi_1^2(t)\,dt = \int_0^{T_b} \frac{2}{T_b}\cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\,dt = 1
$$

assuming $f_c T_b \gg 1$ (i.e., an integer number of carrier half-cycles per bit). So:

$$
r = \sqrt{E_b} + n
$$

where $n = \int_0^{T_b} n(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt$ is a **Gaussian random variable**.

**Step 5:** Characterize the noise. Since $n(t)$ is Gaussian with PSD $N_0/2$, the correlator output noise $n$ is also Gaussian with:

$$
\text{Mean: } E[n] = 0
$$

$$
\text{Variance: } \sigma_n^2 = \frac{N_0}{2}\int_0^{T_b}\phi_1^2(t)\,dt = \frac{N_0}{2}
$$

So $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$.

**Step 6:** Thus under hypothesis $H_1$ (bit `'1'` sent):

$$
r \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\sqrt{E_b},\; \frac{N_0}{2}\right)
$$

Similarly under $H_0$ (bit `'0'` sent):

$$
r \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(-\sqrt{E_b},\; \frac{N_0}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 7:** Optimal decision rule (Maximum A Posteriori with equal priors): **decide $H_1$ if $r > 0$** (threshold is 0 by symmetry).

**Step 8:** Probability of error **given bit 1 sent** = probability $r < 0$:

$$
P(e \mid H_1) = P(r < 0 \mid H_1) = \int_{-\infty}^{0} \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(r - \sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right) dr
$$

**Step 9:** Apply the change of variable $u = \dfrac{r - \sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}$:

When $r = 0$, $u = \dfrac{-\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}} = -\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}$.

The differential $dr = \sqrt{N_0/2}\,du$. The PDF transforms as:

$$
P(e \mid H_1) = \int_{-\infty}^{-\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}} e^{-u^2}\,du
$$

**Step 10:** Recognize this as the **Q-function** (complementary CDF of standard Gaussian):

$$
P(e \mid H_1) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

**Step 11:** By perfect symmetry of the constellation, $P(e \mid H_0) = P(e \mid H_1)$, so the **average** $P_e$ is:

$$
\boxed{P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}
$$

**Step 12:** Using the identity $Q(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$:

$$
P_e = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For a target $P_e = 10^{-5}$, BPSK requires $\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} \approx 9.6$ dB.** This is **3 dB better** than BFSK coherent and **6 dB better** than BFSK non-coherent. This 3-dB gap is a guaranteed KTU exam question.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Power Spectral Density of BPSK

**Step 1:** The baseband NRZ data $m(t) = \sum_k a_k p(t - kT_b)$ has PSD (assuming uncorrelated equiprobable bits $a_k \in \{-1, +1\}$):

$$
S_m(f) = T_b\,\text{sinc}^2(fT_b) = T_b\left(\frac{\sin(\pi f T_b)}{\pi f T_b}\right)^2
$$

**Step 2:** Modulation multiplies $m(t)$ by the carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$, which in frequency domain is a **shift-and-average** operation:

$$
S_{BPSK}(f) = \frac{1}{4}\left[S_m(f - f_c) + S_m(f + f_c)\right]
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the sinc form:

$$
S_{BPSK}(f) = \frac{T_b}{4}\left[\text{sinc}^2\!\left((f-f_c)T_b\right) + \text{sinc}^2\!\left((f+f_c)T_b\right)\right]
$$

**Step 4:** The first null occurs at $f - f_c = \pm 1/T_b$, giving a **null-to-null main-lobe bandwidth**:

$$
\boxed{B_{NULL} = \frac{2}{T_b} = 2R_b}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Matched-Filter Output SNR for BPSK

The matched filter impulse response is $h(t) = s(T_b - t)$. For a symbol $s(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ lasting $T_b$ seconds, output at $t = T_b$:

$$
y(T_b) = \int_0^{T_b} s(t)\,h(T_b - t)\,dt = \int_0^{T_b} s^2(t)\,dt = \frac{A_c^2 T_b}{2} = E_b
$$

The noise variance at matched-filter output:

$$
\sigma_n^2 = \frac{N_0}{2}\int_0^{T_b} h^2(t)\,dt = \frac{N_0}{2} E_b \cdot \frac{2}{A_c^2}\cdot \frac{A_c^2}{2} = \frac{N_0}{2}
$$

Wait — for a normalized BPSK correlator, the output SNR simplifies to:

$$
\text{SNR}_{out} = \frac{(\text{signal})^2}{\sigma_n^2} = \frac{E_b^2}{N_0/2} = \frac{2E_b}{N_0}
$$

Hence the argument of the Q-function is $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$, confirming the result in Derivation I.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A BPSK system operates at a bit rate of $R_b = 1$ Mbps over an AWGN channel with $N_0 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz. The received signal amplitude is $A_c = 10\,\mu\text{V}$ into a 50 Ω antenna at the decision instant. Compute the bit energy, the $E_b/N_0$ ratio, and the probability of bit error.

**Solution:**

$$
T_b = \frac{1}{R_b} = \frac{1}{10^6} = 1\;\mu\text{s}
$$

$$
E_b = \frac{A_c^2 T_b}{2} = \frac{(10 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 5 \times 10^{-17}\;\text{J}
$$

$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-17}}{10^{-20}} = 5000 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 10\log_{10}(5000) \approx 36.99\;\text{dB}
$$

$$
P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5000}{1}}\right) = Q(\sqrt{10000}) = Q(100)
$$

Since $Q(100) \approx 0$ (effectively zero errors — practically error-free), we conclude the system has **excellent margin**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's note:** Always convert $E_b/N_0$ to dB using $10\log_{10}(E_b/N_0)$ for a final answer, and **report $P_e$ in scientific notation** (e.g., $P_e \approx 2.87 \times 10^{-7}$) when $E_b/N_0$ is moderate (5 to 15 dB).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 BPSK Modulator — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Binary Bitstream d_k] --> nrzA[NRZ Encoder +1 to minus 1]
    nrzA --> multA[Balanced Multiplier]
    oscA[Local Oscillator cos 2 pi f_c t] --> multA
    multA --> bpfA[Bandpass Filter]
    bpfA --> ampA[RF Power Amplifier]
    ampA --> antA[Transmit Antenna]
    antA --> chanA[AWGN Channel n t]
    chanA --> rcvA[Receive Antenna]
    rcvA --> lnaA[Low Noise Amplifier]
    lnaA --> multB[Coherent Multiplier]
    oscB[Synchronous Carrier cos 2 pi f_c t plus phi] --> multB
    multB --> lpfA[Integrate and Dump Low Pass Filter]
    lpfA --> sampA[Sampler at t equals T_b]
    sampA --> decA[Threshold Detector greater than 0]
    decA --> outA[Recovered Bits d hat k]
    pllA[PLL or Costas Loop] -.->|phase estimate| oscB
```

### 4.2 BPSK Constellation Diagram (Signal-Space Map)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Constellation["In-Phase I Axis 1-D Signal Space"]
        p1((S1 equals plus sqrt E_b)) --- orig((Decision Boundary 0)) --- p0((S0 equals minus sqrt E_b))
    end
    note1["Bit 1 Region: r greater than 0"] -.-> orig
    note2["Bit 0 Region: r less than 0"] -.-> orig
```

### 4.3 BPSK Waveform Time-Domain Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    t1[Time axis t] --> a1
    subgraph a1[Bit pattern: 1 0 1 1 0]
        b1[Bit 1: +A_c cos 2 pi f_c t phase 0 deg]
        b2[Bit 0: minus A_c cos 2 pi f_c t phase 180 deg]
        b3[Bit 1: +A_c cos 2 pi f_c t phase 0 deg]
        b4[Bit 1: +A_c cos 2 pi f_c t phase 0 deg]
        b5[Bit 0: minus A_c cos 2 pi f_c t phase 180 deg]
    end
    f1[Carrier frequency f_c unchanged throughout] -.-> a1
    f2[Amplitude A_c unchanged throughout] -.-> a1
    f3[Phase flips by 180 deg at every bit boundary] -.-> a1
```

### 4.4 BPSK Receiver — Coherent Detection with Carrier Recovery

```mermaid
graph LR
    rfA[Received RF signal r t] --> bfA[Bandpass Filter matched to f_c]
    bfA --> spA[Signal Splitter]
    spA --> mulA[Mixer I branch]
    spA --> mulB[Mixer Q branch]
    loA[Voltage Controlled Oscillator VCO] --> pShift[90 deg Phase Shifter]
    loA --> mulA
    pShift --> mulB
    mulA --> intA[Integrator I arm]
    mulB --> intB[Integrator Q arm]
    intA --> decA[Sampler I]
    intB --> decB[Sampler Q]
    decA --> cmpA[Decision Device I greater than 0]
    decB --> cmpB[Decision Device Q greater than 0]
    cmpA --> outA[Recovered Bit d hat k]
    cmpB -->|error signal| lpA[Loop Filter]
    lpA --> vcoA[VCO Control Voltage]
    vcoA --> loA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram interpretation:** The Costas loop in the receiver is a *carrier-recovery* subsystem. It locks the local VCO to the suppressed-carrier BPSK signal by feeding back the cross-product of the I and Q arms. This is required because BPSK has **no carrier component** in its spectrum (the data toggles the phase by 180°, eliminating any DC line in the power spectrum).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Set B]**
Define Binary Phase Shift Keying. Sketch its constellation diagram and mention the phase values used to represent binary bits.

**Model Answer:**
Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK) is a digital modulation technique in which the phase of a sinusoidal carrier is varied between **0° and 180°** to represent the two binary symbols `0` and `1`, while keeping amplitude and frequency constant. The transmitted signal is given by

$$
s(t) = m(t)\,A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

where $m(t) = +1$ for bit `1` and $m(t) = -1$ for bit `0`.

**Constellation:** Two antipodal points on the in-phase axis at $+\sqrt{E_b}$ (bit 1) and $-\sqrt{E_b}$ (bit 0), with the decision boundary at the origin.

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Set A]**
State the expression for the probability of bit error of BPSK in an AWGN channel. What is the value of $E_b/N_0$ in dB required to achieve a $P_e = 10^{-5}$?

**Model Answer:**
The probability of bit error for coherent BPSK over AWGN is:

$$
P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

For $P_e = 10^{-5}$, we need $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right) = 10^{-5}$, i.e., $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0} \approx 4.265$, giving $E_b/N_0 \approx 9.1$ (linear) ≈ **9.6 dB**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**Question A** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the probability of bit error expression for a BPSK system operating in an AWGN channel. Assume coherent detection. (7 marks)

**(b)** A BPSK system transmits at a bit rate of $R_b = 2$ Mbps over an AWGN channel with noise PSD $N_0/2 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz. The received carrier amplitude at the detector input is $A_c = 50\;\mu\text{V}$ into 50 Ω. Calculate the bit error probability and the null-to-null bandwidth. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Step 1:** BPSK signals are antipodal:

$$
s_1(t) = \sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t), \quad s_0(t) = -\sqrt{E_b}\,\phi_1(t)
$$

where $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is the orthonormal basis function.

**Step 2:** Received signal $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ is correlated with $\phi_1(t)$:

$$
r = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt
$$

**Step 3:** This yields the test statistic $r = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n$, where $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$.

**Step 4:** Decision rule: choose $s_1$ if $r > 0$, else $s_0$.

**Step 5:** Probability of error under $H_1$:

$$
P(e \mid H_1) = \int_{-\infty}^{0} \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(r - \sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right)dr
$$

**Step 6:** Substituting $u = (r - \sqrt{E_b})/\sqrt{N_0/2}$:

$$
P(e \mid H_1) = \int_{-\infty}^{-\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-u^2/2}\,du = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

**Step 7:** By symmetry, average $P_e$ is the same:

$$
\boxed{P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}
$$

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1:** Compute bit duration:

$$
T_b = \frac{1}{R_b} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.5\;\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute signal power (over 50 Ω):

$$
P_s = \frac{A_c^2}{2R_L} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})^2}{2 \times 50} = 2.5 \times 10^{-11}\;\text{W}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute bit energy:

$$
E_b = P_s \cdot T_b = 2.5 \times 10^{-11} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} = 1.25 \times 10^{-17}\;\text{J}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $E_b/N_0$:

$$
N_0 = 2 \times 10^{-20}\;\text{W/Hz}
$$

$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-17}}{2 \times 10^{-20}} = 625
$$

In dB: $10\log_{10}(625) \approx 27.96$ dB.

**Step 5:** Compute probability of error:

$$
P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \times 625}\right) = Q(\sqrt{1250}) = Q(35.36) \approx 1.4 \times 10^{-269}
$$

(Practically zero — system is error-free.)

**Step 6:** Null-to-null bandwidth:

$$
B_{NULL} = 2R_b = 2 \times 2\;\text{Mbps} = \mathbf{4\;\text{MHz}}
$$

---

### 5.3 Part B Question Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**Question B** **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the generation and detection of BPSK signals. Discuss the role of the carrier recovery circuit. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare BPSK with BFSK in terms of bandwidth, bandwidth efficiency, and noise immunity. For the same bit energy $E_b$ and noise PSD $N_0$, what is the difference in $E_b/N_0$ requirement (in dB) at $P_e = 10^{-4}$? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Generation:** The BPSK modulator consists of an NRZ encoder that maps binary `0` → $-1$ V and `1` → $+1$ V. This NRZ waveform is multiplied with the carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ in a **balanced modulator** (a doubly-balanced mixer), producing $s(t) = m(t) A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$. The output is bandpass-filtered, amplified, and transmitted. (Block diagram shown in Section 4.1.)

**Detection (Coherent):** The received noisy signal $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ is multiplied with a **locally generated carrier** that is phase- and frequency-locked to the transmitter's carrier. The product contains a baseband term $\pm A_c^2/2$ and a $2f_c$ term that is removed by a low-pass filter. Sampling at $t = T_b$ and comparing with threshold 0 yields the recovered bit.

**Role of Carrier Recovery:** BPSK is a *suppressed-carrier* modulation — its spectrum has **no discrete line at $f_c$**. Therefore, the receiver cannot directly extract a reference carrier via a simple PLL on the received signal. A **Costas loop** or **squaring loop** is used:
- *Squaring loop:* Square the BPSK signal → $2f_c$ component emerges → divide by 2 with a PLL to recover the carrier.
- *Costas loop:* Uses two parallel I and Q correlators; the cross-product generates an error signal that drives a VCO to lock the local oscillator.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | BPSK | BFSK (coherent) | BFSK (non-coherent) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth | $2R_b$ | $2R_b$ (orthogonal) or $R_b$ (MSK-like) | $\geq 2R_b$ |
| Bandwidth efficiency | 0.5 bps/Hz | $\leq 0.5$ bps/Hz | $\leq 0.5$ bps/Hz |
| $P_e$ formula | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/2N_0}$ |
| Noise immunity | **Best (3 dB better than BFSK coherent)** | Moderate | Worst |
| Complexity | Needs coherent detection | Simpler | Easiest |

**Numerical comparison at $P_e = 10^{-4}$:**

For BPSK: $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}) = 10^{-4}$ → $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0} \approx 3.719$ → $E_b/N_0 \approx 6.91$ → **8.4 dB**.

For coherent BFSK: $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}) = 10^{-4}$ → $\sqrt{E_b/N_0} \approx 3.719$ → $E_b/N_0 \approx 13.83$ → **11.4 dB**.

**Difference: $11.4 - 8.4 = 3$ dB in favor of BPSK.** This is the canonical 3-dB gain of BPSK over coherent BFSK.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ½ in $E_b$:** Many students write $E_b = A_c^2 T_b$ (forgetting the time-average over $\cos^2$ gives a factor of ½). This single mistake costs **2 marks** in any derivation.
> 2. **Mixing Q and erfc:** $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$. Both are acceptable, but mixing them mid-solution loses coherence marks.
> 3. **Plotting the wrong PSD:** The BPSK PSD has **no impulse at $f_c$** because the data is zero-mean (equal numbers of `+1` and `-1`). Drawing a delta at $f_c$ is incorrect — that belongs to plain AM or DSB-SC with pilot.
> 4. **Threshold ≠ 0.5 V:** In BPSK, the decision threshold is exactly **0 V** at the correlator output, not 0.5 of the signal amplitude. Confusing with unipolar NRZ is a classic blunder.
> 5. **Not citing carrier recovery:** Full-mark answers to "explain BPSK detection" must mention **Costas loop or squaring loop**. Skipping this loses 2 marks in part (a).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BPSK = 2-PSK = PRK** is the simplest and most noise-immune binary modulation. **Phase alone carries information.**
- The two signal vectors are **antipodal** (180° apart, distance $2\sqrt{E_b}$).
- **Modulator:** NRZ data × carrier in a balanced mixer. **Demodulator:** Coherent correlator + threshold detector at 0.
- **Carrier recovery is mandatory** because BPSK has a suppressed carrier — use Costas loop or squaring loop.
- **$P_e$ formula:** $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ — the single most important equation.
- **Null-to-null bandwidth:** $B = 2R_b$. **Bandwidth efficiency:** 0.5 bps/Hz.
- **PSD shape:** $\text{sinc}^2$ lobes centered at $\pm f_c$; **no spectral line at $f_c$**.
- **Bit energy:** $E_b = A_c^2 T_b/2$. **Power:** $P_s = A_c^2/2$ (over 1 Ω) or $A_c^2/(2R_L)$ (over load $R_L$).
- **3-dB rule:** BPSK is **3 dB better** than coherent BFSK, and **6 dB better** than non-coherent BFSK at the same $P_e$.
- **Use BPSK when** robustness is paramount (deep-space, satellite command, low-SNR IoT). Avoid it when spectral efficiency matters — use **QPSK/OQPSK/π/4-DQPSK** instead.
- **Synchronization is critical:** Bit-timing recovery (early-late gate or Gardner algorithm) and carrier phase recovery must both work, or the BER degrades catastrophically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "derive the probability of error," always start with the orthonormal basis function, project the received signal, characterize the noise variance, write the two Gaussian PDFs $p(r \mid H_1)$ and $p(r \mid H_0)$, and integrate from the threshold to $-\infty$ or $+\infty$ depending on the hypothesis. This 6-step ritual is what examiners look for — deviation from this structure costs organization marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
