# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Digital Modulator

The constellation is generated by mapping bits to I/Q voltages and then upconverting them with orthogonal carriers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Binary Source] --> enc[Channel Encoder]
    enc --> group[Bit-to-Symbol Grouping k bits]
    group --> map[Constellation Mapper I and Q values]
    map --> di[DAC Low Pass Filter]
    di --> mixI[Multiplier 1]
    di --> mixQ[Multiplier 2]
    osc[Carrier Oscillator fc] --> mixI
    osc2[90 deg Phase Shifter] --> mixQ
    mixI --> add[Linear Adder]
    mixQ --> add
    add --> ant[Transmit Antenna]
```

| Block | Function | Output Domain |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Bit-to-Symbol Grouping | Collects $k = \log_2 M$ bits | Bit stream |
| Constellation Mapper | Maps bits to I and Q amplitudes | Baseband I/Q voltages |
| DAC + LPF | Smooths baseband pulse shapes | Analogue baseband |
| Carrier Oscillator | Generates $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | RF reference |
| 90° Phase Shifter | Produces $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ | Quadrature RF |
| Multipliers (×2) | Upconverts I and Q to bandpass | Two bandpass signals |
| Linear Adder | Combines into single RF signal | $s(t)$ ready for antenna |

## 4.2 Decision Flow at the Receiver (Minimum-Distance Detector)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rx[Received Noisy Signal r of t] --> cohI[Coherent I Demodulator]
    rx --> cohQ[Coherent Q Demodulator]
    oscR[Local Oscillator fc] --> cohI
    oscR2[90 deg Phase Shifter] --> cohQ
    cohI --> sampI[I Sample and Hold]
    cohQ --> sampQ[Q Sample and Hold]
    sampI --> dist[Compute Distance to All M Points]
    sampQ --> dist
    dist --> det[Select Closest Point and Output Symbol]
    det --> out[Recovered k bits]
```

| Stage | Operation | KTU Relevance |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Coherent Demodulation | Correlate with $\cos$ and $\sin$ | Yields noisy I and Q |
| Sample and Hold | Latch I and Q at decision instant | Provides $(I_r, Q_r)$ |
| Distance Computation | $\sqrt{(I_r - I_i)^2 + (Q_r - Q_i)^2}$ for each $i$ | Inner product of vectors |
| Decision | Choose index with minimum distance | ML detection under AWGN |

## 4.3 Conceptual Map of the Constellation Landscape

```mermaid
graph TD
    constellation[Digital Modulation Constellation] --> psk[PSK Family]
    constellation --> qam[QAM Family]
    constellation --> ask[ASK Family]
    constellation --> fsk[FSK Family]
    psk --> bpsk[BPSK M equals 2]
    psk --> qpsk[QPSK M equals 4]
    psk --> psk8[8 PSK M equals 8]
    psk --> psk16[16 PSK M equals 16]
    qam --> qam16[16 QAM Square Grid]
    qam --> qam64[64 QAM Square Grid]
    qam --> qam256[256 QAM]
    ask --> ook[OOK On Off Keying]
    ask --> ask4[4 ASK]
    fsk --> bfsk[BFSK Coherent]
    fsk --> bfskn[BFSK Non Coherent]
    fsk --> msk[MSK and GMSK]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Map:** PSK keeps the amplitude constant (points on a circle). QAM varies both amplitude and phase (points on a lattice). ASK varies only amplitude (points on the I-axis). FSK does not easily map to the 2-D plane because it varies **frequency**, not amplitude or phase.

## 4.4 Constellation Comparison Schematic (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1[Constellation Shape] --> a2[Power Efficiency dmin]
    a1 --> a3[Spectral Efficiency k]
    a2 --> a4[BER at Given SNR]
    a3 --> a4
    a4 --> a5[Application Choice]
    a1 --> b1[2 Points BPSK]
    a1 --> b2[4 Points QPSK]
    a1 --> b3[8 Points 8 PSK]
    a1 --> b4[16 Points 16 QAM]
    a1 --> b5[64 Points 64 QAM]
    a5 --> c1[Deep Space Telemetry BPSK]
    a5 --> c2[Cellular Voice QPSK]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** What is meant by a *signal constellation diagram*? Why is the minimum Euclidean distance $d_{min}$ between adjacent constellation points the single most important parameter?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **signal constellation diagram** is a two-dimensional I-Q plot in which each permissible transmitted symbol of a digitally modulated signal is represented as a discrete point whose horizontal and vertical coordinates are the in-phase $(I)$ and quadrature $(Q)$ components of the symbol phasor. (1 mark)

The minimum Euclidean distance $d_{min}$ is the smallest separation between any two constellation points. Under additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN), the probability of a symbol error is dominated by the noise vectors that push a received point across a decision boundary. (1 mark)

The probability of error is approximately $P_s \approx 2Q\!\left(\dfrac{d_{min}}{2\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right)$, hence a larger $d_{min}$ reduces the BER exponentially. Thus $d_{min}$ quantifies the **noise immunity** of the modulation scheme and is the primary metric used to compare schemes at equal average energy. (1 mark)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** Distinguish between **M-PSK** and **M-QAM** signal constellations. In which application is M-QAM preferred over M-PSK and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | M-PSK | M-QAM |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Constellation shape | Points lie on a **single circle** of constant radius | Points lie on a **rectangular lattice** |
| Amplitude variation | Constant amplitude | Variable amplitude |
| Bandwidth efficiency | Lower (for high $M$) | Higher (linear growth in $k$) |
| Power efficiency | Worse (envelope of M-PSK collapses) | Better — packing is denser |

(2 marks for the comparison)

M-QAM is preferred in **bandwidth-limited, high-data-rate applications** such as **Wi-Fi (802.11), 4G/5G NR, and digital cable TV (DVB-C)** because it packs more bits per Hz of bandwidth. (1 mark) However, M-QAM requires **linear power amplifiers** to preserve amplitude information, whereas M-PSK can be amplified by saturating (non-linear) amplifiers, which is why satellite links often prefer M-PSK.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2]**
> **Q3 (a).** Draw the signal constellation diagrams for **BPSK, QPSK, and 16-QAM**. For each, list the coordinates, the number of bits per symbol, and the minimum Euclidean distance in terms of the average symbol energy $E_s$. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

**BPSK** — 2 points on the I-axis:

| Point | $I$ | $Q$ | Phase |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $s_0$ | $+\sqrt{E_b}$ | $0$ | $0°$ |
| $s_1$ | $-\sqrt{E_b}$ | $0$ | $180°$ |

Number of bits per symbol: $k = 1$. Minimum distance: $d_{min} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$.

**QPSK** — 4 points on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$ at $\pm 45°, \pm 135°$:

| Point | $I$ | $Q$ | Phase |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $s_{00}$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $45°$ |
| $s_{01}$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $135°$ |
| $s_{11}$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $225°$ |
| $s_{10}$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $315°$ |

Number of bits per symbol: $k = 2$. Minimum distance: $d_{min} = \sqrt{2E_s}$.

**16-QAM** — 16 points on a $4 \times 4$ rectangular grid. With amplitude levels $\pm a$ and $\pm 3a$ on each axis:

$$d_{min}^{(16\text{-QAM})} = 2a, \quad E_{s,avg} = 10a^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; d_{min} = 2\sqrt{\tfrac{E_{s,avg}}{10}}\cdot \sqrt{2} = \sqrt{\tfrac{8E_{s,avg}}{10}}$$

Number of bits per symbol: $k = 4$. Minimum distance: $d_{min} = \sqrt{8E_{s,avg}/10} = \sqrt{0.8\,E_{s,avg}}$.

> **Diagram (in I-Q plane):** For BPSK plot two points on horizontal axis; for QPSK plot four points on the diagonals (45° spacing); for 16-QAM plot the $4 \times 4$ grid of 16 points centred at origin. (2 marks for diagrams + 5 marks for tabulated values)

---

> **(b)** A digital communication system uses **QPSK** with a carrier frequency of 2.4 GHz and a symbol rate of 1 MSymbol/s. Compute (i) the bit rate, (ii) the spectral efficiency, and (iii) the minimum distance if the average symbol energy is $10^{-15}\,\text{J}$. Given an AWGN spectral density of $N_0/2 = 10^{-20}\,\text{W/Hz}$, find (iv) the bit error probability using $P_b \approx Q\!\big(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\big)$. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

**(i) Bit Rate** — QPSK carries $k = 2$ bits per symbol:

$$R_b = k \cdot R_s = 2 \times 10^6 = 2 \;\text{Mbps}$$

**(ii) Spectral Efficiency** — Assuming Nyquist filtering, the minimum bandwidth equals the symbol rate:

$$\eta = \frac{R_b}{B} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{1 \times 10^6} = 2 \;\text{bits/s/Hz}$$

**(iii) Minimum Distance** — For QPSK, $E_b = E_s/k$:

$$E_b = \frac{10^{-15}}{2} = 5 \times 10^{-16}\,\text{J}$$

$$d_{min}^{(\text{QPSK})} = \sqrt{2 E_s} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^{-15}} = 1.414 \times 10^{-7.5} = 1.414 \times 10^{-7.5}\,\text{V·s}^{1/2}$$

Numerically, $d_{min} = 1.414 \times 10^{-7.5} \approx 4.47 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{V (in matched-filter units)}$.

**(iv) Bit Error Probability**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{2E_b}{N_0} &= \frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-16}}{2 \times 10^{-20}} = 5 \times 10^{4} \\
\sqrt{2E_b/N_0} &= \sqrt{5 \times 10^4} = 223.6 \\
P_b &\approx Q(223.6) \approx 0
\end{aligned}$$

The result indicates that at this SNR the system is essentially **error-free** — a typical KTU observation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> 1. **Common Mistake (–1 mark):** Confusing $E_s$ and $E_b$. Always write $E_b = E_s/\log_2 M$ explicitly.
> 2. **Common Mistake (–1 mark):** Using $R_b = R_s$ for QPSK. QPSK has $k = 2$, so $R_b = 2 R_s$.
> 3. **Common Mistake (–2 marks):** Reporting BER for BPSK formula when the question specifies QPSK. The two are numerically equal *for AWGN with Gray coding*, but the student must say so and justify using the same Q-function.
> 4. **Pitfall:** Failing to mention the unit of $d_{min}$. It is **not** in metres or volts alone, but in $\sqrt{\text{energy}}$ or, equivalently, volts-seconds$^{1/2}$ in matched-filter output.

---

## 5.3 Alternative Choice Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 2]**
> **Q4 (a).** With a neat constellation diagram, explain **16-QAM**. Derive the expression for the average transmitted energy per symbol in terms of the minimum distance $d_{min}$. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

**Constellation Diagram (textual representation):**

A 16-QAM constellation has 16 points arranged in a $4 \times 4$ square grid. The I and Q coordinates each take the values $\{-3a, -a, +a, +3a\}$ where $a$ is a positive real number. Adjacent points are spaced by $d_{min} = 2a$ horizontally or vertically.

**Derivation of Average Energy:**

The in-phase amplitudes are $\pm a$ and $\pm 3a$, occurring with equal frequency. The mean square of the I-component is:

$$\langle I^2 \rangle = \frac{1}{4}\big(a^2 + 9a^2 + a^2 + 9a^2\big) = 5a^2$$

By symmetry $\langle Q^2 \rangle = 5a^2$. The average symbol energy is:

$$E_{s,avg} = \langle I^2 \rangle + \langle Q^2 \rangle = 10a^2$$

Expressing $a$ in terms of $d_{min} = 2a$:

$$a = \frac{d_{min}}{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; E_{s,avg} = 10\left(\frac{d_{min}}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{10\,d_{min}^{\,2}}{4} = \frac{5\,d_{min}^{\,2}}{2}$$

For general square M-QAM ($M = L^2$), the analogous derivation yields:

$$\boxed{\;E_{s,avg} = \frac{(M-1)\,d_{min}^{\,2}}{6}\;}$$

(marks distribution: 3 for the diagram and explanation, 4 for the derivation)

---

> **(b)** Compare the **spectral efficiency** of BPSK, QPSK, 8-PSK, 16-QAM, and 64-QAM for a fixed bit rate of 100 Mbps. Comment on the trade-off between bandwidth and power efficiency. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

For a fixed bit rate $R_b$, the symbol rate is $R_s = R_b/\log_2 M$. Assuming Nyquist filtering, the required bandwidth is $B \approx R_s$. The spectral efficiency is $\eta = R_b/B = \log_2 M$.

| Scheme | $M$ | $\log_2 M$ | $R_s$ (Msps) | $B$ (MHz) | $\eta$ (bits/s/Hz) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| BPSK | 2 | 1 | 100 | 100 | 1 |
| QPSK | 4 | 2 | 50 | 50 | 2 |
| 8-PSK | 8 | 3 | 33.3 | 33.3 | 3 |
| 16-QAM | 16 | 4 | 25 | 25 | 4 |
| 64-QAM | 64 | 6 | 16.67 | 16.67 | 6 |

**Trade-off Discussion (3 marks):**

1. As $M$ increases, the **bandwidth** required for the same bit rate decreases proportionally to $1/\log_2 M$. This is the *bandwidth advantage* of high-order modulation.
2. However, the **minimum distance** $d_{min}$ shrinks as $M$ grows, and the **energy per bit** required to maintain the same BER increases. For M-PSK, $E_b \propto 1/\sin^2(\pi/M)$, while for M-QAM, $E_b \propto (M-1)/(3 \log_2 M)$.
3. Therefore, **higher-order schemes (64-QAM, 256-QAM) require higher SNR** to operate at the same error rate. They are chosen only when the channel SNR is high (e.g., short-range, line-of-sight) and bandwidth is the bottleneck. Low-order schemes (BPSK, QPSK) are preferred when power is the constraint (e.g., deep-space links, cellular uplink).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> – Forgetting the Nyquist assumption: without Nyquist filtering, $B > R_s$ and the spectral efficiency is **lower** than $\log_2 M$. Always state "under Nyquist filtering" explicitly.
> – Confusing *spectral efficiency* with *power efficiency*. They are inversely related for fixed BER — this is the **Shannon limit trade-off**.
> – Failing to note that 64-QAM is **6 bits/symbol** (not 5 or 7). $2^6 = 64$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2 — Signal Constellations)**

- A **signal constellation** is the I–Q plane plot of all permitted transmitted symbols of a digital modulation scheme. **(Core concept)**
- The **two orthogonal basis functions** are $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$. The coordinates of each point are $(I_i, Q_i)$.
- **Bits per symbol:** $k = \log_2 M$.
- **Symbol energy:** $E_s = k E_b$.
- **BPSK:** 2 antipodal points on I-axis; $d_{min} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$.
- **QPSK:** 4 points at $45°$ spacing; same $d_{min}$ as BPSK at the same $E_b$ but **half the bandwidth**.
- **M-PSK:** points on a single circle; $d_{min} = 2\sqrt{E_s}\sin(\pi/M)$. Power efficiency drops with $M$.
- **M-QAM (square):** points on a square lattice; average energy $E_{s,avg} = (M-1)\,d_{min}^{\,2}/6$. More power-efficient than M-PSK for the same $M > 4$.
- **BFSK (coherent):** orthogonal vectors; $d_{min} = \sqrt{2E_b}$. ~3 dB worse than BPSK but more robust to phase errors.
- **Decision regions:** Voronoi cells around each point. Receiver picks the closest point (maximum-likelihood under AWGN).
- **Spectral efficiency** (Nyquist, ideal): $\eta = \log_2 M$ bits/s/Hz.
- **Power vs bandwidth trade-off:** Higher $M$ ⇒ higher $\eta$ but smaller $d_{min}$ and higher required $E_b/N_0$.
- **Gray coding:** adjacent constellation points differ in only 1 bit, so a single nearest-neighbour error causes only 1 bit error, not $k$.
- **Standard KTU numerical values to memorise:** $d_{min}$ for BPSK, QPSK, BFSK, 8-PSK, 16-QAM; average energy for 16-QAM; spectral efficiency for the 5–6 most common schemes.
- **Lab connection:** An oscilloscope in **I-Q mode** displays the live constellation of a real transmitted signal. A "cloudy" plot indicates noise; a "rotating" plot indicates frequency offset; a "smeared arc" indicates phase noise.
- **Common KTU traps:** unit confusion between $E_s$ and $E_b$, forgetting $k$ in the spectral-efficiency formula, ignoring Gray coding, and drawing constellations without explicit axis labels ($-I$ to $+I$, $-Q$ to $+Q$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT416 - PRINCIPLES OF COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transmission Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In digital communication systems, **Transmission Rate** is the fundamental metric that quantifies the speed at which digital information propagates from a source to a destination across a physical communication channel. It is expressed as the number of information units (bits or symbols) successfully delivered per unit time, typically measured in **bits per second (bps)** or **symbols per second (Baud)**.

The transmission rate is governed by two critical, often confused, parameters:

- **Bit Rate ($R_b$)**: The number of *bits* transmitted per second. Unit: **bits per second (bps)**.
- **Baud Rate / Symbol Rate ($R_s$)**: The number of *signal elements (symbols)* transmitted per second. Unit: **Baud (Bd)** or **symbols per second**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A common student misconception is treating Bit Rate and Baud Rate as identical. They are equal **only** for binary signaling ($M=2$). For $M$-ary signaling, $R_b > R_s$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Model

Imagine a toll-booth highway where each toll booth represents a **signal symbol**:

- **Bits** are *individual passengers* entering vehicles.
- **Symbols** are *vehicles* passing the toll booth per second.
- A **car** can carry 1 passenger (binary, 1 bit/symbol) or a **bus** can carry 4 passengers ($M=16$, 4 bits/symbol).

If 100 buses/sec pass through (Baud Rate = 100), and each bus carries 4 passengers, then the **Bit Rate** = 100 × 4 = **400 bits/sec**, even though only 100 vehicles crossed.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Bit Rate** answers: *"How much digital information arrives per second?"* The **Baud Rate** answers: *"How fast do channel transitions (signaling events) occur per second?"*

### 1.3 Engineering Metrics Summary

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Bit Rate | $R_b$ | **bps** (bits/s) | Information throughput |
| Symbol Rate | $R_s$ | **Baud (Bd)** | Channel symbol transitions |
| Bandwidth | $B$ | **Hz** | Spectral occupancy of channel |
| Channel Capacity | $C$ | **bps** | Maximum achievable bit rate |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Transmission Rate

The transmission rate is not arbitrarily chosen — it is constrained by **three physical laws** of the channel:

1. **Channel Bandwidth ($B$)** — The channel acts as a low-pass filter; faster transitions require wider spectra.
2. **Signal-to-Noise Ratio ($S/N$)** — A noisier channel demands lower rates to maintain acceptable error performance.
3. **Coding Scheme ($M$-ary signaling)** — Each symbol can carry $\log_2(M)$ bits, allowing higher bit rates within the same bandwidth at the cost of noise immunity.

### 2.2 The Three Governing Theorems

**Theorem 1 — Nyquist Rate (Noiseless Channel, Maximum Signaling Rate):**

For an ideal noiseless channel of bandwidth $B$, the maximum symbol rate is limited to $2B$ transitions/second (based on the Nyquist sampling theorem applied to pulse shaping).

**Theorem 2 — Nyquist Bit Rate (Noiseless Channel, Bit Rate Limit):**

For an $M$-ary system (where $M$ is the number of distinct signal levels/symbols), the maximum achievable bit rate is:

$$C_{Nyquist} = 2B \log_2(M) \text{ bps}$$

**Theorem 3 — Shannon-Hartley Theorem (Noisy Channel Capacity):**

For a channel with additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN), the maximum error-free bit rate (channel capacity) is:

$$C_{Shannon} = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \text{ bps}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Distinction:**
> - **Nyquist** defines the *ideal* theoretical limit assuming no noise and perfect pulses.
> - **Shannon** defines the *ultimate* limit considering thermal noise; it cannot be exceeded by *any* coding scheme.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $R_b = R_s \cdot \log_2(M)$ | Bit Rate from Symbol Rate | bps; $M$ = number of levels |
| 2 | $R_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Symbol Rate from Symbol Duration | Baud; $T_s$ = symbol period (s) |
| 3 | $R_b = \dfrac{1}{T_b}$ | Bit Rate from Bit Duration | bps; $T_b$ = bit period (s) |
| 4 | $T_b = T_s \cdot \log_2(M)$ | Bit Duration vs Symbol Duration | seconds |
| 5 | $C_{Nyquist} = 2B\log_2(M)$ | Max bit rate (noiseless) | bps; bandwidth $B$ (Hz) |
| 6 | $C_{Shannon} = B\log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Max bit rate (noisy) | bps; $S/N$ is power ratio |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{R_b}{B}$ | Bandwidth Efficiency | bits/s/Hz |
| 8 | $S/N_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(S/N)$ | SNR in decibels | dB (linear ratio $S/N$) |

> [!NOTE]
> Always convert SNR from **dB to linear scale** before plugging into the Shannon formula. Example: $30 \text{ dB} \Rightarrow S/N = 10^{30/10} = 1000$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **5G NR (New Radio):** Uses adaptive $M$-QAM (QPSK to 256-QAM) to dynamically trade bit rate for noise robustness.
- **Optical Fiber Links:** Operate near Shannon limits using advanced FEC (Forward Error Correction).
- **Satellite Communications:** Severely power-limited; use very low symbol rates with high-order modulation.
- **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3):** Bit rates are designed using Nyquist criterion with raised-cosine pulse shaping to limit intersymbol interference.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Bit Rate and Symbol Rate

**Given:** Each signal symbol conveys $N$ bits of information, where $M$ discrete signal levels are used.

**Step 1 — Information per Symbol:**
By the fundamental definition of information (Hartley's logarithmic measure), the number of bits carried by one of $M$ equiprobable symbols is:

$$N = \log_2(M) \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Step 2 — Bit Rate Definition:**
Bit rate is the number of bits transmitted per second. If $R_s$ symbols are transmitted each second, and each symbol carries $N$ bits, then:

$$R_b = R_s \times N$$

**Step 3 — Substitution:**
Substituting $N = \log_2(M)$ into the equation:

$$R_b = R_s \cdot \log_2(M) \text{ bps}$$

**Step 4 — Special Case (Binary, $M=2$):**
When $M=2$ (e.g., NRZ, Manchester), $\log_2(2) = 1$, hence $R_b = R_s \cdot 1 = R_s$.

$\therefore$ In binary transmission, **Bit Rate = Symbol Rate**.

**Step 5 — General Case ($M=4$, QPSK):**
$\log_2(4) = 2$, so $R_b = 2R_s$. The bit rate is **twice** the symbol rate.

### 3.2 Derivation: Shannon Channel Capacity

**Given:** A channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz with signal power $S$ and additive white Gaussian noise power $N$.

**Step 1 — Shannon's Information-Theoretic Argument:**
Shannon (1948) proved that the maximum number of distinguishable signal levels in a band-limited, noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ is proportional to the channel's capacity to resolve signal states. He defined the **Number of Distinct Signal Levels** that can be reliably distinguished in time $T$ as:

$$M_{max} = \left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)^{2BT}$$

**Step 2 — Bits per Symbol (Hypothesis Testing):**
The information conveyed per symbol (in bits) is the base-2 logarithm of distinguishable states:

$$\text{Bits/symbol} = \log_2(M_{max}) = 2BT \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Bits per Second (Capacity):**
Dividing by the transmission time $T$ to obtain bits per second:

$$C = \frac{\text{Bits/symbol}}{T} = \frac{2BT \log_2(1 + S/N)}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Final Simplification:**
The $T$ terms cancel, yielding the canonical Shannon-Hartley theorem:

$$C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \text{ bps}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Exam Pattern)

#### Example 1: Binary vs 16-QAM Comparison

**Problem:** A channel has bandwidth $B = 4000$ Hz. Compute the maximum bit rate for (a) Binary signaling, (b) 16-QAM signaling (assuming noiseless conditions).

**Solution (a) — Binary ($M=2$):**
$$C_{Nyquist} = 2B\log_2(M) = 2(4000)\log_2(2) = 2(4000)(1)$$
$$C = 8000 \text{ bps}$$

**Solution (b) — 16-QAM ($M=16$):**
$$C_{Nyquist} = 2(4000)\log_2(16) = 2(4000)(4)$$
$$C = 32000 \text{ bps}$$

**Result:** 16-QAM provides **4× higher bit rate** in the same bandwidth by using 16 distinguishable amplitude/phase states.

#### Example 2: Shannon Capacity with SNR

**Problem:** A telephone channel has $B = 3100$ Hz and $S/N = 30$ dB. Find the Shannon capacity.

**Solution:**

Step A — Convert dB to linear scale:
$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000$$

Step B — Apply Shannon's formula:
$$C = B \log_2(1 + S/N) = 3100 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000)$$
$$C = 3100 \cdot \log_2(1001)$$

Step C — Evaluate logarithm:
$$\log_2(1001) = \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} \approx \frac{3.0004}{0.3010} \approx 9.967 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

Step D — Final capacity:
$$C = 3100 \times 9.967 \approx 30898 \text{ bps} \approx 30.9 \text{ kbps}$$

This matches the historical limit of the voice-band modem (≈ 33.6 kbps max).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Transmission Rate Hierarchy

The following block diagram illustrates the conceptual relationship between the source data rate, channel symbol rate, and Shannon-imposed physical limits.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Information Source"] --> B["Source Encoder"]
    B --> C["Channel Encoder FEC"]
    C --> D["Digital Modulator M-ary"]
    D --> E["Channel with Bandwidth B and Noise"]
    E --> F["Digital Demodulator"]
    F --> G["Channel Decoder"]
    G --> H["Source Decoder"]
    H --> I["Information Sink"]

    subgraph Limits["Governing Physical Limits"]
        L1["Nyquist Limit 2B log2 M bps"]
        L2["Shannon Limit B log2 1 plus SN bps"]
    end

    D -.imposes.-> L1
    E -.imposes.-> L2

    subgraph Rates["Rate Definitions"]
        R1["Bit Rate Rb bits per second"]
        R2["Symbol Rate Rs Baud"]
    end

    R1 --> D
    R2 --> D
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow — Selecting Maximum Transmission Rate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start Given Channel B and SN"] --> Q1{"Is the channel noise free?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> N["Apply Nyquist Formula C equals 2B log2 M"]
    Q1 -- No --> S2["Apply Shannon Formula C equals B log2 1 plus SN"]
    N --> N1["Choose M to maximize C"]
    S2 --> S3["C is the absolute upper bound"]
    S3 --> S4["No code can exceed C"]
    N1 --> End["Achievable Rate"]
    S4 --> End
```

### 4.3 Comparative Summary — Bit Rate vs Symbol Rate

| Scenario | Modulation Type | M value | log base 2 of M | Bit Rate vs Symbol Rate |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Binary NRZ / Manchester | 2-PAM, BPSK | 2 | 1 | $R_b = R_s$ |
| QPSK / 4-QAM | 4-PSK | 4 | 2 | $R_b = 2R_s$ |
| 16-QAM | 16-QAM | 16 | 4 | $R_b = 4R_s$ |
| 64-QAM | 64-QAM | 64 | 6 | $R_b = 6R_s$ |
| 256-QAM | 256-QAM | 256 | 8 | $R_b = 8R_s$ |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Bit Rate and Baud Rate. How are they related for an M-ary signaling system?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Bit Rate ($R_b$):** The number of bits transmitted per second over a communication channel, measured in **bits per second (bps)**. (1 mark)
- **Baud Rate / Symbol Rate ($R_s$):** The number of signal elements (symbols) transmitted per second, measured in **Baud (Bd)**. (1 mark)
- **Relationship:** For an $M$-ary system, $R_b = R_s \cdot \log_2(M)$ bps. For binary ($M=2$), $R_b = R_s$. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State the Shannon-Hartley theorem. What is its significance in digital communication?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement:** The maximum error-free transmission rate (channel capacity) over a noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$ is: $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ bps. (2 marks)
- **Significance:** It establishes the *absolute theoretical upper bound* on the bit rate; no amount of clever coding or modulation can exceed it. It guides engineers in trading off bandwidth, power, and data rate. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *Digital Hierarchy & Nyquist Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the bit rate ($R_b$), symbol rate ($R_s$), and the number of signal levels ($M$) in a digital communication system. Why is the Nyquist rate defined as $2B$ symbols/second? Explain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A channel of bandwidth $200$ kHz is used to transmit data using a 64-QAM scheme. Calculate: (i) the maximum symbol rate, (ii) the maximum bit rate, and (iii) the bandwidth efficiency. Assume an ideal noiseless channel. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

1. Each of $M$ distinct symbols carries $N = \log_2(M)$ bits of information. *(1 mark)*
2. If $R_s$ symbols are sent per second, then bits per second = symbols per second × bits per symbol. *(1 mark)*
3. Hence: $R_b = R_s \log_2(M)$ bps. *(1 mark)*
4. Nyquist's sampling theorem states that a band-limited signal of bandwidth $B$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from samples taken at $f_s \geq 2B$ samples/second. *(2 marks)*
5. Applied to pulse transmission: to detect each pulse without intersymbol interference, the pulse rate (symbol rate) must not exceed $2B$ pulses per second. This is the **Nyquist rate** for signaling. *(1 mark)*
6. Substituting $R_s \leq 2B$ into the bit-rate equation gives the Nyquist bit-rate limit: $C_{Nyquist} = 2B \log_2(M)$ bps. *(1 mark)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given: $B = 200$ kHz, $M = 64$ (64-QAM), ideal noiseless channel.

(i) **Maximum Symbol Rate** *(2 marks)*

$$R_{s,max} = 2B = 2 \times 200 \text{ kHz} = 400 \text{ k symbols/sec}$$

(ii) **Maximum Bit Rate** *(3 marks)*

$$\log_2(64) = 6 \text{ bits/symbol}$$
$$R_{b,max} = 2B \log_2(M) = 2(200{,}000) \times 6$$
$$R_{b,max} = 2{,}400{,}000 \text{ bps} = 2.4 \text{ Mbps}$$

(iii) **Bandwidth Efficiency** *(2 marks)*

$$\eta = \frac{R_b}{B} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^3} = 12 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Shannon Capacity & Noisy Channel Design*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Alternative]**

**(a)** Derive the Shannon-Hartley theorem for the capacity of a noisy channel. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A voice-band channel has $B = 3.4$ kHz and an SNR of $40$ dB. Determine: (i) the Shannon capacity, (ii) the minimum SNR (in dB) required to double the capacity while keeping bandwidth constant. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

1. **Assumptions:** AWGN channel, bandwidth-limited to $B$ Hz, signal power $S$, noise power $N$. *(1 mark)*
2. By Shannon's information theory, the number of distinguishable signal levels transmittable in time $T$ over such a channel is: $M = (1 + S/N)^{2BT}$. *(2 marks)*
3. Each level conveys $\log_2(M)$ bits, so the total information transmitted in time $T$ is: $I = 2BT \log_2(1 + S/N)$ bits. *(2 marks)*
4. Capacity = information per second = $I/T$, hence: $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ bps. *(1 mark)*
5. **Key insight:** This is the *absolute* upper bound; achievable only with ideal coding (infinite code length). *(1 mark)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given: $B = 3.4$ kHz, SNR = $40$ dB.

(i) **Shannon Capacity** *(3 marks)*

Convert SNR to linear: $S/N = 10^{40/10} = 10^4 = 10{,}000$.

$$C = B \log_2(1 + S/N) = 3400 \cdot \log_2(10001)$$
$$\log_2(10001) \approx 13.288$$
$$C \approx 3400 \times 13.288 \approx 45{,}180 \text{ bps} \approx 45.2 \text{ kbps}$$

(ii) **SNR for Doubled Capacity** *(4 marks)*

New capacity: $C' = 2C = 90.36$ kbps.

$$\frac{C'}{B} = 2 \log_2(1 + S/N_{new})$$
$$\frac{90{,}360}{3400} = 26.576 = 2 \log_2(1 + S/N_{new})$$
$$\log_2(1 + S/N_{new}) = 13.288$$
$$1 + S/N_{new} = 2^{13.288} = 10{,}001$$
$$S/N_{new} \approx 10{,}000$$

**Observation:** Doubling capacity requires *squaring* the SNR (from $10^4$ to $10^8$). Converting back: $10\log_{10}(10^8) = 80$ dB. So SNR must increase from **40 dB to 80 dB** — a 40 dB (10,000×) power increase! This demonstrates the **diminishing returns** of power scaling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert — Transmission Rate Problems:**
> 1. **dB vs Linear Confusion:** Forgetting to convert dB to linear scale before applying $\log_2(1+S/N)$ is the **#1 cause of full-mark loss**. Always write: $S/N = 10^{(S/N)_{dB}/10}$.
> 2. **Confusing Nyquist with Shannon:** Nyquist's $2B\log_2(M)$ applies to *noiseless* channels. If SNR is mentioned, **always use Shannon's formula** — do NOT mix them.
> 3. **Units Mismatch:** Bandwidth must be in **Hz** (not kHz) and capacity in **bps** (not kbps) when using SI in formulas. Convert at the start.
> 4. **Missing $\log_2(M)$:** Many students write $R_b = 2B$ for QAM systems, forgetting the bits-per-symbol factor.
> 5. **Showing No Justification:** For derivation questions, merely stating the formula gets **at most 1 mark**. Always show the logical steps (assumptions → formulation → simplification).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Transmission Rate** is the rate at which information flows over a channel, quantified by **bit rate** ($R_b$) or **symbol rate** ($R_s$).
- **Bit Rate vs Baud Rate:** $R_b = R_s \cdot \log_2(M)$. They are equal **only** for binary signaling.
- **Nyquist Theorem (Noiseless):** Maximum bit rate = $2B \log_2(M)$ bps. Defines the pulse-rate limit of $2B$ symbols/s.
- **Shannon-Hartley Theorem (Noisy):** Absolute capacity = $B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ bps. **Cannot be exceeded** by any coding/modulation.
- **Bandwidth Efficiency** ($\eta = R_b / B$): measured in **bits/s/Hz**; higher-order M-ary schemes improve this.
- **SNR Conversion Rule:** $S/N_{\text{linear}} = 10^{(S/N)_{dB}/10}$. Always do this *before* applying Shannon.
- **Design Trade-off:** ↑ Bandwidth → ↑ Capacity (linearly). ↑ Power → ↑ Capacity (logarithmically). ↑ Modulation order $M$ → ↑ Bit rate (but ↓ noise immunity).
- **KTU 2024 Weightage Tip:** Module 2 typically carries 14–20% of ESE marks; transmission rate questions are a **guaranteed 7- or 14-mark question** in every paper.
- **Historical Benchmark:** Standard voice channel (3 kHz, 30 dB SNR) yields ≈ 30 kbps — this is the classical modem limit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OSI Layering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT416 - PRINCIPLES OF COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** OSI Layering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) Model** is a conceptual reference framework, standardized by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO) under ISO/IEC 7498-1**, that standardizes the functions of a telecommunication or computing system into abstraction layers. It describes how data moves from an application on one networked device through physical media to an application on another networked device.

In the context of **Principles of Communication Techniques (PERAT416)**, the OSI model represents the **layered architecture of data communication systems**, where each layer performs a specific set of well-defined functions and communicates only with its adjacent layers through standardized interfaces known as **Service Access Points (SAPs)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The OSI model consists of **7 layers**, grouped into **2 functional categories**:
> 1. **Lower Subnet Layers (Layers 1–3):** Concerned with the physical transport of data.
> 2. **Upper Host Layers (Layers 4–7):** Concerned with the logical/software-side processing of data.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Letter" System

Imagine you (the **sender**) want to send a hand-written letter to your friend in another city (the **receiver**). Before mailing, you:

| Step | Real-World Action | OSI Layer Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Write the letter content | Application + Presentation + Session |
| 2 | Translate the letter into a common language (e.g., English) | Presentation |
| 3 | Enclose it in an envelope with the destination address | Network + Data Link |
| 4 | Drop the envelope at the post office (physical transport) | Physical |
| 5 | Postal service tracks delivery confirmation | Transport |

This **"envelope-within-envelope"** structure is exactly how the OSI model packages data: each layer adds its own **header (and sometimes trailer)** called a **Protocol Data Unit (PDU)**, which is stripped off at the corresponding layer on the receiving end.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology:**
> - **PDU (Protocol Data Unit):** The formatted unit of data at any given layer.
> - **SDU (Service Data Unit):** The data payload passed from the layer above.
> - **ICI (Interface Control Information):** The header/trailer added by the current layer.

## 1.3 Standardized Communication Metrics

The OSI model is bound by the following universal constants/metrics:

- **Total Layers = 7** (fixed by ISO/IEC 7498-1).
- **Header/PDU encapsulation occurs at every layer except the Physical Layer** (which transmits raw **bits**).
- **Data unit size is governed by the Maximum Transmission Unit (MTU)** of the underlying Data Link Layer, typically **1500 bytes** for Ethernet.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 7 Layers of the OSI Model — Exhaustive Functional Breakdown

The OSI architecture is structured as a **vertical stack of abstractions**. Each layer $L_i$ (where $i \in \{1,2,3,4,5,6,7\}$) provides a defined set of services to the layer directly above it, denoted $L_{i+1}$, while requesting services from the layer directly below it, denoted $L_{i-1}$.

### Layer 7 — Application Layer

- **Function:** Closest to the end user. Provides network services directly to user applications (e.g., HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SNMP).
- **PDU Name:** **Data** (or Message).
- **KTU High-Yield Point:** The Application layer is **NOT** the application itself (like Chrome or Outlook). It is the **protocol** that the application uses (like HTTP for browsers).

### Layer 6 — Presentation Layer

- **Function:** Handles data translation, encryption/decryption, and compression. Ensures data is in a usable format. Standard formats include **ASCII, EBCDIC, JPEG, MPEG, TLS/SSL**.
- **PDU Name:** **Data**.

### Layer 5 — Session Layer

- **Function:** Manages and controls connections (sessions) between two communicating hosts. Responsible for **establishment, maintenance, and termination of sessions**. Handles authentication and reconnection after interruption.
- **PDU Name:** **Data**.
- **Examples:** **NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP**.

### Layer 4 — Transport Layer

- **Function:** Provides **end-to-end** communication, error recovery, and flow control. Ensures complete data transfer.
- **Two Core Protocols:**
  - **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol):** Connection-oriented, reliable, uses 3-way handshake.
  - **UDP (User Datagram Protocol):** Connectionless, unreliable, low overhead.
- **PDU Name:** **Segment** (TCP) or **Datagram** (UDP).

### Layer 3 — Network Layer

- **Function:** Provides **logical addressing** (IP addresses) and **path determination (routing)** between networks.
- **PDU Name:** **Packet**.
- **Key Devices:** **Routers, Layer 3 Switches**.

### Layer 2 — Data Link Layer

- **Function:** Provides **node-to-node** data transfer on the same network segment. Handles **MAC addressing, error detection (not correction)**, and frame synchronization.
- **Sub-layers:**
  - **LLC (Logical Link Control) — IEEE 802.2**
  - **MAC (Media Access Control) — IEEE 802.3 (Ethernet), 802.11 (Wi-Fi)**
- **PDU Name:** **Frame**.
- **Key Devices:** **Switches, Bridges, NICs**.

### Layer 1 — Physical Layer

- **Function:** Transmits raw **bitstream** over a physical medium (copper, fiber, wireless). Defines **electrical, mechanical, and procedural** aspects.
- **PDU Name:** **Bits**.
- **Key Devices/Components:** **Hubs, Repeaters, Cables, Connectors, Network Interface Cards (physical portion)**.
- **KTU High-Yield Standards:** **EIA/TIA-232, RJ45, 1000BASE-T, V.35**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The OSI model is descriptive rather than mathematical, but its encapsulated data size and timing are governed by physical-layer relations that frequently appear in KTU exam problems.

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Data Efficiency} \; \eta &= \frac{L_{\text{payload}}}{L_{\text{payload}} + L_{\text{header}} + L_{\text{trailer}}} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
\text{Propagation Delay} \; T_p &= \frac{d}{v} \quad \text{where } d = \text{distance}, \; v = \text{propagation speed} \\[6pt]
\text{Transmission Delay} \; T_t &= \frac{L}{R} \quad \text{where } L = \text{packet length (bits)}, \; R = \text{bandwidth (bps)} \\[6pt]
\text{End-to-End Delay} \; T_{\text{end}} &= N \cdot T_p + (N-1) \cdot T_t + T_{\text{proc}}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Rule:**
> The "encapsulation efficiency" formula is the **most tested** quantitative question. If header size is $H$ bytes and payload is $P$ bytes, efficiency is:
> $$\eta = \frac{P}{P + H} \times 100\%$$

| OSI Layer | PDU Name | Address Type | Key Protocol Examples | KTU-Mandated Mnemonic |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 7. Application | Data / Message | None (uses port no.) | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS | **A**ll |
| 6. Presentation | Data | None | SSL/TLS, JPEG, MPEG | **P**eople |
| 5. Session | Data | None | NetBIOS, RPC | **S**eem |
| 4. Transport | Segment / Datagram | Port Number (16-bit) | TCP, UDP | **T**o |
| 3. Network | Packet | Logical (IP) | IP, ICMP, OSPF, BGP | **N**eed |
| 2. Data Link | Frame | Physical (MAC, 48-bit) | Ethernet, PPP, Wi-Fi | **D**ata |
| 1. Physical | Bits | None | USB, Bluetooth, 1000BASE-T | **P**rocessing |

**Mnemonic (Bottom-Up):** **"Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"**
**Mnemonic (Top-Down):** **"All People Seem To Need Data Processing"**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The OSI model's true engineering value lies in its **interoperability and modularity**:
- A software developer writing an HTTP application **does not need to know** how the physical bits travel over fiber-optic cable.
- A network engineer designing a fiber backbone **does not need to know** the contents of the web page being served.

This **separation of concerns** is what makes the modern Internet possible. The **TCP/IP model** used today is a 4-layer practical simplification of the 7-layer OSI reference model, but the OSI model remains the **canonical teaching tool** in all KTU curricula.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Encapsulation Efficiency at Each Layer

Consider a user message $M$ of size $S_M$ bytes originating at the Application layer. As it descends the OSI stack, each layer $L_i$ adds a header $H_i$ (and occasionally a trailer $T_i$). The efficiency at layer $i$ is defined as the ratio of the original payload to the total encapsulated size at that layer.

### Derivation — Step 1: Initial Payload

At the Application Layer (L7), the payload is the raw user message:

$$
S_7 = S_M \quad \text{(bytes)}
$$

*Explanation:* No header is added at the application layer itself; the data is the user's message.

### Derivation — Step 2: Add Session Header

At the Session Layer (L5), a session header $H_5$ is prepended:

$$
S_5 = S_7 + H_5 = S_M + H_5
$$

*Explanation:* The session header contains session IDs, synchronization tokens, etc.

### Derivation — Step 3: Add Presentation Header

$$
S_6 = S_5 + H_6 = S_M + H_5 + H_6
$$

*Explanation:* The presentation header may include encryption metadata or format identifiers.

### Derivation — Step 4: Add Transport Header (TCP Segment)

$$
S_4 = S_6 + H_4 = S_M + H_5 + H_6 + H_4
$$

*Explanation:* The transport header contains source/destination **port numbers** (16-bit each), sequence numbers, and flags. For TCP, $H_4 = 20$ bytes (minimum, without options).

### Derivation — Step 5: Add Network Header (IP Packet)

$$
S_3 = S_4 + H_3 = S_M + \sum_{i=3}^{6} H_i
$$

*Explanation:* The IP header is $H_3 = 20$ bytes (IPv4, no options). It contains source/destination **IP addresses** (32-bit each).

### Derivation — Step 6: Add Data Link Header AND Trailer (Frame)

The Data Link layer is the **only layer that also adds a trailer** (for the Frame Check Sequence, FCS):

$$
S_2 = S_3 + H_2 + T_2
$$

For standard Ethernet: $H_2 = 14$ bytes, $T_2 = 4$ bytes (FCS/CRC).

### Derivation — Step 7: Final Transmitted Frame

$$
\boxed{S_1 = S_M + H_5 + H_6 + H_4 + H_3 + H_2 + T_2}
$$

### Derivation — Step 8: Encapsulation Efficiency

The efficiency at the Physical Layer (the actual transmitted frame) is:

$$
\eta = \frac{S_M}{S_M + H_5 + H_6 + H_4 + H_3 + H_2 + T_2} \times 100\%
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given:
- $S_M = 1460$ bytes (typical TCP payload)
- $H_4 = 20$, $H_3 = 20$, $H_2 = 14$, $T_2 = 4$
- $H_5 = H_6 = 0$ (often negligible for simple HTTP)

Compute the on-wire efficiency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= 1460 + 0 + 0 + 20 + 20 + 14 + 4 \\
S_1 &= 1518 \; \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{1460}{1518} \times 100\% \\
\eta &= 0.96179 \times 100\% \\
\eta &\approx 96.18\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This $1518$-byte value is the **Maximum Ethernet Frame Size**, which directly corresponds to the standard **MTU of 1500 bytes** (since $1518 - 14 - 4 = 1500$). This is a **classic KTU numerical problem**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Service Provided vs. Service Used

| Layer $i$ | Service Provided to $L_{i+1}$ | Service Used from $L_{i-1}$ |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 7. Application | Access to network services | — |
| 6. Presentation | Data representation, encryption | End-to-end connection |
| 5. Session | Dialog control, synchronization | Reliable or unreliable transport |
| 4. Transport | End-to-end segments, flow control | Routing & addressing |
| 3. Network | Logical addressing & best-effort delivery | Reliable bit transmission over link |
| 2. Data Link | Reliable framing on a single link | Raw bit transmission |
| 1. Physical | Bit stream over physical medium | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 7-Layer OSI Reference Model (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete OSI 7-layer architecture**, showing how data flows vertically through the stack on a sending host, traverses the physical medium, and is reassembled on a receiving host.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph sendingHost["SENDING HOST"]
        direction TB
        nodeApp7["LAYER 7: APPLICATION LAYER (HTTP, FTP, DNS)"]
        nodePres6["LAYER 6: PRESENTATION LAYER (SSL, JPEG, MPEG)"]
        nodeSess5["LAYER 5: SESSION LAYER (NetBIOS, RPC)"]
        nodeTrans4["LAYER 4: TRANSPORT LAYER (TCP, UDP)"]
        nodeNet3["LAYER 3: NETWORK LAYER (IP, ICMP)"]
        nodeDl2["LAYER 2: DATA LINK LAYER (Ethernet, MAC)"]
        nodePhy1["LAYER 1: PHYSICAL LAYER (Bits, Cables)"]
    end

    subgraph medium["TRANSMISSION MEDIUM"]
        nodeMediumX["Physical Channel: Copper / Fiber / Wireless"]
    end

    subgraph receivingHost["RECEIVING HOST"]
        direction TB
        nodePhy1R["LAYER 1: PHYSICAL LAYER (Bits)"]
        nodeDl2R["LAYER 2: DATA LINK LAYER (Ethernet)"]
        nodeNet3R["LAYER 3: NETWORK LAYER (IP)"]
        nodeTrans4R["LAYER 4: TRANSPORT LAYER (TCP)"]
        nodeSess5R["LAYER 5: SESSION LAYER (RPC)"]
        nodePres6R["LAYER 6: PRESENTATION LAYER (SSL)"]
        nodeApp7R["LAYER 7: APPLICATION LAYER (HTTP)"]
    end

    nodeApp7 --> nodePres6
    nodePres6 --> nodeSess5
    nodeSess5 --> nodeTrans4
    nodeTrans4 --> nodeNet3
    nodeNet3 --> nodeDl2
    nodeDl2 --> nodePhy1
    nodePhy1 --> nodeMediumX
    nodeMediumX --> nodePhy1R
    nodePhy1R --> nodeDl2R
    nodeDl2R --> nodeNet3R
    nodeNet3R --> nodeTrans4R
    nodeTrans4R --> nodeSess5R
    nodeSess5R --> nodePres6R
    nodePres6R --> nodeApp7R
```

## 4.2 Data Encapsulation Flow (PDU Transformation)

The following diagram illustrates how the **Protocol Data Unit (PDU) transforms** as it descends from the Application Layer to the Physical Layer.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["APPLICATION LAYER: DATA (e.g., GET /index.html)"]
    nodeB["PRESENTATION LAYER: DATA (Encrypted, Formatted)"]
    nodeC["SESSION LAYER: DATA (Session ID added)"]
    nodeD["TRANSPORT LAYER: SEGMENT (TCP Header + Data)"]
    nodeE["NETWORK LAYER: PACKET (IP Header + Segment)"]
    nodeF["DATA LINK LAYER: FRAME (MAC Header + Packet + FCS Trailer)"]
    nodeG["PHYSICAL LAYER: BITS (Encoded Electrical/Optical Pulses)"]

    nodeA -->|H6| nodeB
    nodeB -->|H5| nodeC
    nodeC -->|H4| nodeD
    nodeD -->|H3| nodeE
    nodeE -->|H2 + T2| nodeF
    nodeF -->|Encoding| nodeG
```

## 4.3 Layer Interaction with Peer-to-Peer Protocol (Virtual Communication)

A critical KTU concept is that **each layer on the sending host communicates logically with its peer on the receiving host**, while physically communicating only with its adjacent layer.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph sendStack["SENDING HOST"]
        L7S["App"]
        L6S["Pres"]
        L5S["Sess"]
        L4S["Trans"]
        L3S["Net"]
        L2S["DLL"]
        L1S["Phys"]
    end

    subgraph recvStack["RECEIVING HOST"]
        L7R["App"]
        L6R["Pres"]
        L5R["Sess"]
        L4R["Trans"]
        L3R["Net"]
        L2R["DLL"]
        L1R["Phys"]
    end

    L7S -. "Peer-to-Peer Virtual Link" .-> L7R
    L6S -. "Peer-to-Peer Virtual Link" .-> L6R
    L5S -. "Peer-to-Peer Virtual Link" .-> L5R
    L4S -. "Peer-to-Peer Virtual Link" .-> L4R
    L3S -. "Peer-to-Peer Virtual Link" .-> L3R
    L2S -. "Peer-to-Peer Virtual Link" .-> L2R
    L1S -- "Actual Physical Signal" --> L1R

    L7S --- L6S
    L6S --- L5S
    L5S --- L4S
    L4S --- L3S
    L3S --- L2S
    L2S --- L1S
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

**Explain the functions of the Transport Layer in the OSI model. List any two protocols operating at this layer.**

**Model Answer:**

The Transport Layer (Layer 4) is responsible for **end-to-end communication, error recovery, flow control, and segmentation/reassembly** of data between two host systems. It ensures that data is delivered completely and in the correct sequence, acting as the bridge between the upper application-oriented layers and the lower network-oriented layers.

**Two key protocols:**
1. **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol):** Connection-oriented, reliable, ensures ordered delivery via sequence numbers and acknowledgments. Uses a 3-way handshake.
2. **UDP (User Datagram Protocol):** Connectionless, unreliable, low overhead. Used for real-time applications like VoIP and video streaming.

The PDU at this layer is called a **Segment** (TCP) or **Datagram** (UDP). [3 marks]

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]

**Differentiate between the Network Layer and the Data Link Layer in terms of addressing scheme and device used.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Network Layer (L3) | Data Link Layer (L2) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Addressing Scheme** | Logical Addressing (IP Address, 32-bit IPv4 / 128-bit IPv6) | Physical Addressing (MAC Address, 48-bit) |
| **Key Device** | Router | Switch, Bridge |
| **PDU Name** | Packet | Frame |
| **Scope** | Inter-network (end-to-end across networks) | Intra-network (single link) |
| **Function** | Routing, path determination, logical addressing | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection |

[3 marks]

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **OSI 7-layer reference model**. Describe the functions of each layer in detail. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the concepts of **encapsulation, Protocol Data Unit (PDU)**, and **peer-to-peer communication** in the OSI model. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The OSI model is a 7-layer abstract reference model defined by **ISO/IEC 7498-1**. It standardizes how data is transmitted between two open systems. The layers, from top to bottom, are:

1. **Layer 7 — Application Layer:** Provides network services to end-user applications. Protocols: HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS. PDU: Data.
2. **Layer 6 — Presentation Layer:** Handles data translation (ASCII ↔ EBCDIC), encryption/decryption (TLS/SSL), and compression (JPEG, MPEG). PDU: Data.
3. **Layer 5 — Session Layer:** Establishes, manages, and terminates sessions between applications. Examples: NetBIOS, RPC. PDU: Data.
4. **Layer 4 — Transport Layer:** Provides end-to-end communication, segmentation, flow control, and error recovery. Protocols: TCP (reliable) and UDP (unreliable). PDU: Segment/Datagram.
5. **Layer 3 — Network Layer:** Handles logical addressing (IP) and routing of packets across networks. Protocols: IP, ICMP, OSPF. Device: Router. PDU: Packet.
6. **Layer 2 — Data Link Layer:** Provides reliable node-to-node delivery, MAC addressing, and error detection. Sub-layers: LLC and MAC. Protocols: Ethernet, Wi-Fi. Device: Switch. PDU: Frame.
7. **Layer 1 — Physical Layer:** Transmits raw bits as electrical/optical signals over the medium. Defines cables, connectors, voltages. PDU: Bits. Device: Hub/Repeater.

[7 marks — 1 mark per layer + 0 marks spare for diagram and final coherence]

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Encapsulation** is the process by which each layer adds its own **header (and sometimes trailer)** to the data received from the layer above. This creates a new Protocol Data Unit (PDU) for that layer.

**PDU (Protocol Data Unit)** is the formatted unit of data at any given OSI layer. The naming convention is:
- L7/L6/L5: **Data**
- L4: **Segment** (TCP) or **Datagram** (UDP)
- L3: **Packet**
- L2: **Frame**
- L1: **Bits**

**Peer-to-Peer Communication:** Although each layer on the sending host communicates **physically only with its adjacent layer**, **logically** each layer communicates with its **corresponding peer layer** on the remote host. For example, the Transport layer on Host A conceptually exchanges data with the Transport layer on Host B — the actual physical transmission is carried out by the Physical layer below.

[7 marks — 2 marks for encapsulation, 2 marks for PDU table, 3 marks for peer-to-peer with example]

### Question B (Internal Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Compare the **OSI Reference Model** and the **TCP/IP Model** in terms of number of layers, layer names, and key protocols. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A message of **1200 bytes** is to be transmitted. The Transport layer adds a **20-byte header**, the Network layer adds a **20-byte header**, and the Data Link layer adds a **14-byte header** and a **4-byte trailer**. Calculate the **transmission efficiency** at the Physical Layer. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Parameter | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Number of Layers** | 7 | 4 (or 5 in some versions) |
| **Layer Names** | Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical | Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access (Link) |
| **Key Protocols** | HTTP, TCP, IP, Ethernet, SSL (theoretical) | HTTP, FTP, TCP, UDP, IP, Ethernet (actual) |
| **Developed By** | ISO (theoretical reference) | DARPA / IETF (practical implementation) |
| **OSI vs TCP/IP Mapping** | L7+L6+L5 → Application (TCP/IP) | TCP/IP is a practical implementation of OSI principles |

[7 marks — 4 for comparison table, 2 for differences, 1 for conclusion]

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Payload (Message) $S_M = 1200$ bytes
- Transport Header $H_4 = 20$ bytes
- Network Header $H_3 = 20$ bytes
- Data Link Header $H_2 = 14$ bytes
- Data Link Trailer $T_2 = 4$ bytes
- Session & Presentation headers are assumed negligible ($H_5 = H_6 = 0$).

**Step 1: Total Frame Size at Physical Layer**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= S_M + H_4 + H_3 + H_2 + T_2 \\
S_1 &= 1200 + 20 + 20 + 14 + 4 \\
S_1 &= 1258 \; \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Each layer adds its overhead in sequence as the data moves down the stack.

**Step 2: Encapsulation Efficiency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{S_M}{S_1} \times 100\% \\
\eta &= \frac{1200}{1258} \times 100\% \\
\eta &= 0.95389 \times 100\% \\
\eta &\approx 95.39\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**

The **overhead** introduced by all the headers and trailers is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Overhead} &= H_4 + H_3 + H_2 + T_2 = 20 + 20 + 14 + 4 = 58 \; \text{bytes} \\[6pt]
\text{Overhead \%} &= 100\% - 95.39\% = 4.61\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, the transmission efficiency at the Physical Layer is approximately **95.39%**. [7 marks — 2 for setup, 2 for calculation, 2 for final answer, 1 for interpretation]

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Trailer ($T_2$):** Many students omit the 4-byte FCS trailer at the Data Link layer. **Always include both header and trailer for Layer 2.**
> 2. **Wrong PDU Name:** Writing "Data" for L4 or L3 instead of "Segment" or "Packet" loses 0.5–1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Confusing Application Layer with Application Software:** The Application layer is **not** Chrome or WhatsApp; it is the **protocol** (HTTP, SMTP) used by them.
> 4. **Forgetting Peer-to-Peer Logic:** Always emphasize that logical communication is peer-to-peer, while physical communication is layer-to-adjacent-layer.
> 5. **Unit Errors in Efficiency:** Always express efficiency in **%**, not as a fraction (e.g., 0.95 — this is a frequent KTU deduction).

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **7 Layers** in order: **A**pplication, **P**resentation, **S**ession, **T**ransport, **N**etwork, **D**ata **L**ink, **P**hysical. Mnemonic (Top-Down): **"All People Seem To Need Data Processing"**. Mnemonic (Bottom-Up): **"Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"**.
- **PDU names (Top→Bottom):** Data → Data → Data → **Segment/Datagram** → **Packet** → **Frame** → **Bits**.
- **Addressing:** MAC (48-bit, L2) vs. IP (32-bit IPv4, L3) vs. Port (16-bit, L4).
- **Key Devices:** Hub (L1) → Switch (L2) → Router (L3) → Gateway (L4–L7).
- **Encapsulation Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Payload}}{\text{Payload} + \text{All Headers} + \text{Trailer}} \times 100\%$.
- **Standard Ethernet Frame:** Header = 14 bytes, Trailer (FCS) = 4 bytes, Max Frame = 1518 bytes, MTU = 1500 bytes.
- **OSI is the Reference Model; TCP/IP is the Practical Model.** The TCP/IP Internet layer corresponds to OSI's Network layer; the TCP/IP Application layer encompasses OSI's Application, Presentation, and Session layers.
- **Peer-to-Peer Communication:** Logical data exchange occurs between the **same numbered layers** on two hosts, but the **physical signal** travels only between Physical layers.
- **TCP vs. UDP:** TCP = reliable, connection-oriented, used for HTTP/FTP/Email. UDP = unreliable, connectionless, used for VoIP/Video/DNS.
- **Service Access Points (SAPs):** Interface between adjacent layers. Example: The port number is the SAP between Transport and Application layers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TCP/IP Layering, Switching: Circuit Switching and Packet Switching, Data Link Layer: Flow Control (Stop & Wait and Sliding Window)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT416 - PRINCIPLES OF COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** TCP/IP Layering, Switching: Circuit Switching and Packet Switching, Data Link Layer: Flow Control (Stop & Wait and Sliding Window) ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 TCP/IP Layering Model > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** The **TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol)** model is a four-layer (sometimes described as five-layer) conceptual framework that standardizes the communication functions of a telecommunication or computing system into abstraction layers. Each layer serves the layer above it and is served by the layer below it, enabling heterogeneous networks to interoperate over the global Internet. The four canonical layers of the TCP/IP model are: 1. **Application Layer** – End-user services (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, Telnet). 2. **Transport Layer** – Host-to-host data delivery, multiplexing, reliability (TCP, UDP). 3. **Internet Layer** – Logical addressing and best-effort packet routing across networks (IP, ICMP, ARP). 4. **Link (Network Access) Layer** – Physical medium access, framing, error detection on the local link (Ethernet, Wi-Fi, PPP). > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — The Postal System:** > Think of TCP/IP layering like the **international postal service**. > - **Application Layer** = You writing a letter (your message). > - **Transport Layer** = The postal clerk who decides whether to send it tracked (TCP) or regular (UDP). > - **Internet Layer** = The address written on the envelope (IP address) that routes it through multiple countries. > - **Link Layer** = The actual trucks, planes, and roads used to physically carry the envelope. > > Each layer performs **one specific job** and hands off the result to the next — they never "skip" a layer. --- ### 1.2 Switching: Circuit Switching and Packet Switching > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** **Switching** is the technique used in telecommunication networks to establish communication paths between two or more endpoints, deciding how bandwidth is allocated and how data is routed from source to destination. #### 1.2.1 Circuit Switching A dedicated physical communication path (a "circuit") is established between sender and receiver **before** data transfer begins and is held for the entire duration of the session. The three classical phases are: - **Circuit Establishment** - **Data Transfer** - **Circuit Teardown** > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Old Telephone Exchange:** > When you made a landline call in the 1980s, an operator physically connected your wire to the receiver's wire, creating a **dedicated copper path**. Even when you both stayed silent, the line was reserved. That is Circuit Switching. #### 1.2.2 Packet Switching Data is broken into small, independently-routed units called **packets**, each carrying source/destination addresses. Packets may travel **different paths** and arrive **out of order**; the receiver reassembles them. There are two sub-types: - **Datagram Packet Switching** (connectionless, e.g., IP) - **Virtual Circuit Packet Switching** (connection-oriented, e.g., ATM, Frame Relay) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Modern Highway System:** > Sending a 100-page book via packet switching is like tearing out the pages, putting each into its own envelope with the destination address, and letting postal trucks take **different routes** to the destination. The receiver then reassembles the pages. If one truck is delayed, others may still arrive on time. --- ### 1.3 Data Link Layer — Flow Control > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** **Flow Control** is a Data Link Layer mechanism that regulates the rate at which a sender transmits data frames so that a slower receiver is not overwhelmed by an incoming data stream faster than it can process. The two principal KTU-prescribed techniques are: - **Stop-and-Wait ARQ** (simplest, 1-frame window) - **Sliding Window Protocol** (Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Coffee Shop Counter:** > Imagine a fast barista preparing 10 drinks per minute, but a customer can only carry 1 drink at a time. The barista must wait until the customer signals "ready" before serving the next one. **Stop-and-Wait** is exactly that. The **Sliding Window** is like a cafeteria tray system — the barista can keep 3 drinks ready on the counter, and as soon as one is taken, the next is placed (window slides forward). ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 TCP/IP Layer Deep Dive (Why & How) **Layer 4 — Application Layer** - *Why:* Different apps need different services (web ≠ email ≠ file transfer). - *How:* Defines protocols like **HTTP (port 80)**, **HTTPS (port 443)**, **SMTP (port 25)**, **FTP (port 21)**, **DNS (port 53)**. - *PDU:* **Message / Data**. **Layer 3 — Transport Layer** - *Why:* Hosts may run multiple apps simultaneously; we need multiplexing. - *How:* Uses **port numbers (0–65535)** to direct data to the correct application. - **TCP** → Connection-oriented, reliable, 3-way handshake (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK), retransmission, flow & congestion control. - **UDP** → Connectionless, unreliable, low overhead, used for DNS, VoIP, streaming. - *PDU:* **Segment (TCP)** or **Datagram (UDP)**. **Layer 2 — Internet Layer** - *Why:* The network is a "network of networks"; each device needs a unique global identifier. - *How:* **IP addressing** (IPv4 = 32 bits, IPv6 = 128 bits) + **routing** (RIP, OSPF, BGP). - *PDU:* **Packet / Datagram**. **Layer 1 — Link (Network Access) Layer** - *Why:* Bits must be physically encoded on copper, fiber, or radio. - *How:* Defines **framing**, **MAC addressing (48 bits)**, **error detection (CRC)**, and **medium access (CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA)**. - *PDU:* **Frame**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Data Encapsulation Logic:** As data moves DOWN the layers, each layer adds its own header (and sometimes trailer). Moving UP, each layer strips its header and passes the payload up. This is called the **PDU Sandwich**. --- ### 2.2 Circuit vs. Packet Switching — Analytical Comparison | Feature | Circuit Switching | Packet Switching | |---|---|---| | Path Setup | Required before transfer | No prior setup | | Dedicated Channel | Yes (reserved end-to-end) | No (shared statistically) | | Resource Utilization | Poor during silence | Excellent (statistical multiplexing) | | Latency | Constant after setup | Variable (queueing delay) | | Order of Arrival | In-order guaranteed | May be out-of-order | | Reliability | High (fixed path) | Lower (best-effort) | | Best Suited For | Voice (legacy PSTN) | Data, Internet | | Example | Old telephone, ISDN | Ethernet, Internet, LTE | > [!NOTE] > **Performance Insight:** For **bursty data** (web browsing, email), circuit switching wastes 80–90% of reserved bandwidth. Packet switching achieves **statistical multiplexing gain**, often 3–5×. --- ### 2.3 Stop-and-Wait ARQ — Theory **Operational Logic:** - Sender transmits **one frame** of size $L$ bits. - Sender **stops and waits** for an ACK. - Receiver accepts the frame, sends ACK. - If ACK is lost/corrupted, sender's **timer expires** and the frame is **retransmitted**. **Efficiency Limitation:** The sender is **idle** during the round-trip propagation time — a major waste for high-bandwidth or long-distance links. --- ### 2.4 Sliding Window Protocol — Theory **Conceptual Logic:** - A "window" of $W$ frames can be sent before an ACK is required. - Receiver maintains a window of size $W_r$. - As ACKs arrive, the window **slides forward**. - Two variants: **Go-Back-N ARQ** (retransmit all from error) and **Selective Repeat ARQ** (retransmit only the erroneous one). **Why Sliding Window Wins:** It allows the sender to **pipeline** multiple frames, keeping the channel busy — a massive efficiency boost on high-bandwidth-delay product links. --- ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All variables, units, and boundary conditions are tabulated below for instant KTU exam recall.** | # | Parameter | Symbol / Formula | Unit | Notes / Boundary Condition | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Frame Length (data) | $L$ | bits | Length of one information frame | | 2 | Bandwidth | $B$ | bits/sec (bps) | Channel bit rate | | 3 | One-way Propagation Delay | $t_p = d / v$ | seconds | $d$ = distance (m), $v$ = propagation speed (~2×10⁸ m/s in fiber) | | 4 | Transmission Time | $t_t = L / B$ | seconds | Time to push one frame onto wire | | 5 | Round-Trip Time | $RTT = 2 t_p$ | seconds | Assuming equal uplink/downlink | | 6 | Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = B \times t_p$ | bits | Capacity of the "pipe" | | 7 | Stop-and-Wait Efficiency | $\eta_{S\&W} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$ | dimensionless (ratio) | $a = t_p / t_t$ | | 8 | Sliding Window Efficiency | $\eta_{SW} = \dfrac{W}{1 + 2a}$ | dimensionless (ratio) | For $W \geq 1 + 2a$, $\eta = 1$ | | 9 | Channel Utilization | $U = \eta$ | % (×100) | Fraction of time channel carries useful data | | 10 | Window Size for 100% Util. | $W \geq 2a + 1$ | integer | Always round up | | 11 | Go-Back-N Max Seq. Number | $2^k - 1$ | integer | $k$ = number of sequence bits | | 12 | Selective Repeat Max Seq. | $2^{k-1}$ | integer | $k$ = number of sequence bits | | 13 | Throughput | $S = \eta \times B$ | bps | Effective data rate | | 14 | Max Files/sec in circuit switching | $f_{max} = 1 / (s + L/B)$ | files/sec | $s$ = setup time, $L/B$ = transmit time | **Critical Substitution Rule:** $\;a = \dfrac{t_p}{t_t} = \dfrac{B \cdot d}{L \cdot v}\;$ — memorize this single combined expression as it appears in nearly every KTU numerical. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Stop-and-Wait Protocol Efficiency > [!NOTE] > **Goal:** Derive a closed-form expression for the **channel utilization (efficiency)** of the Stop-and-Wait ARQ protocol over a noiseless channel. **Step 1 — Identify the timeline components.** - $t_t$ = time taken to transmit ONE frame onto the channel. - $t_p$ = one-way propagation delay (sender → receiver). - ACK transmission time is considered negligible (or subsumed in $t_p$). - $t_A$ = ACK transmission time (negligible for thin ACKs). - Total cycle time for one successful frame: $T_{cycle} = t_t + t_p + t_A + t_p$. **Step 2 — Simplify for thin ACKs.** Since ACKs are tiny, $t_A \to 0$, and the cycle becomes: $$T_{cycle} = t_t + 2 t_p$$ **Step 3 — Define utilization.** Efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of *useful transmission time* to *total cycle time*: $$\eta_{S\&W} = \frac{\text{Useful Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} = \frac{t_t}{t_t + 2 t_p}$$ **Step 4 — Introduce the dimensionless parameter $a$.** Define $a = t_p / t_t$, so $t_p = a \cdot t_t$. Substitute: $$\eta_{S\&W} = \frac{t_t}{t_t + 2 (a t_t)} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}$$ **Step 5 — Express $a$ in measurable quantities.** Because $t_t = L/B$ and $t_p = d/v$, we have: $$a = \frac{d \cdot B}{v \cdot L}$$ **Step 6 — Final consolidated form.** $$\boxed{\eta_{S\&W} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{2 \cdot d \cdot B}{v \cdot L}}}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** - If $a \to 0$ (short distance, high $L$): $\eta \to 1$ (perfect utilization). - If $a \to \infty$ (long distance, small $L$): $\eta \to 0$ (catastrophic waste). --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Sliding Window Protocol Efficiency > [!NOTE] > **Goal:** Derive efficiency when the sender can transmit $W$ frames before requiring an ACK. **Step 1 — Transmission phase.** Sender continuously transmits $W$ frames back-to-back: $$T_{send} = W \cdot t_t$$ **Step 2 — Wait for ACK of first frame.** After the last frame is sent, the ACK for the *first* frame arrives roughly $2 t_p$ later (only one propagation gap is needed because pipelining hides the rest). **Step 3 — Total cycle.** $$T_{cycle} = W \cdot t_t + 2 t_p$$ **Step 4 — Efficiency ratio.** $$\eta_{SW} = \frac{W \cdot t_t}{W \cdot t_t + 2 t_p} = \frac{W}{W + 2a}$$ where $a = t_p / t_t$. **Step 5 — 100% Utilization Condition.** For $\eta_{SW} = 1$, we need $2a / W \to 0$, i.e., $W \gg 2a$. The minimum integer window for full utilization: $$W_{min} = \lfloor 2a \rfloor + 1$$ --- ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Stop-and-Wait) **Problem:** Consider a satellite link at $B = 1$ Mbps, distance $d = 36{,}000$ km, frame length $L = 1000$ bits, propagation speed $v = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. Find (a) $a$, (b) efficiency. **Step A — Compute $t_p$:** $$t_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{36 \times 10^6}{3 \times 10^8} = 0.12 \text{ sec}$$ **Step B — Compute $t_t$:** $$t_t = \frac{L}{B} = \frac{1000}{10^6} = 10^{-3} \text{ sec}$$ **Step C — Compute $a$:** $$a = \frac{t_p}{t_t} = \frac{0.12}{10^{-3}} = 120$$ **Step D — Compute efficiency:** $$\eta_{S\&W} = \frac{1}{1 + 2 \times 120} = \frac{1}{241} \approx 0.415\%$$ **Step E — Required window for 100% utilization:** $$W_{min} = 2 \times 120 + 1 = 241$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** A satellite link with Stop-and-Wait is **99.6% wasted bandwidth**. A 241-frame window would saturate the channel. This is why protocols like TCP use **large dynamic windows** for satellite and long-haul fiber. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Scenario | $a$ Value | Stop-and-Wait $\eta$ | Sliding Window $\eta$ ($W=7$) | Verdict | |---|---|---|---|---| | LAN, large frame | 0.01 | 98% | 99.9% | Both excellent | | MAN | 1.0 | 33% | 87.5% | Sliding Window wins | | WAN fiber | 10 | 4.8% | 41% | Stop-and-Wait fails | | Geostationary satellite | 120 | 0.42% | 5.5% | Sliding Window mandatory | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 TCP/IP Four-Layer Model (with PDU & Example Protocols) ```mermaid flowchart TB APP["LAYER 4 APPLICATION LAYER
PDU: MESSAGE
PROTOCOLS: HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS
FUNCTION: USER SERVICES"] TRANS["LAYER 3 TRANSPORT LAYER
PDU: SEGMENT OR DATAGRAM
PROTOCOLS: TCP, UDP
FUNCTION: HOST TO HOST PORTS"] NET["LAYER 2 INTERNET LAYER
PDU: PACKET
PROTOCOLS: IP, ICMP, ARP
FUNCTION: GLOBAL ROUTING"] LINK["LAYER 1 LINK LAYER
PDU: FRAME
PROTOCOLS: ETHERNET, WIFI, PPP
FUNCTION: PHYSICAL ACCESS"] APP -->|Data| TRANS TRANS -->|Segment| NET NET -->|Packet| LINK LINK -->|Bits over medium| PHY[("PHYSICAL MEDIUM COPPER FIBER WIRELESS")] PHY --> LINK LINK -->|Packet| NET NET -->|Segment| TRANS TRANS -->|Data| APP ``` --- ### 4.2 Circuit Switching vs. Packet Switching — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CS["CIRCUIT SWITCHING"] CS1["SETUP PHASE DIAL ESTABLISH DEDICATED PATH"] --> CS2["DATA TRANSFER
FIXED CHANNEL BANDWIDTH"] CS2 --> CS3["TEARDOWN PHASE RELEASE PATH"] end subgraph PS["PACKET SWITCHING"] PS1["NO SETUP
PACKETS CREATED AT SOURCE"] --> PS2["INDEPENDENT ROUTING
EACH PACKET TAKES OWN PATH"] PS2 --> PS3["REASSEMBLY AT DESTINATION
MAY ARRIVE OUT OF ORDER"] end S1["TELEPHONE NETWORK PSTN ISDN"] -.->|example| CS S2["INTERNET ETHERNET 4G 5G"] -.->|example| PS ``` --- ### 4.3 Stop-and-Wait ARQ — Protocol Timeline ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant S as SENDER participant R as RECEIVER Note over S: TIMER STARTS S->>R: FRAME 0 Note over R: RECEIVE AND CHECK R->>S: ACK 0 Note over S: ACK RECEIVED WINDOW SLIDE S->>R: FRAME 1 R->>S: ACK 1 Note over S: ON TIMER EXPIRY RETRANSMIT S->>R: FRAME 2 Note over R: ACK LOST Note over S: TIMEOUT RESEND S->>R: FRAME 2 RETRY R->>S: ACK 2 ``` --- ### 4.4 Sliding Window Protocol — Window Sliding Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FW["FRAME WINDOW OF SIZE 4"] F1["FRAME 0 SENT"] --> F2["FRAME 1 SENT"] --> F3["FRAME 2 SENT"] --> F4["FRAME 3 SENT AWAIT ACK"] end subgraph ACKS["ACK STATE"] A1["ACK 0 RECEIVED"] --> A2["ACK 1 RECEIVED"] --> A3["ACK 2 PENDING"] --> A4["ACK 3 PENDING"] end FW -->|transmits| MEDIUM[("CHANNEL PIPE")] MEDIUM -->|arrives| ACKS SLIDE["WINDOW SLIDES FORWARD NEW FRAME 4 ENTERS SLOT 0"] -.->|on each ACK| FW SLIDE -.->|updates| ACKS ``` --- ### 4.5 Stop-and-Wait vs. Sliding Window — Channel Utilization Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SAW["STOP AND WAIT CHANNEL TIMELINE"] T1["FRAME OUT t_t"]:::busy I1["IDLE WAIT FOR ACK 2 t_p"]:::idle T2["NEXT FRAME t_t"]:::busy I2["IDLE 2 t_p"]:::idle T1 --> I1 --> T2 --> I2 end subgraph SW["SLIDING WINDOW CHANNEL TIMELINE W=4"] X1["F0 F1 F2 F3 PIPELINED 4 t_t"]:::busy X2["CONTINUE F4 F5 F6 F7"]:::busy X1 --> X2 end classDef busy fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef idle fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** In Stop-and-Wait, the **red idle gaps** are wasted bandwidth. In Sliding Window, the channel is **blue and continuously busy** — frames are pipelined to fill the idle gaps. This is the visual essence of why TCP and modern protocols use sliding windows. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]` > **List the four layers of the TCP/IP model and state one function of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The four layers are: 1. **Application Layer (1 mark):** Provides network services to end-user applications; e.g., HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS. 2. **Transport Layer (1 mark):** Provides host-to-host, process-to-process delivery using port numbers; e.g., TCP (reliable) and UDP (unreliable). 3. **Internet Layer (1 mark):** Performs logical addressing (IP) and routing of packets across interconnected networks. *(Layer 4 in 5-layer model = Link layer – accepts for 0.5 mark if mentioned as bonus.)* > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]` > **Differentiate between circuit switching and packet switching in any three aspects.** **Model Answer (3 marks = 1 mark per aspect):** 1. **Path Setup:** Circuit switching requires a dedicated path to be established before data transfer; packet switching does not require any prior setup. 2. **Resource Utilization:** Circuit switching wastes bandwidth during silence; packet switching uses statistical multiplexing, achieving higher utilization. 3. **Order of Delivery:** Circuit switching guarantees in-order delivery; packet switching may deliver packets out of order, requiring reassembly. *(Acceptable alternatives: latency, reliability, suitability, example systems.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted Model]` > > **(a)** Explain the TCP/IP protocol suite in detail with a neat diagram of the four-layer model. Describe the function of each layer with one example protocol. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** With a neat timing diagram, explain the operation of the **Stop-and-Wait ARQ** protocol. Derive its efficiency. A channel has $B = 4$ kbps and one-way propagation delay $t_p = 20$ ms. Frame size $L = 800$ bits.
