Find the efficiency. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) TCP/IP Model (7 marks):** - **1 mark** for diagram (must show 4 layers with arrows). - **1 mark** per layer explanation: 4 × 1 = 4 marks. - **1 mark** for example protocol. - **1 mark** for encapsulation/decapsulation note. **Detailed Answer:** **Layer 1 — Link Layer:** Handles physical transmission of bits; framing, MAC addressing, error detection (CRC). *Example:* Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11). *PDU:* Frame. **Layer 2 — Internet Layer:** Logical addressing and routing. *Example:* IPv4, IPv6, ICMP. *PDU:* Packet. **Layer 3 — Transport Layer:** End-to-end delivery, multiplexing via port numbers. *Example:* TCP (connection-oriented, reliable) and UDP (connectionless, fast). *PDU:* Segment / Datagram. **Layer 4 — Application Layer:** Network services to user processes. *Example:* HTTP (web), SMTP (mail), DNS (name resolution). *PDU:* Message. *Encapsulation:* Each layer prepends its header as data passes downward. **(b) Stop-and-Wait ARQ (7 marks):** - **2 marks** for operation explanation with timing diagram. - **2 marks** for efficiency derivation. - **3 marks** for numerical computation. **Derivation:** $$\eta_{S\&W} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}, \quad a = \frac{t_p}{t_t} = \frac{t_p \cdot B}{L}$$ **Numerical:** $$a = \frac{20 \times 10^{-3} \times 4000}{800} = \frac{80}{800} = 0.1$$ $$\eta_{S\&W} = \frac{1}{1 + 2(0.1)} = \frac{1}{1.2} = 0.8333 = 83.33\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — STOP-AND-WAIT NUMERICALS:** > Students often confuse **$a$** with **$2a$** and write efficiency as $\frac{1}{1+a}$ — this costs **2 of 7 marks**. > Another common error: substituting $t_p$ in **ms** without converting to **seconds** before multiplying by $B$ in **bps**. KTU examiners mark this as a **unit-mismatch error (-1 mark)**. > Always end with the **percentage** value (e.g., 83.33%) — the **final boxed answer must include units**. --- > **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted Model]` > > **(a)** Compare **circuit switching** and **packet switching** with suitable diagrams. State two advantages and two disadvantages of each. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Explain the **Sliding Window Protocol** with Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat variants. For a satellite link with $B = 1$ Mbps, $d = 36{,}000$ km, $L = 1000$ bits, $v = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, find the **minimum window size** for 100% utilization. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Circuit vs Packet Switching (7 marks):** - **1.5 marks** for circuit switching diagram + explanation. - **1.5 marks** for packet switching diagram + explanation. - **2 marks** for tabulated comparison (4 aspects). - **2 marks** for advantages/disadvantages (1+1 each). **Diagram (Description):** - **Circuit:** Sender → dedicated switches (3 nodes in a row) → Receiver; physical line reserved for the session. - **Packet:** Sender → router1 (splits into 3 packets, takes 2 different paths) → router2 → Receiver (reassembles). **Comparison:** | Aspect | Circuit | Packet | |---|---|---| | Setup | Needed | Not needed | | Delay | Constant | Variable | | Resource use | Wasted | Efficient | | Reliability | High | Lower (best effort) | **Advantages/Disadvantages (any 2 each, 0.5 mark each):** - *Circuit pro:* Guaranteed bandwidth, predictable latency. *Con:* Wastes bandwidth during silence, slow setup. - *Packet pro:* Efficient, flexible routing, no setup delay. *Con:* Variable delay, possible packet loss, out-of-order arrival. **(b) Sliding Window Protocol (7 marks):** - **2 marks** for general sliding window concept. - **2 marks** for Go-Back-N explanation. - **2 marks** for Selective Repeat explanation. - **1 mark** for numerical answer. **Numerical Solution:** **Step 1:** $t_p = d/v = 36 \times 10^6 / 3 \times 10^8 = 0.12$ s **Step 2:** $t_t = L/B = 1000 / 10^6 = 10^{-3}$ s **Step 3:** $a = t_p / t_t = 0.12 / 0^{-3} = 120$ **Step 4:** Minimum window for 100% utilization: $$W_{min} = 2a + 1 = 2(120) + 1 = \mathbf{241}$$ **Step 5:** At $W = 241$, $\eta_{SW} = W/(W+2a) = 241/(241+240) = 241/481 = 0.501$... wait, correction — when $W = 2a+1$, denominator becomes $(2a+1) + 2a = 4a+1$, so $\eta = (2a+1)/(4a+1)$. For **strict 100%** ($W \to \infty$): $W_{min} = 2a + 1$ is the integer that just *enables* the sender to stay ahead of the ACKs; efficiency asymptotically approaches 1. **Correct framing:** "Minimum window size to keep the channel **busy without idle gaps** = $W_{min} = \lceil 2a + 1 \rceil = \lceil 241 \rceil = 241$ frames." > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — SLIDING WINDOW NUMERICALS:** > Most students **forget to add 1** in the formula and write $W = 2a = 240$. This is wrong because you need *strictly more* than $2a$ frames outstanding to keep the pipeline full. The "+1" accounts for the **boundary** frame. KTU explicitly tests this with $W = 2a$ vs $W = 2a+1$ — losing **1.5 marks**. > Also, **do not confuse Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat sequence number limits** — this is a favorite 2-mark concept question. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3** **TCP/IP Layering:** - ☐ 4 layers: Application → Transport → Internet → Link. - ☐ PDU names: Message → Segment/Datagram → Packet → Frame. - ☐ Key protocols: HTTP, TCP/UDP, IP, Ethernet. - ☐ Encapsulation adds headers top-down; decapsulation strips them bottom-up. - ☐ TCP = reliable, connection-oriented; UDP = unreliable, connectionless. **Switching Techniques:** - ☐ **Circuit Switching** = dedicated path, 3 phases (setup, transfer, teardown), best for voice. - ☐ **Packet Switching** = no path, statistical multiplexing, best for data. - ☐ Virtual Circuit = hybrid (logical path, packet-based). - ☐ Datagram = connectionless packet switching. **Flow Control — Stop-and-Wait:** - ☐ 1 frame at a time, sender waits for ACK. - ☐ Efficiency: $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$, where $a = t_p / t_t = Bd/(vL)$. - ☐ Sequence numbers: 0 and 1 alternate. - ☐ Timer-based retransmission on loss. **Flow Control — Sliding Window:** - ☐ Window of $W$ frames can be sent before ACK. - ☐ Efficiency: $\eta = \dfrac{W}{W + 2a}$; maxes at 1 when $W \gg 2a$. - ☐ Minimum window for full utilization: $W_{min} = 2a + 1$ (always round UP). - ☐ **Go-Back-N:** max seq no = $2^k - 1$; retransmits all from error. - ☐ **Selective Repeat:** max seq no = $2^{k-1}$; retransmits only the error. - ☐ Pipelining hides propagation delay, boosting throughput $S = \eta \cdot B$. **Formula Anchor (memorize verbatim):** $$\boxed{\eta = \frac{W}{W + 2a}, \quad a = \frac{B \cdot d}{v \cdot L}, \quad W_{min} = 2a+1}$$ **Engineering Heuristics for KTU Numericals:** - Always convert $d$ to meters, $B$ to bps, $L$ to bits. - Always convert final $\eta$ to % (multiply by 100) — KTU expects it. - Satellite link always has $a \gg 1$ — Sliding Window is mandatory. - LAN has $a \ll 1$ — even Stop-and-Wait is acceptable.

topics.title: Media Access Control: Aloha
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT416 - PRINCIPLES OF COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Media Access Control: Aloha

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**ALOHA** is a **random-access Media Access Control (MAC) protocol** developed at the **University of Hawaii in 1971** by **Norman Abramson** as part of the ALOHAnet project — historically regarded as the **first wireless packet data network** and the conceptual ancestor of modern Ethernet and Wi-Fi MAC sublayers.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, ALOHA belongs to the **Multiple Access (MA) / Contention-Based** family of channel-access techniques, in which multiple uncoordinated users share a common broadcast medium without the aid of a centralized arbiter. There are two canonical variants:

1. **Pure ALOHA (Unslotted ALOHA):** Stations transmit frames *as soon as* they are generated, with no timing reference.
2. **Slotted ALOHA (Roberts, 1975):** Time is quantized into discrete **slots**, and a station may begin transmission *only at the start of a slot*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme Module 3 expects students to (i) state the ALOHA throughput expressions, (ii) derive the vulnerable period, and (iii) compute maximum channel efficiency. Both **Pure ALOHA** and **Slotted ALOHA** are mandatory.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cocktail Party

Imagine a large room where many people wish to speak simultaneously:

- **Pure ALOHA** corresponds to a **completely unmoderated party**: anyone may shout the moment they have something to say. If two people shout *at slightly overlapping times*, both messages become unintelligible (a **collision**) and both speakers must wait a random amount of time before retrying.
- **Slotted ALOHA** corresponds to a **party led by a metronome**: a referee beats a drum every $T$ seconds, and speakers are only allowed to start their sentence *exactly on the beat*. The overlap window is therefore halved, and the chance of two people colliding is dramatically reduced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Idea:** ALOHA trades *deterministic guarantees* for *simplicity*. There is **no carrier sensing, no token, and no scheduler** — the protocol relies purely on **statistical multiplexing** and **random retransmission back-off**.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Industry Standards)

- **Throughput (S):** Average number of *successfully received* frames per **frame transmission time** $T$. Dimensionless, $0 \leq S \leq 1$.
- **Load / Offered Traffic (G):** Average number of *transmission attempts* (new + retransmitted) per frame time $T$. Dimensionless, $G \geq 0$.
- **Channel Utilization (Efficiency, $\eta$):** Maximum attainable $S$, expressed as a percentage: $\eta_{\max} = S_{\max} \times 100\%$.
- **Vulnerable Period ($V_p$):** Time interval during which another transmission will cause a collision. For Pure ALOHA $V_p = 2T$, for Slotted ALOHA $V_p = T$.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** "**G** is **G**oing-in traffic (all attempts); **S** is **S**uccessful traffic." $S \leq G$ always.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pure ALOHA — Operational Logic

The operational sequence of a Pure ALOHA station is captured below as a structured step-by-step logic.

- **Step 1 — Frame Generation:** A station (say, $S_1$) generates a data frame at some random instant $t_0$.
- **Step 2 — Immediate Transmission:** The station broadcasts the frame *immediately* onto the shared channel, *without listening first*.
- **Step 3 — Collision Risk:** If any other station transmits *any portion* of a frame such that the overlap exceeds the receiver's ability to recover, a **collision** occurs. In Pure ALOHA, the vulnerable period is symmetric about $t_0$ and equals $2T$ (one $T$ before, one $T$ after).
- **Step 4 — Acknowledgment Watch:** The sender waits for an **ACK** within a propagation-time timeout (typically $2 \times t_{prop}$). No ACK ⇒ collision assumed.
- **Step 5 — Random Back-off:** The station waits a *random* number of slots (BEB — Binary Exponential Back-off variant) and retransmits.
- **Step 6 — Repeat until Success:** The cycle repeats until ACK is received.

**Why does the vulnerable period equal $2T$?**
Any other transmission starting in $[t_0 - T,\; t_0 + T]$ will overlap the current frame, since the current frame itself occupies $[t_0,\; t_0 + T]$.

### 2.2 Slotted ALOHA — Operational Logic

- **Step 1 — Slot Synchronization:** A central clock (or beacon) partitions time into slots of duration $T$.
- **Step 2 — Slot-Aligned Start:** Stations may begin transmission *only at a slot boundary*.
- **Step 3 — Reduced Vulnerable Period:** Because a new frame can only *start* at a slot edge, an overlap is possible only if *two or more* stations start in the **same** slot. The vulnerable period collapses from $2T$ to $T$.
- **Step 4 — Collision Resolution:** Same as Pure ALOHA: no ACK ⇒ random back-off and retry.

### 2.3 Throughput Derivation Intuition (Poisson Model)

Assume an **infinite population** of users generating frames according to a **Poisson process** with mean rate $\lambda$ attempts per frame time $T$. Then the **load** is:

$$G = \lambda T$$

The probability that exactly **$k$** attempts occur in any interval of length $xT$ is:

$$P(k) = \frac{(\lambda x T)^k \, e^{-\lambda x T}}{k!} = \frac{(xG)^k \, e^{-xG}}{k!}$$

A transmission is **successful** if **no other** station attempts in the vulnerable period. The probability of zero competing attempts over $x$ frame times is $P(0) = e^{-xG}$.

The **conditional throughput** (probability of success) is therefore $P_{\text{succ}} = e^{-xG}$, and the unconditional throughput is:

$$S = G \cdot P_{\text{succ}}$$

Setting $x = 2$ for Pure ALOHA and $x = 1$ for Slotted ALOHA yields the canonical KTU formulas.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Pure ALOHA Expression | Slotted ALOHA Expression | Unit / Range |
|---|----------|----------------------|--------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Vulnerable Period $V_p$ | $2T$ | $T$ | seconds |
| 2 | Load $G$ | $\lambda T$ | $\lambda T$ | dimensionless, $G \geq 0$ |
| 3 | Success Probability $P_{\text{succ}}$ | $e^{-2G}$ | $e^{-G}$ | $0 \leq P \leq 1$ |
| 4 | Throughput $S$ | $S = G \, e^{-2G}$ | $S = G \, e^{-G}$ | frames per frame-time |
| 5 | Optimal Load $G^*$ | $G^* = 1/2$ | $G^* = 1$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Maximum Throughput $S_{\max}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2e} \approx 0.1839$ | $\dfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.3679$ | frames per frame-time |
| 7 | Maximum Efficiency $\eta_{\max}$ | $\approx 18.4\%$ | $\approx 36.8\%$ | percentage |
| 8 | $e$ (Euler's constant) | $e \approx 2.71828$ | $e \approx 2.71828$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Average Retries $E[r]$ | $\dfrac{G}{G e^{-2G}} - 1 = e^{2G} - 1$ | $e^{G} - 1$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical numerical values to memorize for KTU 2024 ESE:** $1/e \approx 0.368$ and $1/(2e) \approx 0.184$. These appear in nearly every textbook and past paper.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Satellite Communication:** Slotted ALOHA is used in **VSAT (Very Small Aperture Terminal)** uplink channels and early **INMARSAT** reservation systems.
- **RFID Tag-to-Reader Links:** The **EPCglobal Class-1 Gen-2** RFID protocol employs a *framed-slotted ALOHA* variant for anti-collision.
- **IoT / LoRaWAN:** Random-access channels in **LoRaWAN** are ALOHA-like.
- **Cable TV Upstream (DOCSIS):** Early DOCSIS 1.0 used a reservation variant of Slotted ALOHA.
- **Pure Research Vehicle:** ALOHA is the reference baseline against which **CSMA**, **CSMA/CD**, and **TDMA** are benchmarked in every networking textbook.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Slotted ALOHA exactly 2× Pure ALOHA in efficiency?** Because the vulnerable period is exactly halved, $e^{-G}$ vs. $e^{-2G}$. At the optimum, the ratio is $\left(\tfrac{1}{e}\right)/\left(\tfrac{1}{2e}\right) = 2$. This is a classic KTU "compare and justify" question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Pure ALOHA Throughput $S = G e^{-2G}$

We assume a **Poisson arrival process** with mean rate $\lambda$ attempts per second. The frame transmission time is $T$ seconds.

**Step 1 — Define the load metric.**
The dimensionless offered load per frame-time is:

$$G = \lambda T$$

This is the *expected number of transmission attempts* (new + retransmissions) per unit frame time.

**Step 2 — Probability of exactly $k$ attempts in time $t$.**
For a Poisson process, in any interval of duration $t$ the count of attempts $N(t)$ follows:

$$P\!\left[N(t) = k\right] = \frac{(\lambda t)^k \, e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of $G$ and frame-times.**
Let $t = xT$ (i.e., $x$ frame-times). Then $\lambda t = \lambda x T = xG$:

$$P[N(xT) = k] = \frac{(xG)^k \, e^{-xG}}{k!}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the vulnerable period.**
In Pure ALOHA, a frame of length $T$ starting at $t_0$ is destroyed if *any* other frame starts in the open interval $(t_0 - T,\; t_0 + T)$. Thus the "danger interval" has length $2T$, so $x = 2$.

**Step 5 — Probability of no collision in the vulnerable period.**
Setting $k = 0$ and $x = 2$:

$$P_{\text{succ}} = P[N(2T) = 0] = \frac{(2G)^0 \, e^{-2G}}{0!} = e^{-2G}$$

**Step 6 — Throughput = Load × Success Probability.**
Only $G e^{-2G}$ out of every $G$ attempted frames survive:

$$S = G \cdot P_{\text{succ}} = G \, e^{-2G}$$

This is the **Pure ALOHA throughput law**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation II — Slotted ALOHA Throughput $S = G e^{-G}$

The derivation is structurally identical, with the critical change in Step 4.

**Step 1 — Same load:** $G = \lambda T$, where $T$ is now the slot duration.

**Step 2 — Same Poisson count:** $P[N(xT) = k] = \dfrac{(xG)^k e^{-xG}}{k!}$.

**Step 3 — Same as before.**

**Step 4 — Reduced vulnerable period.**
In Slotted ALOHA, transmissions are aligned to slot boundaries, so a collision occurs *only* if another station transmits *in the same slot*. The dangerous interval is exactly one slot, $T$, hence $x = 1$.

**Step 5 — Probability of no collision.**

$$P_{\text{succ}} = P[N(T) = 0] = e^{-G}$$

**Step 6 — Throughput.**

$$S = G \cdot e^{-G}$$

This is the **Slotted ALOHA throughput law**. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation III — Maximum Throughput (Optimization)

**For Pure ALOHA:** Differentiate $S(G) = G e^{-2G}$ and set the derivative to zero.

$$\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-2G} + G \cdot (-2) e^{-2G} = e^{-2G}\left(1 - 2G\right) = 0$$

Since $e^{-2G} > 0$ for all finite $G$, the optimum satisfies:

$$1 - 2G^* = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; G^* = \frac{1}{2}$$

Substitute back:

$$S_{\max}^{\text{Pure}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.1839$$

**For Slotted ALOHA:** Differentiate $S(G) = G e^{-G}$.

$$\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; G^* = 1$$

Substitute back:

$$S_{\max}^{\text{Slotted}} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.3679$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Second-derivative test (concavity):** $\dfrac{d^2 S}{dG^2}\big|_{G=G^*} < 0$, confirming that $G^*$ is a **maximum**, not a minimum.

### 3.4 Numerical Problem Pattern — KTU 2024 Style

**Typical problem statement:** *"In a Pure ALOHA system, the offered load is $G = 0.5$. Find (a) the probability of successful transmission, (b) the throughput, and (c) the probability that exactly 3 frames are generated in a vulnerable period."*

**Solution Skeleton:**

- (a) $P_{\text{succ}} = e^{-2 \times 0.5} = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$
- (b) $S = G \cdot P_{\text{succ}} = 0.5 \times 0.368 \approx 0.184$
- (c) $P[N(2T)=3] = \dfrac{(2 \times 0.5)^3 \, e^{-1}}{3!} = \dfrac{1 \times 0.368}{6} \approx 0.0613$

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Performance Dimension | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA | Verdict |
|------------------------|------------|---------------|---------|
| Global clock required? | **No** | **Yes** | Pure is simpler |
| Vulnerable period | $2T$ | $T$ | Slotted is safer |
| Maximum throughput | $\approx 0.184$ | $\approx 0.368$ | Slotted is 2× better |
| Implementation complexity | Very low | Moderate (sync) | Pure wins on cost |
| Delay at low load | Lower | Higher (must wait for slot) | Pure wins |
| Practical applications | RFID, sensor burst traffic | VSAT, reservation systems | Domain-specific |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pure ALOHA — Vulnerable Period Timeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Frame of Station 1 starts at t0]:::focus
    B[Vulnerable window 2T centered on t0]:::danger
    C[Frame of Station 2 starts at t0 minus T]:::collide
    D[Frame of Station 3 starts at t0 plus T]:::collide
    E[Both collide with reference frame]:::result
    F[No collision if no other frame in window]:::safe

    A --> B
    B --> C
    B --> D
    C --> E
    D --> E
    B --> F

    classDef focus fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#000
    classDef danger fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#000
    classDef collide fill:#FFA500,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#FFF
    classDef safe fill:#2ECC71,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** The amber blocks are colliding frames, the green outcome is the *desired* (collision-free) path. Any frame starting inside the red $2T$ window destroys the reference frame.

### 4.2 Slotted ALOHA — Time-Slot Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Central Clock Beacon]:::clock
    B[Slot 1 of length T]:::slot
    C[Slot 2 of length T]:::slot
    D[Slot 3 of length T]:::slot
    E[Slot 4 of length T]:::slot
    F[Station A transmits in Slot 2]:::tx
    G[Station B transmits in Slot 2]:::collide
    H[Station C transmits in Slot 4]:::safe
    I[Collision detected in Slot 2]:::result
    J[ACK returned for Slot 4]:::ack

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E
    C --> F
    C --> G
    F --> I
    G --> I
    D --> H
    H --> J

    classDef clock fill:#3498DB,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#FFF
    classDef slot fill:#BDC3C7,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#000
    classDef tx fill:#F39C12,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#000
    classDef collide fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#FFF
    classDef result fill:#C0392B,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#FFF
    classDef safe fill:#27AE60,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#FFF
    classDef ack fill:#16A085,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#FFF
```

### 4.3 ALOHA Station — State Machine (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Idle State - waiting for data]:::idle
    B[Frame ready in buffer]:::ready
    C[Transmit on channel]:::tx
    D[Listen for ACK within timeout]:::wait
    E[ACK received - DONE]:::success
    F[No ACK - assume collision]:::fail
    G[Pick random backoff k in 0 to 2 sup m minus 1]:::backoff
    H[Wait k slots]:::delay
    I[Retransmit]:::retry

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    D --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> D

    classDef idle fill:#95A5A6,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#FFF
    classDef ready fill:#3498DB,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#FFF
    classDef tx fill:#F39C12,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#000
    classDef wait fill:#9B59B6,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#FFF
    classDef success fill:#27AE60,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#FFF
    classDef fail fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#FFF
    classDef backoff fill:#E67E22,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#000
    classDef delay fill:#7F8C8D,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#FFF
    classDef retry fill:#D35400,stroke:#1E1E1E,color:#FFF
```

### 4.4 Throughput vs Load — Qualitative Shape

| Load $G$ | Pure ALOHA $S = G e^{-2G}$ | Slotted ALOHA $S = G e^{-G}$ |
|-----------|---------------------------|------------------------------|
| $0.1$ | $0.1 \cdot e^{-0.2} \approx 0.0819$ | $0.1 \cdot e^{-0.1} \approx 0.0905$ |
| $0.5$ | $0.5 \cdot e^{-1} \approx 0.1839$ | $0.5 \cdot e^{-0.5} \approx 0.3033$ |
| $1.0$ | $1.0 \cdot e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$ | $1.0 \cdot e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$ |
| $2.0$ | $2.0 \cdot e^{-4} \approx 0.0366$ | $2.0 \cdot e^{-2} \approx 0.2707$ |
| $5.0$ | $5.0 \cdot e^{-10} \approx 0.00023$ | $5.0 \cdot e^{-5} \approx 0.0337$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Pattern recognition for KTU:** For $G > 1$, both curves collapse toward zero — this is the **congestion collapse** phenomenon. ALOHA systems must operate in $G < 1$ regime for stable behavior.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term *vulnerable period* in the context of the Pure ALOHA protocol. Why is it different for Slotted ALOHA?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The *vulnerable period* is the time interval during which, if any other station initiates a transmission, a collision will occur with the current frame. For **Pure ALOHA**, since stations can begin transmission at any arbitrary instant, the vulnerable period equals **$2T$** (one $T$ before and one $T$ after the frame start). For **Slotted ALOHA**, transmissions are restricted to slot boundaries, so a collision can occur only when two stations pick the *same* slot — the vulnerable period collapses to **$T$**. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** State the maximum throughput expressions for Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA, and identify the corresponding optimal load values.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Protocol | Throughput $S$ | Optimal Load $G^*$ | $S_{\max}$ |
|----------|---------------|---------------------|-----------|
| Pure ALOHA | $G e^{-2G}$ | $1/2$ | $1/(2e) \approx 0.184$ |
| Slotted ALOHA | $G e^{-G}$ | $1$ | $1/e \approx 0.368$ |

The optimal load is found by setting $dS/dG = 0$. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a) Derive the throughput equation of Pure ALOHA. State clearly the assumptions made.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Infinite population of users, each generating frames according to an independent Poisson process.
2. All frames have the same length $T$ (constant frame time).
3. The channel introduces no errors other than those caused by collisions.
4. No capture effect — colliding frames are always destroyed.
5. Acknowledgment is instantaneous and error-free.

**Derivation (5 marks):**
Let $\lambda$ be the mean arrival rate of *transmission attempts* (new + retries). The offered load is:

$$G = \lambda T$$

For a Poisson process, the probability of $k$ arrivals in time $t$ is:

$$P[N(t) = k] = \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

Substituting $t = 2T$ (the vulnerable period) and $k = 0$:

$$P_{\text{succ}} = \frac{(\lambda \cdot 2T)^0 \, e^{-2\lambda T}}{0!} = e^{-2G}$$

Throughput = (rate of attempts) × (probability of success):

$$\boxed{S = G \, e^{-2G}}$$

Setting $dS/dG = 0$ gives $G^* = 1/2$ and $S_{\max} = 1/(2e) \approx 0.184$.

---

**(b) For a Slotted ALOHA channel, the offered load is $G = 1.2$. Compute (i) the throughput, (ii) the probability of collision per slot, and (iii) the average number of retransmissions per successful frame.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Throughput (2 marks):**

$$S = G \, e^{-G} = 1.2 \times e^{-1.2} = 1.2 \times 0.3012 \approx 0.3614$$

**(ii) Probability of collision per slot (2 marks):**
The probability of a successful (single) transmission in a slot is $e^{-G} = e^{-1.2} \approx 0.3012$. Therefore, the probability of *collision or multiple* transmissions is:

$$P_{\text{coll}} = 1 - e^{-G} = 1 - 0.3012 = 0.6988$$

**(iii) Average retransmissions per successful frame (3 marks):**
If $S$ frames succeed per slot out of $G$ total attempts, the success ratio is $S/G = e^{-G}$. The expected number of *attempts* per success is $1/e^{-G} = e^{G}$. Hence the average *retransmissions* is:

$$E[r] = e^{G} - 1 = e^{1.2} - 1 = 3.3201 - 1 = 2.3201$$

So on average, **2.32 retransmissions** occur for every successful delivery.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a) Explain with neat sketches the operation of Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA. Compare their maximum channel utilization.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Pure ALOHA Operation (3 marks):**
- Stations transmit frames *as soon as* they are ready, with no coordination.
- If two frames overlap in time, a **collision** destroys both.
- Vulnerable period $= 2T$.
- Sender waits for ACK; on timeout, backs off randomly and retransmits.

**Slotted ALOHA Operation (2 marks):**
- Time is divided into slots of duration $T$, synchronized by a clock.
- A station may begin transmission *only* at the start of a slot.
- Vulnerable period $= T$ (reduced by half).
- Collision occurs if two or more stations pick the *same* slot.

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Metric | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA |
|--------|-----------|---------------|
| $S_{\max}$ | $1/(2e) \approx 18.4\%$ | $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ |
| Improvement factor | 1× (baseline) | **2×** |
| Sync required | No | Yes |

Slotted ALOHA is **exactly twice as efficient** as Pure ALOHA at the optimal load.

---

**(b) In a Pure ALOHA network, frames of length $L = 1000$ bits are transmitted at $R = 1$ Mbps. The offered load is $G = 0.4$. Compute (i) frame transmission time $T$, (ii) channel throughput in bits/sec, and (iii) the average number of attempts required for a successful transmission.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Frame transmission time (1 mark):**

$$T = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bits/sec}} = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) Channel throughput in bits/sec (3 marks):**
Throughput in frames per frame-time:

$$S = G \, e^{-2G} = 0.4 \times e^{-0.8} = 0.4 \times 0.4493 = 0.1797$$

Throughput in bits per second:

$$S_{\text{bits}} = \frac{S \cdot L}{T} = 0.1797 \times \frac{1000}{10^{-3}} = 0.1797 \times 10^{6} \approx 179{,}733 \text{ bps}$$

**(iii) Average attempts per success (3 marks):**
The probability of success per attempt is $e^{-2G} = e^{-0.8} \approx 0.4493$. The expected number of attempts per success is:

$$E[\text{attempts}] = \frac{1}{e^{-2G}} = e^{2G} = e^{0.8} \approx 2.2255$$

So on average, **2.23 attempts** are required for a successful transmission.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $G$ and $S$:** Students frequently write $S = G e^{-G}$ for Pure ALOHA (forgetting the factor of 2). The correct exponent is $-2G$ for Pure and $-G$ for Slotted.
> 2. **Wrong vulnerable period:** Many students state $V_p = T$ for Pure ALOHA, losing 2 marks outright. Always derive it from first principles.
> 3. **Forgetting units in throughput:** $S$ is dimensionless (frames per frame-time). Converting to bps requires $S \times L / T$.
> 4. **Skipping assumptions in derivation:** KTU expects the *Poisson process assumption* and *infinite user population* stated explicitly for full marks.
> 5. **Off-by-one in slot indexing:** When solving Slotted ALOHA problems, the slot index starts at the *start* of transmission — getting this wrong flips $e^{-G}$ to $e^{+G}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ALOHA was the first wireless packet network**, developed at the **University of Hawaii in 1971** by Norman Abramson.
- **Pure ALOHA** = transmit-anytime, no clock; **Slotted ALOHA** = clock-synchronized, slot-aligned transmission.
- **Vulnerable period** is **$2T$ for Pure ALOHA** and **$T$ for Slotted ALOHA** — this is the *root cause* of the 2× efficiency difference.
- **Throughput formulas:**
  - Pure: $S = G e^{-2G}$, max $= 1/(2e) \approx 0.184$ at $G = 0.5$.
  - Slotted: $S = G e^{-G}$, max $= 1/e \approx 0.368$ at $G = 1$.
- **Model assumption:** Poisson arrivals, infinite population, equal-length frames, no capture effect.
- **Average attempts per success** $= e^{2G}$ (Pure) or $e^{G}$ (Slotted); **average retries** $= e^{2G} - 1$ and $e^{G} - 1$ respectively.
- **Retransmission strategy:** Random back-off (often BEB — Binary Exponential Backoff) after a collision; ACK-based success detection.
- **Congestion collapse:** $S \to 0$ as $G \to \infty$ — ALOHA is *inherently unstable* under uncontrolled overload, motivating CSMA.
- **Real-world deployments:** VSAT satellite uplinks, RFID (EPC Gen-2 framed-slotted ALOHA), LoRaWAN random access, DOCSIS 1.0 upstream.
- **Comparison anchor:** Slotted ALOHA doubles Pure ALOHA's efficiency at the cost of clock synchronization — a classic engineering trade-off.
- **Higher-layer link:** ALOHA is the conceptual ancestor of Ethernet's CSMA/CD and Wi-Fi's CSMA/CA; the modern MAC layer is essentially "ALOHA + sensing."
- **Key constants to memorize:** $e \approx 2.718$, $1/e \approx 0.368$, $1/(2e) \approx 0.184$.
- **Differentiator from CSMA:** ALOHA has **no carrier sensing**; CSMA listens before transmitting, dramatically reducing collisions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT416 - PRINCIPLES OF COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Slotted Aloha

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Slotted Aloha (S-ALOHA)** is a discrete-time version of the Pure Aloha random access protocol used in shared-medium communication networks. In this scheme, the shared channel time is divided into discrete, equal-length **time slots**, and each station is permitted to begin transmission *only at the start of a slot boundary*. If a collision occurs, the colliding stations retransmit the packet in a subsequent slot with a random back-off delay, governed by a retransmission probability.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Slotted Aloha is categorized under **Multiple Access Protocols** in data communication and computer networks. It is explicitly listed in Module 3 of the PERAT416 syllabus as a refinement of Pure Aloha, often contrasted to highlight the **2x throughput improvement** achieved by synchronization.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a classroom where students are allowed to ask questions to the teacher, but the teacher says: *"You may only speak when the clock's second hand hits 12."* This rule converts a continuous chaos into a **disciplined, slot-bound system**. Even if two students start speaking at the same "12 mark", the entire question is still useful — only an overlap from the previous question (that bled into this slot) or into the next question causes interference.

In **Pure Aloha**, if a student starts speaking at *any* random moment and another starts slightly later, their voices collide. In **Slotted Aloha**, the rule forces everyone to begin on the boundary. This single rule **doubles the system's capacity** (maximum throughput) — one of the most elegant results in random access theory.

### 1.3 Physical/Standard Metrics

- **Maximum Channel Throughput (Smax):** **0.368** or equivalently **1/e** (≈ 36.8% of the channel capacity)
- **Throughput Formula:** S = G · e^(–G), where G is the mean offered load per slot
- **Vulnerable Time:** Reduced from **2T** (Pure Aloha) to **T** (Slotted Aloha)
- **Optimal Offered Load:** G = **1** (one attempt per slot on average at peak efficiency)
- **Collision Window:** Exactly one slot duration (T)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to derive *why* the throughput doubles when going from Pure Aloha to Slotted Aloha. The key is the vulnerable period being halved due to slot synchronization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

1. **Time Discretization:** The channel is segmented into fixed-length time slots, each of duration equal to one packet transmission time **T**.
2. **Slot-Aligned Transmission:** Stations are *synchronized* (usually via a beacon or master clock) and may only initiate packet transmission at the **boundary** of a slot.
3. **Collision Condition:** A collision occurs if *two or more* stations transmit during the *same* slot. Note: since transmissions are slot-aligned, a packet from slot *k* can never overlap a packet from slot *k–1* or *k+1* (apart from partial overlap within slot k itself).
4. **Vulnerable Period Reduction:** The window during which another transmission can cause a collision is reduced from **2T → T** because we no longer need to worry about "left-edge" overlaps.
5. **Acknowledgement (ACK):** A successful transmission is positively acknowledged by the receiver; absence of ACK indicates collision.
6. **Retransmission Strategy:** Colliding stations wait a random number of slots (binary exponential or uniform backoff), then retransmit with probability **pr** in each subsequent slot.
7. **Steady-State Behavior:** As offered load G increases, throughput S rises, peaks at G=1, then collapses due to congestion collapse.

### 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind Slotted Aloha

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental insight of Slotted Aloha is that **the probability of no collision** in a given slot depends only on what happens *in that slot*, not in the adjacent ones. This **memoryless property** drastically simplifies the collision model and doubles efficiency.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Boundary / Optimal Value** | **Unit** |
|--------------|-----------|-------------|------------------------------|----------|
| Offered Load (mean attempts/slot) | G | G = λ · T (Poisson arrivals) | 0 ≤ G < ∞ | dimensionless |
| Throughput | S | S = G · e^(–G) | Smax = 1/e ≈ 0.368 at G = 1 | packets/slot |
| Probability of Zero Collisions | P₀ | e^(–G) | Maximum at G=0 (P₀=1) | dimensionless |
| Vulnerable Time | Tv | T (one slot) | Fixed | seconds |
| Channel Utilization (Peak) | Umax | 1/e | ≈ 36.8% | percent |
| Retransmission Probability | pr | 0 < pr < 1 | Tunable for stability | dimensionless |
| Mean Number of Transmissions | n | G = mean attempts | — | dimensionless |
| Pure Aloha Throughput | SAloha | G · e^(–2G) | 0.184 at G = 0.5 | packets/frame-time |
| Improvement Factor (S-ALOHA / P-ALOHA) | k | 2 | Constant | ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** For all LaTeX rendering inside KTU answer scripts, throughput is typically reported as a fraction of the *channel capacity*. Therefore, S = 0.368 means **36.8%** of the slots carry a successful packet.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Satellite Communication Systems** (e.g., VSAT networks) where a hub synchronizes all terminals.
- **RFID Systems (ISO 18000-63):** Tag-to-reader uplink uses Slotted Aloha variants (e.g., Q-algorithm).
- **IoT and LPWAN networks** (LoRaWAN class A/B, Sigfox) for low-duty-cycle uplink scheduling.
- **Wi-Fi legacy fallback** and certain **cellular ALOHA reservation schemes**.
- **Pure research** in random access mathematical modeling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Throughput of Slotted Aloha

#### Assumption Setup
- The channel has N stations generating new packets according to a Poisson process with mean rate λ per slot.
- Each station retransmits collided packets with probability pr per slot.
- Combined offered load (new + retransmitted) per slot: **G = λT** where T = slot duration.
- Probability of *exactly k* transmission attempts in a slot (Poisson distribution):

$$
P(k) = \frac{G^{k} \cdot e^{-G}}{k!}
$$

#### Step 1: Probability of a Successful Slot
A slot is successful if and only if *exactly one* station attempts transmission in that slot. Setting k = 1:

$$
P(\text{success}) = P(k=1) = \frac{G^{1} \cdot e^{-G}}{1!} = G \cdot e^{-G}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the probability mass function of the Poisson distribution evaluated at k=1, where G is the mean.

#### Step 2: Definition of Throughput
Throughput **S** is defined as the average number of successful packets transmitted per slot. Since each slot carries at most one success:

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-G}
$$

*Explanation:* Multiply the mean offered load (G) by the probability that a given attempt is "the only one" (e^–G). This is the *successful packet rate*.

#### Step 3: Maximization of Throughput
To find the peak throughput, take the derivative of S with respect to G and set it to zero:

$$
\frac{dS}{dG} = \frac{d}{dG}\left(G \cdot e^{-G}\right) = e^{-G} - G \cdot e^{-G} = e^{-G}(1 - G)
$$

Apply the first-order optimality condition:

$$
e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0
$$

Since e^(–G) > 0 for all real G, we require:

$$
1 - G = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad G = 1
$$

*Explanation:* Setting the derivative to zero identifies the stationary point. The exponential factor never vanishes, so the bracketed term must equal zero.

#### Step 4: Second-Order Confirmation (Maximum)
Second derivative test:

$$
\frac{d^{2}S}{dG^{2}} = \frac{d}{dG}\left[e^{-G}(1 - G)\right] = -e^{-G}(1 - G) - e^{-G} = e^{-G}(G - 2)
$$

At G = 1:

$$
\left.\frac{d^{2}S}{dG^{2}}\right|_{G=1} = e^{-1}(1 - 2) = -\frac{1}{e} < 0
$$

*Explanation:* A negative second derivative confirms the stationary point is a **maximum**, not a minimum.

#### Step 5: Compute Maximum Throughput
Substitute G = 1 back into the throughput equation:

$$
S_{\max} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368
$$

*Explanation:* At the optimal offered load, the channel carries successful packets 36.8% of the time.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison with Pure Aloha

| **Parameter** | **Pure Aloha** | **Slotted Aloha** | **Improvement** |
|---------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Vulnerable Period | 2T | T | 2× reduction |
| Throughput Formula | G · e^(–2G) | G · e^(–G) | — |
| Peak Throughput | 1/(2e) ≈ 0.184 | 1/e ≈ 0.368 | **2× higher** |
| Optimal G | 0.5 | 1 | — |
| Synchronization Required | No | **Yes** | Cost: clock sync |
| Implementation Complexity | Low | Moderate | Trade-off |

### 3.3 Derivation: The "Doubles the Capacity" Result

Starting from Pure Aloha's throughput:

$$
S_{\text{Pure}} = G_{\text{Pure}} \cdot e^{-2 G_{\text{Pure}}}
$$

At its peak (G = 0.5):

$$
S_{\text{Pure, max}} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184
$$

For Slotted Aloha, the slot-aligned constraint means a packet can only collide with packets from the *same* slot. If packet duration is T, a collision requires that two packets start within T of each other — and in slotted mode, the *worst case* shift between two packets is still 0 (they start at the same slot edge), so the effective vulnerable window is just one T.

This halves the exponent:

$$
S_{\text{Slotted}} = G_{\text{Slotted}} \cdot e^{- G_{\text{Slotted}}}
$$

At G = 1:

$$
S_{\text{Slotted, max}} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{S_{\text{Slotted, max}}}{S_{\text{Pure, max}}} = \frac{1/e}{1/(2e)} = 2
$$

*Explanation:* Synchronization exactly doubles the achievable throughput — a 100% capacity gain for a single architectural constraint.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Timing Diagram — Slotted Aloha Channel Access

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T0 as Slot Boundary 0
    participant S1 as Station A
    participant S2 as Station B
    participant S3 as Station C
    participant S4 as Station D
    participant T1 as Slot Boundary 1
    participant T2 as Slot Boundary 2
    participant T3 as Slot Boundary 3

    Note over T0,T1: SLOT 0 (Duration T)
    S1->>T0: Transmits at t = 0
    Note right of S1: Packet occupies entire slot

    Note over T1,T2: SLOT 1
    S2->>T1: Transmits at slot 1 boundary
    S3->>T1: Also transmits! COLLISION
    Note over S2,S3: Both packets corrupted

    Note over T2,T3: SLOT 2
    S4->>T2: Transmits at slot 2 boundary
    Note right of S4: Success (only one user)

    Note over T3: SLOT 3 onward
    S2->>T3: Retransmits (after backoff)
    Note right of S2: Random delay applied
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice that transmissions in Slot 0, 1, 2, 3 all *start exactly* at the slot boundary — this is the defining property of Slotted Aloha. In contrast, Pure Aloha would allow transmissions to start at any instant t, creating 2T vulnerable windows.

### 4.2 Throughput vs. Load (S vs. G) Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Offered Load G increases from 0] --> B[G = 0: S = 0]
    B --> C[G = 0.5: S ≈ 0.303]
    C --> D[G = 1: S = 1/e ≈ 0.368 PEAK]
    D --> E[G = 2: S ≈ 0.271]
    E --> F[G = 3: S ≈ 0.149]
    F --> G[G → ∞: S → 0 Congestion Collapse]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px
    style G fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#8b0000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The curve rises, peaks sharply at G=1, and then falls off because too many stations retransmit, causing frequent collisions. This is the classic **congestion collapse** behavior of uncontrolled ALOHA systems.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N[Network of N Stations] --> GEN[Packet Generator]
    GEN --> SYNC[Slot Synchronizer]
    SYNC --> TX[Transmitter with Backoff Logic]
    TX --> CH[Shared Channel Time-Slotted]
    CH --> RX[Receiver]
    RX --> ACK[ACK Feedback]
    ACK --> RETX{ACK Received?}
    RETX -->|Yes| DONE[Packet Delivered]
    RETX -->|No / Collision| BACKOFF[Random Backoff Timer]
    BACKOFF --> TX

    style SYNC fill:#fff4b3
    style CH fill:#ffcccb
    style RETX fill:#c8e6c9
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Notes:** The **Slot Synchronizer** is the key block that distinguishes Slotted Aloha from Pure Aloha. The **Backoff Logic** prevents synchronized re-collisions and is essential for stability.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define Slotted Aloha. How does it differ from Pure Aloha?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Slotted Aloha is a random access protocol where the channel time is divided into discrete slots, and stations may only begin transmission at slot boundaries.

**Differences from Pure Aloha:**
- **Synchronization:** Slotted Aloha requires slot-level synchronization; Pure Aloha does not.
- **Vulnerable Time:** Slotted Aloha has Tv = T; Pure Aloha has Tv = 2T.
- **Throughput:** Smax = 0.368 (Slotted) vs. 0.184 (Pure) — **2× improvement**.
- **Implementation:** Slotted Aloha needs a central clock or beacon.

---

#### **Question 2: State the throughput expression and maximum throughput of Slotted Aloha.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-G}
$$

The maximum throughput is:

$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368
$$

This occurs when the offered load **G = 1**, i.e., on average one transmission attempt per slot.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the throughput expression S = G · e^(–G) for Slotted Aloha. Identify the value of G at which throughput is maximized. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Slotted Aloha network has 5 stations, each generating a new packet in a slot with probability p = 0.1. Compute (i) the mean offered load G, and (ii) the probability that a given slot is successful. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Let N stations be considered, each independently generating a new packet per slot with probability p, and retransmitting collided packets with probability pr.

The mean number of transmission attempts per slot:

$$
G = N \cdot p_{\text{total}}
$$

The probability that *exactly k* stations transmit in a slot follows a Poisson distribution (for large N):

$$
P(k) = \frac{G^{k} e^{-G}}{k!}
$$

A slot is successful when *exactly one* station transmits:

$$
P_{\text{success}} = P(k=1) = G e^{-G}
$$

Since throughput = (mean attempts) × (probability of success per attempt):

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-G}
$$

For maximum throughput:

$$
\frac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad G = 1
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{S_{\max} = e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368}
$$

**Part (b):**

**(i) Mean offered load:**

With 5 stations, each with p = 0.1, and assuming all packets are *new* (no retransmissions initially):

$$
G = N \cdot p = 5 \times 0.1 = 0.5
$$

**(ii) Probability of success:**

$$
P_{\text{success}} = G \cdot e^{-G} = 0.5 \times e^{-0.5}
$$

$$
P_{\text{success}} = 0.5 \times 0.6065 = 0.3033
$$

**Final Answer:** P_success ≈ **0.303** (about 30.3% of slots carry a successful transmission).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare Pure Aloha and Slotted Aloha in terms of vulnerable time, throughput formula, maximum throughput, and synchronization requirement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a Slotted Aloha system, find the probability of a successful transmission when the offered load is G = 0.8. Comment on whether the system is operating below or above the optimum. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

| **Parameter** | **Pure Aloha** | **Slotted Aloha** |
|---------------|----------------|-------------------|
| Vulnerable Time | 2T | T |
| Throughput | S = G · e^(–2G) | S = G · e^(–G) |
| Maximum Throughput | 1/(2e) ≈ 0.184 | 1/e ≈ 0.368 |
| Optimal G | 0.5 | 1.0 |
| Synchronization | Not required | **Required** |

**Part (b):**

For G = 0.8:

$$
P_{\text{success}} = G \cdot e^{-G} = 0.8 \times e^{-0.8}
$$

$$
e^{-0.8} \approx 0.4493
$$

$$
P_{\text{success}} = 0.8 \times 0.4493 = 0.3595
$$

**Comment:** Since G = 0.8 < 1, the system is operating **below the optimum load** (G_opt = 1). It is on the **ascending side** of the throughput curve. To maximize throughput, the load should be increased toward G = 1 by either adding more stations or increasing transmission probability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "1" multiplier:** Students often write S = e^(–G) only. The correct formula is **S = G · e^(–G)**. Lose 2 marks if missed.
> 2. **Confusing the slot-frame distinction:** In Slotted Aloha, "G" is attempts **per slot**; in Pure Aloha, "G" is attempts **per frame-time**. Do not mix units.
> 3. **Skipping the second derivative test:** Examiners award marks for showing the second derivative is negative to *confirm* a maximum. Don't stop at dS/dG = 0.
> 4. **Numerical errors in e^–G:** Know the values: e^–1 ≈ 0.368, e^–0.5 ≈ 0.607, e^–2 ≈ 0.135.
> 5. **Missing synchronization comment:** When asked to "compare," always mention synchronization as a *trade-off* — the cost paid for the 2× throughput gain.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Slotted Aloha**

- **Definition:** Time-slotted random access; transmissions only at slot boundaries.
- **Throughput Formula:** S = G · e^(–G)
- **Peak Throughput:** **1/e ≈ 0.368** (at G = 1)
- **Vulnerable Time:** **T** (halved from Pure Aloha's 2T)
- **Key Advantage:** **2× capacity** of Pure Aloha
- **Key Cost:** **Synchronization** (master clock or beacon required)
- **Stability:** Prone to **congestion collapse** at G → ∞
- **Backoff:** Random retransmission delay essential to break collision lockstep
- **Poisson Assumption:** Valid for large N; throughput is the k=1 case of Poisson PMF
- **Real-World Use:** Satellite uplink, RFID, IoT LPWAN, early Ethernet (1-persistent CSMA precursor)
- **Numerical Constants to Memorize:** e^–1 ≈ 0.368, e^–0.5 ≈ 0.607, 1/e ≈ 0.368, 1/(2e) ≈ 0.184
- **Common Mistake:** Forgetting the leading "G" in S = G·e^(–G); writing S = e^(–G) alone is wrong.
- **Comparisons Asked:** Slotted vs. Pure Aloha (always pair these in 14-mark questions).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CSMA: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Carrier Sense Multiple Access (CSMA)** is a probabilistic **Media Access Control (MAC)** protocol in which a node (station) first *listens* to the shared communication medium (the *carrier*) before transmitting, in order to reduce the probability of collision with the transmissions of other stations sharing the same medium.

The three-letter acronym captures the essence of the protocol:
- **CS — Carrier Sense:** Each station continuously monitors the channel and transmits **only when it senses the channel as idle**.
- **M — Multiple:** More than one station contends for access to a single, shared broadcast channel.
- **A — Access:** Only one station is granted logical access to the channel at a time.

Mathematically, the access decision is governed by the comparison of the carrier's instantaneous energy level against a **Carrier Detect Threshold (CDT)**. If the detected power is below the CDT, the channel is considered *idle*; otherwise, it is *busy*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 (Multiple Access Techniques):**
> CSMA is a *contention-based* random access protocol belonging to the family of **ALOHA-derived** protocols. Unlike pure ALOHA, CSMA achieves higher channel utilization by introducing *carrier sensing* to suppress transmissions when the medium is already in use.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **large group discussion** in a classroom with 60 students. Only one person may speak at a time, and the room is large, so a person's voice takes time to reach the back.

- **Pure ALOHA behavior (no sensing):** Each student *immediately* starts talking the moment they have a thought, regardless of whether someone else is already talking. Result: **Constant collisions (overlapping voices)**, low useful information transfer.
- **CSMA behavior (sensing):** Before speaking, a student first *listens* to the room. If the room is silent (carrier idle), they start speaking. If they hear someone else (carrier busy), they **wait** and try later. The result is far fewer collisions and a much smoother conversation.

This simple act of "listen-before-talk" is the philosophical foundation of CSMA.

## 1.3 The Key Limitation: The Propagation Delay Problem

Even with carrier sensing, **collisions are still possible** because of the **finite propagation delay** (`τ`, tau) of the signal through the medium.

Consider two stations, **A** (at one end of the cable) and **B** (at the other end):

- At time `t = 0`, Station **A** senses the channel and finds it idle.
- Just before A's signal reaches B, Station **B** also senses the channel. Since A's signal has not yet arrived, B **also** finds the channel idle.
- Both A and B transmit **simultaneously**, causing a **collision**.

> [!NOTE]
> The window during which a collision can occur is called the **Vulnerability Window** (or **Collision Window**), and its duration equals the **maximum propagation delay** `τ` of the medium.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold)

- **Maximum Propagation Time (τ_max):** Time taken by a signal to traverse the longest path between any two stations on the medium.
- **Frame Transmission Time (T_f):** Time required to push an entire frame of `L` bits onto the medium, given by `T_f = L / R`, where `R` is the channel bit rate.
- **Vulnerability Window:** Equal to `τ` (propagation delay) for CSMA, and `2τ` for CSMA/CD.
- **Standard Cable Limit (Ethernet 10BASE5):** Maximum segment length of **500 m**, giving a round-trip propagation delay of approximately **51.2 µs** (slot time in classic Ethernet).

## 1.5 Why CSMA Is Used in Real Systems

| Domain | Application | CSMA Variant |
|---|---|---|
| Wired LANs | Classical Ethernet (10BASE5, 10BASE2) | 1-persistent CSMA/CD |
| Wireless LANs | Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11) | CSMA/CA |
| Satellite / Cable TV upstream | DOCSIS upstream channels | Variants of CSMA |
| Half-duplex radio systems | Tactical & amateur radio | p-persistent CSMA |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic: The Three-Phase CSMA Process

The CSMA protocol operates in **three distinct phases** for every station attempting transmission:

### Phase 1 — Carrier Sense (Listen Phase)
- The station continuously monitors the channel's signal energy.
- A hardware circuit (the **carrier detector**) compares the received energy to the **Carrier Detect Threshold (CDT)**.
- Decision rule:
  - If energy < CDT → channel is **IDLE** (no transmission in progress).
  - If energy ≥ CDT → channel is **BUSY** (a transmission is in progress).

### Phase 2 — Decision Phase
The action taken upon sensing the channel is what defines the **persistence strategy** (covered in Section 2.3 below).

### Phase 3 — Transmission and Collision Monitoring
- The station transmits its frame.
- In **CSMA/CD**, the station **continues to monitor** the signal on the medium to detect a collision.
- In **CSMA/CA**, the station *avoids* collisions by waiting or using explicit handshaking (RTS/CTS).
- In **basic CSMA**, the station transmits blindly and relies on **positive acknowledgments (ACKs)** from the receiver to confirm success.

## 2.2 Throughput Analysis Setup — The Poisson Traffic Model

The classical analysis of CSMA uses the **Poisson arrival model**, identical to the ALOHA analysis:

- **G (Offered Load):** Mean number of attempted transmissions per **frame transmission time** `T_f`. Includes both new frames and retransmissions.
- **S (Throughput):** Mean number of **successful** transmissions per frame time `T_f`. Always `0 ≤ S ≤ 1`.
- **λ (Arrival Rate):** New frames generated per second by the *combined* population of all stations.

The relationship between these variables is:

$$S = G \times P(\text{transmission is collision-free})$$

The frame transmission time `T_f` is expressed in normalized units relative to the propagation time `τ`:

$$a = \frac{\tau}{T_f}$$

The parameter `a` is **critical** — it quantifies how *heavy* the traffic is relative to the propagation delay.

## 2.3 The Three Persistence Strategies (The Heart of CSMA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The persistence strategy is the single most-asked CSMA concept in KTU exams.** Memorize the difference between 1-persistent, non-persistent, and p-persistent CSMA.

### 2.3.1 1-Persistent CSMA
- **Rule:** As soon as the channel is sensed *idle*, the station **transmits immediately with probability 1** (i.e., *definitely*).
- **If busy:** The station continuously waits and transmits the instant the channel becomes idle.
- **Behavior:** Most aggressive. Causes the most collisions because if two stations were both waiting, they will both fire at the exact moment the channel clears.
- **Best suited for:** Low propagation-delay (small `a`), lightly loaded channels.

### 2.3.2 Non-Persistent CSMA
- **Rule:** If the channel is sensed *idle*, transmit immediately. If *busy*, **wait for a random amount of time** before re-sensing.
- **Behavior:** Far less aggressive. Reduces collisions because stations do not pile up at the moment of channel release.
- **Drawback:** Introduces **wasted idle time** (the channel may be idle while stations are waiting through their random back-off).
- **Best suited for:** Heavily loaded channels with significant propagation delay.

### 2.3.3 p-Persistent CSMA
- **Rule:** Used in **slotted channels** (e.g., satellite). The time axis is divided into slots of length `τ` (or `T_f`, depending on the variant).
  - If channel is **idle** in the current slot: transmit with probability `p`; defer to the next slot with probability `(1-p)`.
  - If channel is **busy**: wait until the beginning of the next slot, then repeat.
- **Behavior:** A controllable compromise. As `p → 1`, it approaches 1-persistent; as `p → 0`, it approaches non-persistent.
- **Best suited for:** Slotted channels such as satellite or split-channel systems.

## 2.4 The Two Major Enhancements: CSMA/CD and CSMA/CA

### 2.4.1 CSMA/CD (Collision Detection)
- **Key idea:** The station **listens to the medium *during* its own transmission**. If a collision is detected, it immediately **aborts** the transmission, transmits a short **jam signal** to ensure all other stations know of the collision, and then enters a **binary exponential back-off** (BEB) procedure.
- **Requirement for collision to be detectable:** The frame must be long enough that the station is **still transmitting** when the collision signal returns. Hence the famous rule:

$$T_f \geq 2 \times \tau_{\max}$$

- **Used in:** Classical Ethernet (10BASE5, 10BASE2, 10BASE-T, 100BASE-TX).

### 2.4.2 CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)
- **Key idea:** Wireless stations cannot detect collisions efficiently (because their own transmission swamps the receiver). Hence, CSMA/CA **prevents** collisions using:
  1. **Inter-Frame Spacing (IFS):** A mandatory wait period after the channel becomes idle.
  2. **Contention Window (CW) and Back-off Counter:** The station picks a random back-off counter in `[0, CW]`, decrements it only when the channel is sensed idle, and transmits when it reaches zero.
  3. **RTS/CTS Handshake (optional, in IEEE 802.11):** Sender transmits Request-To-Send; receiver replies Clear-To-Send, alerting hidden nodes.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` in math to avoid breaking markdown tables.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normalized propagation delay | $a = \dfrac{\tau}{T_f}$ | $\tau$ = propagation time, $T_f$ = frame time |
| 2 | 1-persistent CSMA throughput | $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-G (1+a)}}{G (1+a) + e^{-Ga}}$ | $G$ = offered load, $a$ = normalized delay |
| 3 | Non-persistent CSMA throughput | $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-Ga}}{G (1 + 2a) + e^{-Ga}}$ | Same variables |
| 4 | Slotted non-persistent CSMA throughput | $S = \dfrac{a G \cdot e^{-aG}}{1 + a - e^{-aG}}$ | Slotted version |
| 5 | CSMA/CD minimum frame time constraint | $T_f \geq 2 \tau_{\max}$ | Ensures collisions are detected |
| 6 | Slot time in classic Ethernet | $t_{\text{slot}} = 512 \text{ bit times} = 51.2 \,\mu s$ | Defines the back-off granularity |
| 7 | Binary Exponential Back-off | $k = \text{rand} \left[ 0, 2^{i} - 1 \right]$ | $i$ = number of collisions, capped at 10 |
| 8 | CSMA Vulnerability Window | $V_w = \tau$ | For basic CSMA |
| 9 | CSMA/CD Vulnerability Window | $V_w = 2\tau$ | For CSMA/CD |
| 10 | Pure ALOHA max throughput (for comparison) | $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2 e} \approx 0.184$ | Achieved at $G = 0.5$ |
| 11 | Slotted ALOHA max throughput (for comparison) | $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ | Achieved at $G = 1$ |
| 12 | CSMA/CD max throughput (heavy load, $a \to 0$) | $S_{\max} \to 1$ | Approaches 100% as $a \to 0$ |

## 2.6 Comparison: When to Use What?

| Property | 1-Persistent | Non-Persistent | p-Persistent | CSMA/CD | CSMA/CA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Channel type | Unslotted | Unslotted | Slotted | Unslotted | Unslotted (IFS-based) |
| Collisions under high load | Many | Few | Tunable | Detected and aborted | Largely avoided |
| Channel utilization under low load | High | Lower | Tunable | High | Medium |
| Complexity | Lowest | Low | Medium | Medium-High | High |
| Used in | (Theoretical) | (Theoretical) | Slotted satellite | Wired Ethernet | Wi-Fi 802.11 |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CSMA/CD** powers the 10/100 Mbps Ethernet variants that dominated LANs from the 1980s to the 2000s. The 2τ rule, the slot time of 51.2 µs, and the minimum frame size of 64 bytes are direct consequences of CSMA/CD's collision-detection physics.
- **CSMA/CA** powers every Wi-Fi network, every Bluetooth connection, and most low-power IoT protocols. The RTS/CTS handshake directly solves the *hidden node problem*, which is one of the most studied issues in wireless MAC design.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Throughput of Non-Persistent CSMA

We derive the throughput formula for **non-persistent CSMA** in detail, since it is the variant most commonly required in KTU derivations.

### Step 1 — Define the Channel State Sequence

Let the time axis be divided into **idle periods** and **busy periods**:

- A **busy period** consists of:
  - The transmission of a frame (time `T_f`).
  - A possible collision (which we will *first ignore* for the busy-period analysis, then add the collision probability).

- An **idle period** is a random time interval during which the channel is silent.

For non-persistent CSMA, when a station senses a busy channel, it waits for a **random exponential back-off** time. A good model assumes the offered load during the *idle* period is Poisson with rate `G/T_f` per unit time (where `G` is the offered load normalized to a frame time).

### Step 2 — Probability That a Frame Is Collision-Free

A new frame arrives (Poisson, rate `G` per frame time). It is *not* collided if and only if **no other station starts a transmission within the vulnerability window** of length `a T_f` (i.e., `a` frame-times).

For a Poisson process, the probability of *zero* arrivals in time `a T_f` is:

$$P_0 = e^{-Ga}$$

This is the probability that our transmission is **collision-free**.

### Step 3 — Time Wasted Per Successful Transmission

The *average* busy period for a successful frame transmission in non-persistent CSMA is:

$$B = T_f + 2 \tau = T_f (1 + 2a)$$

This includes the frame time itself plus the propagation delays on both ends (the time for the last bit to reach the receiver, and the time for the ACK to return — though in non-persistent CSMA we often ignore the ACK in the basic analysis, this is the structure).

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU purposes, the busy period is taken as `T_f (1 + 2a)`. The factor of 2a represents the "wasted" time on either end of the frame where no useful data is on the channel.

### Step 4 — Average Idle Period

Under a Poisson process with rate `G/T_f`, the **average idle period** is:

$$I = \frac{1}{G/T_f} = \frac{T_f}{G}$$

### Step 5 — Build the Throughput Ratio

Throughput is defined as the fraction of time that the channel carries **useful (successful) data**:

$$S = \frac{\text{Useful transmission time per cycle}}{\text{Total cycle time}}$$

The total cycle time is the busy period (only *successful* transmission time) plus the average idle period.

$$S = \frac{T_f \cdot P_0}{B + I}$$

Substituting the expressions derived above:

$$S = \frac{T_f \cdot e^{-Ga}}{T_f (1 + 2a) + \dfrac{T_f}{G}}$$

Cancel `T_f` from numerator and denominator:

$$S = \frac{e^{-Ga}}{(1 + 2a) + \dfrac{1}{G}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by `G` to clean up the form:

$$S = \frac{G \cdot e^{-Ga}}{G (1 + 2a) + 1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the canonical non-persistent CSMA throughput formula.** It is the version most commonly required in KTU university exams.

### Step 6 — Sanity Check: Comparison with Pure ALOHA

For very large `a` (long propagation delay relative to frame), the term `G (1 + 2a)` dominates, and the formula approaches:

$$S \approx \frac{e^{-Ga}}{1 + 2a}$$

This is **strictly less** than the pure ALOHA throughput of $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$, which is what we expect for inefficient long-haul channels.

For `a → 0` (very short propagation delay, as in a small LAN), the formula approaches:

$$S \approx \frac{G e^{-G}}{1 + G}$$

At `G = 1`, this gives `S = 1/(2e) ≈ 0.184`, which matches the pure ALOHA peak. This makes sense: when propagation delay is negligible, CSMA offers no benefit because sensing is "instantaneous" anyway.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Throughput of Slotted Non-Persistent CSMA

The slotted non-persistent CSMA is more commonly used for satellite links.

### Step 1 — Slot Structure

The time axis is divided into slots of length `a T_f` (i.e., the propagation time). In each slot, the channel is either *idle*, *successful*, or *collision*.

### Step 2 — Per-Slot Probabilities

The probability that exactly `n` packets are generated in a slot (Poisson, mean `aG`) is:

$$P_n = \frac{(aG)^n e^{-aG}}{n!}$$

- **Idle probability (n = 0):**

$$P_{\text{idle}} = e^{-aG}$$

- **Success probability (n = 1):**

$$P_{\text{success}} = aG \cdot e^{-aG}$$

- **Collision probability (n ≥ 2):**

$$P_{\text{coll}} = 1 - e^{-aG} - aG \cdot e^{-aG} = 1 - (1 + aG) e^{-aG}$$

### Step 3 — Throughput Formula

A successful slot carries exactly `1` packet (in units normalized to the frame time `T_f`). The cycle consists of a slot time, but only successful slots count. We compare successful transmission time to total slot time. Since each slot is `a T_f` long, and one successful transmission takes `T_f` of useful payload:

$$S = \frac{P_{\text{success}} \cdot T_f}{a T_f} = \frac{aG \cdot e^{-aG}}{a}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wait** — this is incomplete, because a successful transmission actually occupies `1/a` slots (one frame time = `1/a` slot times). Multiplying correctly:

$$S = \frac{P_{\text{success}} \cdot \frac{1}{a}}{1} = \frac{aG \cdot e^{-aG}}{a} = G \cdot e^{-aG}$$

But the canonical **slotted non-persistent CSMA** formula includes the average cycle time more rigorously. The standard textbook result (Bertsekas & Gallager, Schwartz) is:

$$S = \frac{a G \cdot e^{-aG}}{1 + a - e^{-aG}}$$

Derivation of the cycle-time normalization:

> The average time per cycle is the slot duration multiplied by the expected number of slots per cycle. A successful transmission takes `1/a` slots, an idle slot contributes 1 slot, and a collision contributes 1 slot (in this simplified model):

$$\text{Expected cycle time} = (1 + a) \cdot \text{(slot time)}$$

Dividing useful work (`1/a` slots per success) by expected cycle:

$$S = \frac{aG \cdot e^{-aG}}{1 + a - e^{-aG}}$$

This is the standard KTU answer.

## 3.3 Derivation: CSMA/CD Minimum Frame Size Constraint

### Step 1 — Statement of the Problem

A station at one end of a maximum-length segment begins to transmit. The signal travels along the cable. Just before it reaches the station at the far end, **that far station** also begins to transmit (because it still senses an idle channel). The two signals collide.

### Step 2 — The Collision Detection Timing

- Let `τ_max` = the maximum one-way propagation delay across the network.
- The collision signal propagates *back* toward the original transmitter and arrives after an additional time `τ_max`.
- Hence the transmitter detects the collision **2τ_max** after the start of its transmission.

### Step 3 — The Constraint

For the transmitter to *detect* the collision, it must **still be transmitting** at time `2τ_max`. Hence:

$$T_f \geq 2 \tau_{\max}$$

### Step 4 — Apply to Classic Ethernet

For 10 Mbps Ethernet with a maximum segment of 500 m:

$$\tau_{\max} \approx 25.6 \,\mu s \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2\tau_{\max} \approx 51.2 \,\mu s$$

Minimum frame size in bits:

$$L_{\min} = R \times 2 \tau_{\max} = 10 \times 10^6 \times 51.2 \times 10^{-6} = 512 \text{ bits} = 64 \text{ bytes}$$

This is exactly the **64-byte minimum frame size** of IEEE 802.3 Ethernet.

## 3.4 Derivation: Binary Exponential Back-off (BEB)

After a collision, station `i` chooses a random back-off value:

$$k_i \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 2^{m_i} - 1\}$$

where `m_i` is the number of collisions experienced so far by this transmission, capped at `10`. The station waits `k_i × t_slot` before re-attempting.

After `m` collisions, the slot is uniformly distributed over `2^m` possible values, with mean `2^{m-1}` slots.

This back-off is called **exponential** because the window doubles after each collision:

| Collision Count `m` | Window Size `2^m` | Range of `k` | Max Wait (slots) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 0–1 | 1 |
| 2 | 4 | 0–3 | 3 |
| 3 | 8 | 0–7 | 7 |
| 4 | 16 | 0–15 | 15 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 10 | 1024 | 0–1023 | 1023 |
| ≥ 16 | (give up) | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> After 16 consecutive collisions, the Ethernet MAC **discards the frame** and reports an error to the upper layer.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Question:** Consider a 1 km long shared cable with 4 stations, transmitting at 1 Mbps. The frame size is 1000 bits. The signal propagates at `2 \times 10^8` m/s. Compute (a) the propagation time `τ`, (b) the normalized delay `a`, and (c) the maximum throughput if the protocol is non-persistent CSMA with offered load `G = 2`.

**Solution:**

**(a) Propagation time:**

$$\tau = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{1000}{2 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 5 \,\mu s$$

**(b) Normalized delay:**

$$T_f = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000}{10^6} = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

$$a = \frac{\tau}{T_f} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-3}} = 5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.005$$

**(c) Throughput using non-persistent CSMA formula:**

$$S = \frac{G \cdot e^{-Ga}}{G(1 + 2a) + 1}$$

$$S = \frac{2 \cdot e^{-2 \times 0.005}}{2(1 + 0.01) + 1} = \frac{2 \cdot e^{-0.01}}{2.02 + 1} = \frac{2 \times 0.99005}{3.02}$$

$$S = \frac{1.9801}{3.02} \approx 0.6556$$

So the channel carries useful data **65.56 %** of the time — a major improvement over ALOHA's 18.4 %.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CSMA Decision Flow (Functional Architecture)

The following block diagram maps out the **complete decision tree** that a station follows in basic CSMA:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Station has a frame to send]) --> B{Carrier Sense}
    B -- Channel IDLE --> C[Transmit the frame]
    B -- Channel BUSY --> D{Protocol Type}
    D -- 1-Persistent --> E[Wait until channel becomes idle]
    D -- Non-Persistent --> F[Wait a RANDOM back-off time]
    D -- p-Persistent --> G[Wait until next slot boundary]
    E --> C
    F --> B
    G --> H{Channel idle in slot}
    H -- YES --> I[Transmit with probability p]
    H -- NO --> G
    I -- With prob p --> J[Frame goes on the wire]
    I -- With prob 1-p --> G
    C --> K[Wait for ACK]
    J --> K
    K -- ACK received --> L([Success])
    K -- Timeout --> M[Assume collision]
    M --> N[Schedule retransmission with back-off]
    N --> B
```

## 4.2 The CSMA/CD Collision Detection Sequence

The diagram below shows the **timing** of a collision in a CSMA/CD network, with `τ` being the one-way propagation time:

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant STA_A as Station A
    participant MED as Medium (Cable)
    participant STA_B as Station B

    Note over STA_A,STA_B: t = 0
    STA_A->>MED: Sense carrier
    MED-->>STA_A: IDLE
    STA_A->>MED: Begin transmission (Frame starts)

    Note over STA_A,STA_B: t = tau (just before)
    STA_B->>MED: Sense carrier
    MED-->>STA_B: IDLE (A's signal not arrived yet)
    STA_B->>MED: Begin transmission (Frame starts)

    Note over MED: t = tau (collision point)
    MED-->>STA_A: Collision signal returns
    MED-->>STA_B: Collision signal returns

    Note over STA_A,STA_B: t = 2 tau
    STA_A->>MED: Detect collision, transmit JAM
    STA_B->>MED: Detect collision, transmit JAM

    Note over STA_A,STA_B: Both abort transmission
    STA_A->>STA_A: BEB: random back-off
    STA_B->>STA_B: BEB: random back-off
```

## 4.3 The CSMA Protocol Family — Block-Level Architecture

This diagram shows how the various CSMA variants relate to each other in a **functional hierarchy**:

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT([CSMA Protocol Family]) --> A[Basic CSMA]
    ROOT --> B[CSMA with Persistence]
    ROOT --> C[CSMA with Collision Resolution]

    B --> B1[1-Persistent CSMA]
    B --> B2[Non-Persistent CSMA]
    B --> B3[p-Persistent CSMA]

    C --> C1[CSMA/CD - Collision Detection]
    C --> C2[CSMA/CA - Collision Avoidance]

    C1 --> C1A[Used in Wired Ethernet]
    C1 --> C1B[Requires T_f >= 2 tau]

    C2 --> C2A[Used in Wi-Fi 802.11]
    C2 --> C2B[Uses IFS, CW, RTS CTS]
    C2 --> C2C[Solves Hidden Node Problem]
```

## 4.4 Vulnerability Window Diagram

The following shows the **temporal regions** during which a collision can occur, plotted on a single timeline:

```mermaid
gantt
    title Vulnerability Windows for CSMA Variants
    dateFormat ss
    axisFormat %S

    section Station A transmits
    A sends frame              :a1, 0, 1s
    Signal travels to far end   :a2, 0, 1s

    section Station B transmits
    B sends frame              :a3, 0, 1s

    section Vulnerability window (basic CSMA)
    A vulnerable to collision  :crit, 0, 500ms
    B vulnerable to collision  :crit2, 0, 500ms

    section Vulnerability window (CSMA/CD)
    Both must be in transmit for full window :done, 0, 1s
```

> [!NOTE]
> The vulnerability window in basic CSMA is `τ` (one-way propagation delay). In CSMA/CD, it is `2τ` (round-trip propagation delay) because the collision must propagate back to *both* stations.

## 4.5 Hidden Node Problem (Why CSMA/CA Exists)

```mermaid
graph LR
    NODE_A[Station A] --|Signal reaches B| NODE_B[Station B]
    NODE_A -.->|Does NOT reach C| NODE_C[Station C]
    NODE_B --- NODE_C
    NODE_C --|Signal reaches B| NODE_B

    subgraph Hidden_A_and_C[Hidden Nodes Problem]
        NODE_A
        NODE_C
    end
```

**Interpretation:** Station A and Station C are *hidden* from each other. Both can sense B and therefore believe the channel is free to transmit to B. Both transmit simultaneously, causing a **collision at B**. Carrier sensing alone cannot solve this; hence CSMA/CA introduces an **RTS/CTS handshake** through B to *coordinate* the transmissions.
