# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define subnetting. Given the IP address `172.20.0.0/16`, design 8 subnets. List the new subnet mask, block size, and the first three subnet IDs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Subnetting is the process of dividing a large IP network into smaller sub-networks by borrowing bits from the host portion of an IP address, thereby extending the network prefix.

> **Calculation (2 marks):**
> `172.20.0.0/16` is a Class B network. Host bits = 16. For `S = 8` subnets, we need $2^{n} \geq 8 \Rightarrow n = 3$ borrowed bits.
> New CIDR = `/16 + 3 = /19` → Mask = `255.255.224.0`. Block size $B = 256 - 224 = 32$.
> First three subnets: `172.20.0.0/19`, `172.20.32.0/19`, `172.20.64.0/19`.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**What is Distance Vector Routing? State the Bellman–Ford equation used by DVR and explain the count-to-infinity problem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** DVR is a dynamic routing algorithm where each router maintains a table of distances (metrics) to all destinations and exchanges these tables with **directly connected neighbours** periodically.

> **Equation (1 mark):** $D_{x}(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \left\{ c(x,v) + D_{v}(y) \right\}$

> **Count-to-infinity (1 mark):** When a link fails, routers keep advertising stale routes. The metric keeps increasing by 1 hop per update cycle until it reaches 16 (treated as infinity in RIP), causing slow convergence and routing loops. Mitigated by **split horizon, poison reverse, and hold-down timers**.

---

## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Subnetting problems:** Most students lose 2–3 marks by *forgetting to subtract 2* for network and broadcast addresses. Always write the formula $2^{h} - 2$.
> - **DVR tables:** Students frequently mark the wrong next-hop. The next-hop is **always the neighbour that advertised the route**, not the destination itself.
> - **Bellman–Ford:** Examiners expect you to **show the substitution explicitly** in the $c(x,v) + D_{v}(y)$ form — not just the final number.
> - **VLSM:** Always allocate the **largest subnet first** to avoid fragmentation; failing this costs full marks on that sub-part.

---

### 🔵 Question A (14 Marks) — Subnetting + DVR

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Given the network `192.168.50.0/24`, perform subnetting to create **6 subnets** with the **maximum possible hosts** per subnet. List:
   (i) New subnet mask and CIDR
   (ii) Block size / magic number
   (iii) Subnet IDs, first usable host, last usable host, and broadcast for all 6 subnets. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the network diagram below, construct the **converged distance vector routing tables** for routers R1, R2, and R3. Use hop count as the metric. Show **at least two iterations** of table exchange. **(7 marks)**

```
        R1 ----5---- R2
         \         /
          \       /
           3     2
            \   /
             R3
     N1--R1   N2--R2   N3--R3
```

#### Model Solution (a) — Subnetting

> **Step 1:** Class C → host bits = 8. For `S = 6`: $2^{n} \geq 6 \Rightarrow n = 3$.

> **Step 2:** New CIDR = `/27`, Mask = `255.255.255.224`. Block size $B = 256 - 224 = 32$.

> **Step 3:** Hosts per subnet = $2^{5} - 2 = 30$.

| Subnet | Network ID | 1st Host | Last Host | Broadcast |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 192.168.50.0 | .1 | .30 | .31 |
| 2 | 192.168.50.32 | .33 | .62 | .63 |
| 3 | 192.168.50.64 | .65 | .94 | .95 |
| 4 | 192.168.50.96 | .97 | .126 | .127 |
| 5 | 192.168.50.128 | .129 | .158 | .159 |
| 6 | 192.168.50.160 | .161 | .190 | .191 |
| Reserved | 192.168.50.192 | — | — | 192.168.50.255 |

#### Model Solution (b) — DVR Tables

**Iteration 1 — Initial Tables (Direct links only):**

| R1 | Dest | Dist | Next-Hop |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| | N1 | 0 | Direct |
| | N2 | ∞ | — |
| | N3 | ∞ | — |

| R2 | Dest | Dist | Next-Hop |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| | N1 | ∞ | — |
| | N2 | 0 | Direct |
| | N3 | ∞ | — |

| R3 | Dest | Dist | Next-Hop |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| | N1 | ∞ | — |
| | N2 | ∞ | — |
| | N3 | 0 | Direct |

**Iteration 2 — R1 receives from R2 (cost 5) and R3 (cost 3):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{R1}(N2) &= \min\bigl\{ 5 + 0,\; 3 + \infty \bigr\} = 5 \quad (\text{via R2}) \\[4pt]
D_{R1}(N3) &= \min\bigl\{ 5 + \infty,\; 3 + 0 \bigr\} = 3 \quad (\text{via R3})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 3 — Convergence (all routers exchange again):**

| R1 (Final) | Dest | Dist | Next-Hop |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| | N1 | 0 | Direct |
| | N2 | 5 | R2 |
| | N3 | 3 | R3 |

| R2 (Final) | Dest | Dist | Next-Hop |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| | N1 | 5 | R1 |
| | N2 | 0 | Direct |
| | N3 | 7 | R1 |

| R3 (Final) | Dest | Dist | Next-Hop |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| | N1 | 3 | R1 |
| | N2 | 5 | R1 |
| | N3 | 0 | Direct |

**Verification (Bellman–Ford at R2 for N3):**
$$
D_{R2}(N3) = \min\bigl\{ 5 + 3,\; 2 + 0 \bigr\} = \min(8, 2) = 2
$$
Wait — re-evaluating with the **actual** topology (R1–R2 = 5, R2–R3 = 2, R1–R3 = 3):

| R2 (Corrected) | Dest | Dist | Next-Hop |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| | N1 | 5 | R1 |
| | N2 | 0 | Direct |
| | N3 | 2 | R3 |

The corrected entry shows that R2 reaches N3 directly via the cost-2 link to R3.

---

### 🟢 Question B (14 Marks) — VLSM + DVR Counting

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Using **VLSM**, design subnets for the network `10.0.0.0/8` to satisfy the following needs: HQ needs **1000 hosts**, Branch A needs **500 hosts**, Branch B needs **200 hosts**, Branch C needs **100 hosts**, and Branch D needs **50 hosts**. Show the allocation table with subnet IDs, masks, and broadcast addresses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **count-to-infinity problem** in distance vector routing with a suitable example. Discuss any **two mitigation techniques** with their mathematical/operational justification. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — VLSM

> **Step 1 — HQ (1000 hosts):** $2^{h} - 2 \geq 1000 \Rightarrow h = 10$ → CIDR = `/22` → Mask = `255.192.0.0`. Block size in 2nd octet = `64`. Range: `10.0.0.0 – 10.3.255.255`.

> **Step 2 — Branch A (500 hosts):** $2^{h} - 2 \geq 500 \Rightarrow h = 9$ → CIDR = `/23`. Block size = `2` in 3rd octet. Range: `10.4.0.0 – 10.5.255.255`.

> **Step 3 — Branch B (200 hosts):** $2^{h} - 2 \geq 200 \Rightarrow h = 8$ → CIDR = `/24`. Block = `1`. Range: `10.6.0.0 – 10.6.255.255`.

> **Step 4 — Branch C (100 hosts):** $2^{h} - 2 \geq 100 \Rightarrow h = 7$ → CIDR = `/25`. Block = `128` in 4th octet. Range: `10.7.0.0 – 10.7.127.255`.

> **Step 5 — Branch D (50 hosts):** $2^{h} - 2 \geq 50 \Rightarrow h = 6$ → CIDR = `/26`. Block = `64`. Range: `10.7.128.0 – 10.7.191.255`.

| Department | Hosts | Mask | CIDR | Network | Broadcast |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| HQ | 1000 | 255.192.0.0 | /22 | 10.0.0.0 | 10.3.255.255 |
| Branch A | 500 | 255.254.0.0 | /23 | 10.4.0.0 | 10.5.255.255 |
| Branch B | 200 | 255.255.0.0 | /24 | 10.6.0.0 | 10.6.255.255 |
| Branch C | 100 | 255.255.128.0 | /25 | 10.7.0.0 | 10.7.127.255 |
| Branch D | 50 | 255.255.192.0 | /26 | 10.7.128.0 | 10.7.191.255 |

#### Model Solution (b) — Count to Infinity

> **Explanation (4 marks):** When a link fails, neighbouring routers do not immediately know that the destination has become unreachable. They continue to advertise stale routes. Since routers have no global view, they trust neighbours' advertisements. If R2 had a route "to N3 with distance 2 via R3" and the R2–R3 link fails, R2 marks the route as invalid (16). However, R1 still advertises "N3 with distance 3" to R2, because R1 learned it from R2 earlier (split horizon would prevent this). R2 then updates "N3 with distance 4 via R1", and R1 updates "N3 with distance 5 via R2". The metric increments by 1 per cycle, slowly climbing toward 16. This is the **count-to-infinity problem**.

> **Mitigation 1 — Split Horizon (1.5 marks):** Rule: "Never advertise a route back to the neighbour from whom you learned it." This breaks the immediate feedback loop that drives count-to-infinity.

> **Mitigation 2 — Poison Reverse (1.5 marks):** Rule: "Advertise the route back to the originating neighbour with metric = 16 (infinity)." This explicitly signals that the route is invalid through that neighbour. Mathematically, this caps the misinformation at one hop and forces routers to seek alternative paths, accelerating convergence.

---

# 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Subnetting, Routing Schemes: DVR**

- ✅ **Subnetting** = borrowing host bits to create sub-networks. Formula: $2^{n} \geq S$ for subnets, $2^{h} - 2 \geq H$ for usable hosts.
- ✅ **Block size (Magic Number)** = $256 - \text{(last non-255 octet of mask)}$. Subnets increment by this value.
- ✅ **Classful boundaries:** Class A `/8`, B `/16`, C `/24`. CIDR is classless.
- ✅ **VLSM rule:** Allocate the **largest** subnet first to avoid address fragmentation.
- ✅ **DVR** = Distance + Vector; each router shares its **full table** with **direct neighbours** only.
- ✅ **Bellman–Ford equation:** $D_{x}(y) = \min_{v} \{c(x,v) + D_{v}(y)\}$.
- ✅ **RIP metrics:** Max hops = 15; 16 = unreachable. Update interval = 30 s.
- ✅ **Convergence in DVR** is **slow**; vulnerable to routing loops.
- ✅ **Count-to-infinity** is mitigated by **Split Horizon**, **Poison Reverse**, **Hold-Down Timers**, and **Triggered Updates**.
- ✅ **Subnets, hosts, masks, and CIDR** are interchangeable representations of the same division — examiners expect you to convert fluently between dotted-decimal, CIDR `/n`, and binary forms.
- ✅ For DVR tables, the **next-hop is the advertising neighbour**, never the destination network itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Mnemonic — DVR Mitigation Techniques → "SPHT":** **S**plit horizon, **P**oison reverse, **H**old-down timers, **T**riggered updates.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Subnetting, Routing Schemes: Distance Vector Routing** 🎓

topics.title: Link State Routing, Transport Layer: TCP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT416 - PRINCIPLES OF COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Link State Routing, Transport Layer: TCP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Link State Routing (LSR) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Link State Routing (LSR)** is a dynamic, intra-domain routing algorithm in which every router maintains a **complete and identical map of the entire network topology**. Each router independently computes its own shortest-path tree (SPT) to every other destination using **Dijkstra's Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm** based on a globally consistent **Link State Database (LSDB)**.

The Open Shortest Path First (**OSPF**) protocol and the Intermediate System to Intermediate System (**IS-IS**) protocol are the two principal implementations of Link State Routing used in modern IP networks.

### 1.2 Transport Layer — Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)

> [!NOTE]
> **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** is a **connection-oriented**, reliable, full-duplex, byte-stream transport layer protocol defined in **RFC 793**. It provides in-order delivery, error recovery, flow control, and congestion control, ensuring that a stream of bytes sent by a source arrives at the destination **without duplication, loss, or reordering**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The GPS vs. The Postal System

> [!TIP]
> **Link State Routing = Google Maps for the network.** Every router, like a driver with full access to Google Maps, knows **every road (link), every city (router), and every traffic condition (cost)** in the country. The driver personally computes the best route from his city to any other city using the **Dijkstra algorithm** — there is no central authority telling him which path to take.
>
> **Distance Vector Routing = Asking locals for directions.** The driver only knows the directions given by his **immediate neighbours**, who in turn only know directions from their neighbours. This is simple, but rumours (count-to-infinity) can spread if a road closes.
>
> **TCP = Registered Post with Acknowledgement Slip.** Before sending a parcel, both sides sign a contract (**3-way handshake**). Every parcel sent has a serial number. The receiver signs an **acknowledgement slip** for each parcel. If a slip is missing, the sender retransmits. The sender also does not flood the receiver's mailbox — he watches the receiver's "window" announcement to know how much the receiver can hold (**flow control**), and he senses network congestion to slow down (**congestion control**).

### 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **LSP** | Link State Packet | Data unit |
| **LSDB** | Link State Database | Per-router table |
| **SPF Tree** | Shortest Path First Tree | Computed topology |
| **cwnd** | Congestion Window | **Bytes** (MSS units) |
| **ssthresh** | Slow Start Threshold | **Bytes** |
| **RTT** | Round Trip Time | **milliseconds (ms)** |
| **MSS** | Maximum Segment Size | **Bytes** (default 1460) |
| **MTU** | Maximum Transmission Unit | **Bytes** (default 1500) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Phases of Link State Routing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every router running LSR executes **five sequential phases**. Understanding these phases is essential for KTU Module 4 questions.

1. **Discovering Neighbours**
   - Each router sends periodic **Hello packets** out of every active interface.
   - On receipt, it learns the **Router ID (RID)** of the neighbour and the cost of the link.

2. **Measuring Link Cost (Metric)**
   - Cost is usually a function of **bandwidth, delay, or administratively assigned value**.
   - For OSPF: $Cost = \dfrac{\text{Reference Bandwidth}}{\text{Interface Bandwidth}}$ with the reference being **100 Mbps**.

3. **Constructing the Link State Packet (LSP / LSA)**
   - LSP contains: **RID, neighbour RID, cost to neighbour, sequence number, age timer**.

4. **Flooding the LSP**
   - When an LSP is received, the router checks the **sequence number**:
     - If new → forward to all interfaces except the incoming one (**reliable flooding**).
     - If duplicate or older → discard.
   - This guarantees every router receives the LSP and builds an **identical LSDB**.

5. **Computing the Shortest Path (Dijkstra's Algorithm)**
   - Every router runs **Dijkstra's SPF algorithm** locally on its LSDB to compute the SPT.
   - The SPT yields the best next-hop for every destination.

### 2.2 Dijkstra's Algorithm — Logical Steps

- **Step 0 (Initialise):** $N = \{s\}$ (visited set contains only the source $s$). Set $D(s) = 0$ and $D(v) = c(s,v)$ for all other vertices $v$, where $c(s,v)$ is the direct link cost or $\infty$ if not directly connected.
- **Step 1 (Find Minimum):** Find vertex $u \notin N$ such that $D(u)$ is minimum.
- **Step 2 (Add to N):** Add $u$ to the visited set $N$.
- **Step 3 (Relax Edges):** For every $v \notin N$, update:

$$D(v) = \min\{D(v),\; D(u) + c(u,v)\}$$

- **Step 4 (Repeat):** Go to Step 1 until $N$ contains all vertices.

### 2.3 TCP Segment Header — Field-by-Field Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> A **TCP segment** consists of a 20-byte (minimum) header plus optional options, followed by data.

| Field | Size (bits) | Purpose |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Source Port** | 16 | Identifies the sending application |
| **Destination Port** | 16 | Identifies the receiving application |
| **Sequence Number** | 32 | Byte-stream position of the first byte in this segment |
| **Acknowledgement Number** | 32 | Next expected byte (cumulative ACK) |
| **Data Offset** | 4 | Header length in 32-bit words |
| **Reserved / Flags** | 6 (URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN) | Control bits |
| **Window Size** | 16 | Receiver's available buffer (flow control) |
| **Checksum** | 16 | End-to-end integrity check |
| **Urgent Pointer** | 16 | Offset of urgent data |
| **Options + Padding** | variable | MSS, Window Scaling, Timestamps, SACK |

### 2.4 TCP Connection Management — Three-Way Handshake

1. **SYN:** Client $\rightarrow$ Server with initial sequence number $x$. Flag `SYN = 1`.
2. **SYN-ACK:** Server $\rightarrow$ Client with $y$ and ACK $= x + 1$. Flags `SYN = 1, ACK = 1`.
3. **ACK:** Client $\rightarrow$ Server with ACK $= y + 1$. Flag `ACK = 1`. Connection **ESTABLISHED**.

Connection termination is a **four-way exchange** using `FIN` flags and a half-close mechanism.

### 2.5 TCP Congestion Control — Four Algorithms

| Algorithm | Behaviour | Trigger | cwnd Update |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Slow Start** | Exponential growth | Connection start / after timeout | $cwnd = cwnd + MSS$ per ACK (effectively doubles per RTT) |
| **Congestion Avoidance** | Linear growth | $cwnd \geq ssthresh$ | $cwnd = cwnd + \dfrac{MSS^2}{cwnd}$ per ACK |
| **Fast Retransmit** | Retransmit on **3 duplicate ACKs** | 3 dup-ACKs received | Resends lost segment immediately (no timeout) |
| **Fast Recovery** | Bypass slow start | After fast retransmit | $ssthresh = cwnd/2$; $cwnd = ssthresh + 3 \cdot MSS$ |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently ask the difference between **TCP Tahoe** and **TCP Reno**. Tahoe performs slow start even after fast retransmit. Reno skips slow start and enters fast recovery directly. Know this distinction — it is a 3-mark favourite.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Dijkstra Relaxation | $D(v) = \min\{D(v), D(u) + c(u,v)\}$ | Computes shortest path in OSPF / IS-IS |
| OSPF Cost | $Cost = \lceil \text{Ref BW} / \text{Link BW} \rceil$ | Default 100 Mbps reference |
| TCP Timeout | $Timeout = \alpha \cdot EstimatedRTT + (1-\alpha) \cdot SampleRTT$ | Karn's algorithm extension |
| Smoothed RTT | $EstimatedRTT_{new} = (1-\alpha) \cdot EstimatedRTT + \alpha \cdot SampleRTT$ | $\alpha$ typically 1/8 |
| RTT Variance | $DevRTT_{new} = (1-\beta) \cdot DevRTT + \beta \cdot \vert SampleRTT - EstimatedRTT \vert$ | $\beta$ typically 1/4 |
| Sliding Window | $LastByteSent - LastByteAcked \leq \min\{rwnd, cwnd\}$ | Combined flow + congestion control |
| Slow Start Growth | $cwnd$ doubles per RTT | First phase of any TCP connection |
| Congestion Avoidance | $cwnd$ increases by 1 MSS per RTT | Steady-state throughput |
| Throughput (steady) | $\approx \dfrac{1.22 \cdot MSS}{RTT \cdot \sqrt{p}}$ | $p$ = packet loss probability |
| Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = BW \times RTT$ | Determines optimal window size |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Link State Routing** underpins the **internet backbone** (OSPF in ISP networks, IS-IS in carrier networks). It enables **fast convergence** within sub-seconds and supports **Variable Length Subnet Masking (VLSM)** and **Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR)**.
- **TCP** is the bedrock of **HTTP/HTTPS (web), SSH, FTP, SMTP (email), and BGP** (which runs over TCP port 179). Every byte of a YouTube stream, a WhatsApp message, or a banking transaction is delivered reliably using TCP's flow and congestion control machinery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Dijkstra's Algorithm on a 5-Node Graph

**Network Topology with Link Costs:**

| Edge | A-B | A-C | A-D | B-C | B-E | C-D | C-E | D-E |
| :--- | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: |
| Cost | 4 | 1 | 8 | 2 | 5 | 6 | 3 | 2 |

**Source Node = A. Find shortest paths to B, C, D, E.**

**Initialisation (Step 0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \{A\} \\
D[A] &= 0,\; D[B]=4,\; D[C]=1,\; D[D]=8,\; D[E]=\infty
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Iteration 1: Pick minimum from D not in N → C with D[C] = 1**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Add C to N: } N = \{A, C\} \\
&\text{Relax edges from C to B, D, E:} \\
&D[B] = \min(4,\; 1+2) = 3 \\
&D[D] = \min(8,\; 1+6) = 7 \\
&D[E] = \min(\infty,\; 1+3) = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**D-state after Iteration 1:** $D[A]=0,\; D[B]=3,\; D[C]=1,\; D[D]=7,\; D[E]=4$

---

**Iteration 2: Pick minimum from D not in N → B with D[B] = 3**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Add B to N: } N = \{A, C, B\} \\
&\text{Relax edges from B to E (only unvisited neighbour):} \\
&D[E] = \min(4,\; 3+5) = 4 \quad (\text{no change})
\end{aligned}
$$

**D-state after Iteration 2:** $D[A]=0,\; D[B]=3,\; D[C]=1,\; D[D]=7,\; D[E]=4$

---

**Iteration 3: Pick minimum from D not in N → E with D[E] = 4**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Add E to N: } N = \{A, C, B, E\} \\
&\text{Relax edges from E to D (unvisited):} \\
&D[D] = \min(7,\; 4+2) = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**D-state after Iteration 3:** $D[A]=0,\; D[B]=3,\; D[C]=1,\; D[D]=6,\; D[E]=4$

---

**Iteration 4: Pick minimum from D not in N → D with D[D] = 6**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Add D to N: } N = \{A, C, B, E, D\} \\
&\text{All nodes visited. Stop.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Final Shortest Path Table from Source A:**

| Destination | Shortest Distance | Path |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| A | 0 | A |
| B | 3 | A $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ B |
| C | 1 | A $\rightarrow$ C |
| D | 6 | A $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ E $\rightarrow$ D |
| E | 4 | A $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ E |

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: LSR vs Distance Vector Routing (DVR)

| Property | Distance Vector (RIP) | Link State (OSPF) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Information shared** | Whole routing table to neighbours | LSP with own links only |
| **Scope of visibility** | Direct neighbours only | Entire network topology |
| **Algorithm used** | Bellman-Ford | Dijkstra |
| **Convergence speed** | Slow (count-to-infinity) | Fast (seconds) |
| **CPU / Memory** | Low | High (LSDB, SPT) |
| **Bandwidth usage** | Low | High during flooding, low afterwards |
| **Hierarchy** | Flat | Supports **area hierarchy** (OSPF) |
| **Loop prevention** | Split horizon, poison reverse | SPF computation prevents loops |
| **Example protocol** | RIP, IGRP | OSPF, IS-IS |
| **Metric** | Hop count | Composite cost (bandwidth-based) |

### 3.3 TCP Congestion Window Evolution — Step-by-Step

Assume $MSS = 1$ KB, $ssthresh = 8$ KB, $RTT = 100$ ms.

**Phase 1 — Slow Start (cwnd < ssthresh):**

| RTT # | cwnd (KB) | Action |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | 1 | Send 1 MSS; ACK received |
| 2 | 2 | cwnd doubles per ACK effectively |
| 3 | 4 | Exponential growth |
| 4 | 8 | Reaches ssthresh → switch to CA |

**Phase 2 — Congestion Avoidance (cwnd ≥ ssthresh):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{For every ACK: } \Delta cwnd = \dfrac{MSS^2}{cwnd} \\
&\text{Equivalent per-RTT increase: } cwnd_{new} = cwnd_{old} + MSS
\end{aligned}
$$

**Phase 3 — Timeout Occurs at cwnd = 12 KB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ssthresh &= \dfrac{cwnd_{timeout}}{2} = 6\ \text{KB} \\
cwnd &= 1\ \text{MSS} \quad (\text{restart Slow Start})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Phase 4 — Three Duplicate ACKs Received (Fast Retransmit + Fast Recovery):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ssthresh &= \dfrac{cwnd}{2} \\
cwnd &= ssthresh + 3 \cdot MSS \quad (\text{account for 3 buffered segments}) \\
&\text{Enter Congestion Avoidance directly (no Slow Start)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 TCP Three-Way Handshake — Full State Trace

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a standard 6-mark to 7-mark derivation in KTU boards. Memorise the sequence numbers and the flag transitions.

| Step | Direction | Flag | Seq | Ack | State Transition |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Client $\rightarrow$ Server | SYN | $x$ | — | CLOSED $\rightarrow$ SYN_SENT |
| 2 | Server $\rightarrow$ Client | SYN, ACK | $y$ | $x+1$ | LISTEN $\rightarrow$ SYN_RCVD |
| 3 | Client $\rightarrow$ Server | ACK | $x+1$ | $y+1$ | SYN_SENT $\rightarrow$ ESTABLISHED |
| 4 | Server receives ACK | — | — | — | SYN_RCVD $\rightarrow$ ESTABLISHED |

Connection teardown uses a **four-way FIN-ACK exchange** with **TIME_WAIT** of $2 \times MSL$ (Maximum Segment Lifetime, default 60 s) to absorb stray packets.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: LSR Flooding Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Router A Boots] --> discA[Discover Neighbours via Hello Packets]
    discA --> measA[Measure Link Cost to Each Neighbour]
    measA --> buildA[Build Local Link State Packet LSP]
    buildA --> floodA[Flood LSP to All Neighbours via Reliable Flooding]
    floodA --> bcastA[Each Neighbour Forwards LSP to its Neighbours]
    bcastA --> dbA[Populate Link State Database LSDB]
    dbA --> dijkA[Run Dijkstra SPF Algorithm on LSDB]
    dijkA --> sptA[Compute Shortest Path Tree SPT]
    sptA --> rtabA[Install Routes in Routing Table]
    rtabA --> monA[Monitor Link Changes and Reflood on Topology Change]
    monA --> floodA

    startB[Router B Boots] --> discB[Discover Neighbours via Hello Packets]
    discB --> measB[Measure Link Cost to Each Neighbour]
    measB --> buildB[Build Local Link State Packet LSP]
    buildB --> floodB[Flood LSP to All Neighbours via Reliable Flooding]
    floodB --> bcastB[Each Neighbour Forwards LSP to its Neighbours]
    bcastB --> dbB[Populate Link State Database LSDB]
    dbB --> dijkB[Run Dijkstra SPF Algorithm on LSDB]
    dijkB --> sptB[Compute Shortest Path Tree SPT]
    sptB --> rtabB[Install Routes in Routing Table]
    rtabB --> monB[Monitor Link Changes and Reflood on Topology Change]
    monB --> floodB

    floodA <-->|LSP Exchange| floodB
    rtabA --- rtabB
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrow between `floodA` and `floodB` represents the **bidirectional reliable flooding** that guarantees every router in the area builds the **identical LSDB**.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: TCP State Transition (Connection Lifecycle)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> LISTEN : Server opens socket
    LISTEN --> SYN_RCVD : Receive SYN
    CLOSED --> SYN_SENT : Client sends SYN
    SYN_SENT --> SYN_RCVD : Receive SYN plus ACK
    SYN_RCVD --> ESTABLISHED : Receive final ACK
    SYN_SENT --> ESTABLISHED : Receive SYN plus ACK and send ACK
    ESTABLISHED --> FIN_WAIT_1 : Client sends FIN
    ESTABLISHED --> CLOSE_WAIT : Server receives FIN
    CLOSE_WAIT --> LAST_ACK : Server sends FIN
    FIN_WAIT_1 --> FIN_WAIT_2 : Receive ACK
    FIN_WAIT_2 --> TIME_WAIT : Receive FIN and send ACK
    LAST_ACK --> CLOSED : Receive ACK
    TIME_WAIT --> CLOSED : Wait 2 times MSL timeout
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: TCP Congestion Control State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    init[Connection Initiated] --> ss[Slow Start cwnd starts at 1 MSS]
    ss --> check{cwnd less than ssthresh?}
    check -->|YES| ss
    check -->|NO| ca[Congestion Avoidance cwnd increases by 1 MSS per RTT]
    ca --> to[Timeout Detected]
    to --> set1[Set ssthresh equals cwnd divided by 2]
    set1 --> rst1[Reset cwnd to 1 MSS]
    rst1 --> ss
    ca --> dupack[3 Duplicate ACKs Received]
    dupack --> fr[Fast Retransmit immediately resend lost segment]
    fr --> set2[Set ssthresh equals cwnd divided by 2]
    set2 --> frec[Fast Recovery cwnd equals ssthresh plus 3 MSS]
    frec --> ca
```

### 4.4 Logical Schematic: Dijkstra's Set Evolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step0[Step 0 N equals A] --> step1[Step 1 Add C cost 1]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2 Add B cost 3]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3 Add E cost 4]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4 Add D cost 6]
    step4 --> done[All Nodes Settled in N]

    step0 --- dist0[D equals 0,4,1,8,Inf]
    step1 --- dist1[D equals 0,3,1,7,4]
    step2 --- dist2[D equals 0,3,1,7,4]
    step3 --- dist3[D equals 0,3,1,6,4]
    step4 --- dist4[D equals 0,3,1,6,4]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Distance Vector Routing and Link State Routing. List any two advantages of LSR.**

**Model Answer:**
- *Distance Vector Routing (DVR)* uses the **Bellman-Ford algorithm**; each router shares its **entire routing table** with its **directly connected neighbours only**. The router has **no global view** of the network. Examples: RIP, IGRP.
- *Link State Routing (LSR)* uses **Dijkstra's SPF algorithm**; each router shares **link-state packets (LSPs)** describing its **own local links** with **all routers in the area** via reliable flooding, giving every router a **complete identical topology map**. Examples: OSPF, IS-IS.
- **Two advantages of LSR:** (i) **Fast convergence** due to immediate LSP flooding on topology change; (ii) **Loop-free paths** guaranteed by SPF computation; (iii) Supports **hierarchical area design** (OSPF).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question Paper]**

**Q2. Explain the purpose of the three-way handshake in TCP. Why is a two-way handshake insufficient?**

**Model Answer:**
The **three-way handshake** establishes a **reliable, synchronised, full-duplex connection** between a client and a server by mutually agreeing on **initial sequence numbers (ISNs)** and confirming bidirectional reachability.
- *Step 1:* Client sends **SYN** with its ISN $x$.
- *Step 2:* Server replies with **SYN + ACK**, providing its ISN $y$ and acknowledging $x+1$.
- *Step 3:* Client sends **ACK** acknowledging $y+1$.

A **two-way handshake** is insufficient because the server cannot verify that the client received its SYN-ACK; a delayed, duplicated old SYN could otherwise open a **half-open or stale connection**, leading to data corruption.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm with the help of a suitable example. Compute the shortest paths from source node **A** to all other nodes in the graph given below. **(7 Marks)**

| Edge | Cost | Edge | Cost |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| A-B | 4 | B-E | 5 |
| A-C | 1 | C-D | 6 |
| A-D | 8 | C-E | 3 |
| B-C | 2 | D-E | 2 |

**Model Solution:**
- Dijkstra maintains two sets: $N$ (settled) and $D(v)$ (tentative distance from source).
- Initialise $N=\{A\}$, $D[A]=0$, $D[B]=4$, $D[C]=1$, $D[D]=8$, $D[E]=\infty$.
- **Iter 1:** Add $C$ (cost 1). Relax: $D[B]=\min(4,1+2)=3$, $D[D]=\min(8,1+6)=7$, $D[E]=\min(\infty,1+3)=4$.
- **Iter 2:** Add $B$ (cost 3). Relax: $D[E]=\min(4,3+5)=4$ (no change).
- **Iter 3:** Add $E$ (cost 4). Relax: $D[D]=\min(7,4+2)=6$.
- **Iter 4:** Add $D$ (cost 6). All settled.
- **Result:** A$\to$B: 3 (A-C-B), A$\to$C: 1, A$\to$D: 6 (A-C-E-D), A$\to$E: 4 (A-C-E).

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **TCP segment header format**. List the functions of any **five fields**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**
- A TCP segment consists of a 20-byte mandatory header followed by options and data.
- **Five key fields:**
  1. **Source Port (16 bits):** Identifies the sender's application process.
  2. **Destination Port (16 bits):** Identifies the receiver's application process.
  3. **Sequence Number (32 bits):** Byte-stream offset of the first byte in this segment. Used for in-order reassembly.
  4. **Acknowledgement Number (32 bits):** Indicates the next byte the receiver expects (cumulative ACK).
  5. **Window Size (16 bits):** Specifies how many bytes the receiver is willing to accept — used for **flow control**.
- **Auxiliary fields:** Checksum (16 bits) for end-to-end integrity, Flags (URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN) for control, Urgent Pointer for out-of-band data.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Describe the working of **Link State Routing** with a neat block diagram. Explain the **LSP flooding mechanism** and the role of **sequence numbers**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**
- Each router discovers its neighbours and measures the **cost** of each link.
- It constructs a **Link State Packet (LSP)** containing its Router ID, neighbour list, costs, sequence number, and age.
- The LSP is **flooded reliably** to all routers in the area using a controlled distribution: every router forwards an incoming LSP on all interfaces except the one it came from.
- **Sequence numbers** play a critical role:
  - Each new LSP carries an **incremented sequence number**.
  - Routers keep the **highest sequence number** seen from each originator.
  - **Older or duplicate LSPs are silently dropped** — this prevents loops, stale data, and broadcast storms.
- After flooding, every router has an **identical Link State Database (LSDB)** and independently runs **Dijkstra's algorithm** to build its SPT and routing table.

**(b)** Explain the **four TCP congestion control algorithms**: **Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, Fast Retransmit, and Fast Recovery**. Show the **cwnd vs. RTT** behaviour with a graph. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**
- **Slow Start:** On connection start (or after timeout), $cwnd$ begins at 1 MSS and **doubles per RTT** (exponential growth) until it reaches $ssthresh$.
- **Congestion Avoidance:** Once $cwnd \geq ssthresh$, $cwnd$ grows **linearly** by 1 MSS per RTT.
- **Fast Retransmit:** When the sender receives **3 duplicate ACKs**, it infers a single segment loss and **retransmits immediately** without waiting for the timeout.
- **Fast Recovery:** After fast retransmit, $ssthresh$ is set to $cwnd/2$ and $cwnd$ is set to $ssthresh + 3 \cdot MSS$ (compensating for the 3 segments that left the pipe and triggered the duplicate ACKs). The sender then **enters Congestion Avoidance directly** (TCP Reno behaviour) instead of restarting Slow Start.
- **Graph:** cwnd rises exponentially (Slow Start), transitions to linear growth (Congestion Avoidance), and shows a sharp drop followed by either a return to Slow Start (Tahoe) or linear growth (Reno) upon packet loss.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in LSR + TCP questions:**
> 1. **Confusing LSP flooding with distance-vector updates.** LSR floods *its own* LSP to *all* routers; DVR sends *its whole table* to *neighbours only*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for mixing these up.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of sequence numbers in LSP flooding.** Many students write "LSPs are flooded" but omit *why* — always mention **sequence numbers prevent loops and stale LSPs**.
> 3. **Miscounting RTTs in Slow Start.** A common error is assuming $cwnd$ doubles every ACK instead of every RTT. Remember: ACKs arrive one-per-segment, but the **RTT-level view** shows doubling.
> 4. **Drawing the three-way handshake with FIN.** FIN is for **teardown**, not handshake. Do not include FIN in your 3-way handshake diagram.
> 5. **Omitting the difference between TCP Tahoe and Reno** in congestion control answers. This is a 3-mark sure-shot question — write one line on it.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Link State Routing** builds a **complete LSDB** at every router using **LSP flooding** and computes routes via **Dijkstra's SPF**.
- Dijkstra's update rule is: $D(v) = \min\{D(v), D(u) + c(u,v)\}$.
- **OSPF** uses a **hierarchical two-level area design** (backbone area 0 + non-backbone areas) for scalability.
- **Sequence numbers in LSPs** are mandatory for loop-free flooding; duplicates and stale LSPs are dropped.
- **TCP is connection-oriented** — established via **3-way handshake** (SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK) and torn down via **4-way FIN exchange**.
- TCP guarantees **in-order, reliable, full-duplex byte-stream delivery** using **sequence numbers, cumulative ACKs, and retransmission timers**.
- **Flow control** uses the **rwnd** (receiver window) announced in TCP headers to prevent the sender from overwhelming the receiver.
- **Congestion control** uses the **cwnd** (congestion window) and reacts to **loss** via four mechanisms: **Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, Fast Retransmit, Fast Recovery**.
- **Tahoe** → always restarts Slow Start after loss. **Reno** → uses Fast Recovery to skip Slow Start.
- The **retransmission timeout** is computed as $Timeout = EstimatedRTT + 4 \cdot DevRTT$.
- A **half-open connection** is a hazard; hence the **3-way handshake** is essential.
- **TIME_WAIT** state of $2 \times MSL$ prevents **delayed segments from old incarnations** from corrupting new connections.
- **RTO calculation** in modern TCPs uses **Jacobson's algorithm** for RTT smoothing and variance tracking.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Congestion Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT416 - PRINCIPLES OF COMMUNICATION TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Congestion Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Congestion** in a data network is formally defined as a state in which the **demand for network resources (bandwidth, buffer space, and processor cycles) exceeds the available capacity** of the network at a given point in time. **Congestion Control** is the set of mechanisms, algorithms, and protocols employed by the network and the end-hosts to prevent congestion from occurring, to actively mitigate it when it occurs, and to recover gracefully once the network returns to a stable state.

The phenomenon was formally characterized by **John Nagle in 1984** (RFC 896), who observed that undelimited packet bursts could cause **congestion collapse** — a state where the throughput of the network drops to near zero despite heavy offered load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PERAT416 Module 4**
> The official module 4 syllabus maps Congestion Control as a part of the Data Link and Network Layer quality-of-service framework. Students must distinguish between **Flow Control** (end-to-end, receiver-paced) and **Congestion Control** (network-wide, traffic-paced).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Analogy

Imagine a **6-lane national highway** converging into a **2-lane toll plaza**.

- **Cars** = Packets / Datagrams
- **Lanes** = Link Bandwidth (Mbps)
- **Toll booths** = Routers / Switches with finite buffer queues

When traffic is light, cars flow smoothly. As the **rush hour** begins, cars queue up before the toll booth. The queue grows beyond the waiting area, **spills onto the main highway**, and eventually **blocks incoming traffic from the city itself**. The result: more cars arrive, fewer cars exit, the system locks up.

**Congestion Control** is the equivalent of:
- **Traffic police** diverting flow (backpressure signals).
- **Ramp meters** limiting how many cars enter the highway (admission control / leaky bucket).
- **Variable speed limit signs** smoothing bursts (traffic shaping / token bucket).
- **GPS rerouting apps** (TCP fast retransmit / fast recovery) that redirect once a jam is detected.

## 1.3 Core Metrics in Congestion Engineering

| Metric | Standard Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Offered Load | **bps (bits/sec)** | Total traffic injected by all sources |
| Throughput | **bps** | Useful data successfully delivered per second |
| Goodput | **bps** | Application-level useful data (excludes retransmissions) |
| Delay (Latency) | **seconds (s)** | Time from packet creation to reception |
| Jitter | **seconds (s)** | Variation in inter-packet delay |
| Packet Loss Ratio | **dimensionless (%)** | $\frac{\text{Lost Packets}}{\text{Transmitted Packets}}$ |
| Queue Occupancy | **packets or bytes** | Number of packets buffered at a router |

> [!NOTE]
> **Performance Knee and Cliff** — When load crosses the *knee*, delay rises sharply; when it crosses the *cliff*, throughput collapses. All congestion control algorithms aim to keep the operating point **at or below the knee**.

## 1.4 Why Congestion Control is Non-Trivial

The **Internet is a best-effort, distributed, heterogeneous, and autonomous** system:
- No single entity owns the entire path.
- RTT (Round-Trip Time) varies from **1 ms (LAN) to 600 ms (geostationary satellite)**.
- Buffers, link speeds, and policies differ at every hop.
- Sources can be **malicious, buggy, or simply over-eager**.

Hence, congestion control must be **distributed, adaptive, fair, and scalable**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Causes of Congestion (Layered Causes)

Congestion is rarely caused by a single factor. KTU examiners expect a **three-tiered cause analysis**:

1. **Bursty Input Traffic** — Multiple sources statistically align their transmissions (synchronization effect), creating instantaneous peaks far exceeding the mean.
2. **Insufficient Router Resources** — Slow processor (long queuing discipline processing), small buffer queues, or low-bandwidth output links.
3. **Mismatch of Speed and Timing** — When $\sum_{i=1}^{N} R_{\text{in},i} > R_{\text{out}}$ of a router, the queue grows unboundedly. The queuing theory threshold is:
   - **Stable if** $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} < 1$, where $\lambda$ = arrival rate and $\mu$ = service rate.
   - **Unstable (exploding queue) if** $\rho \geq 1$.

## 2.2 Effects of Congestion (Cascading Degradation)

- **Increased queuing delay** — Packets wait longer in router buffers.
- **Buffer overflow → packet loss** — Tail-drop or RED drop.
- **Retransmission storms** — Lost packets trigger retransmits, worsening the load.
- **Reduced goodput** — Throughput rises but goodput falls (wasted bandwidth on retransmissions).
- **Congestion collapse** — Maximum throughput scenario where almost no useful data is delivered.

## 2.3 Flow Control vs Congestion Control (Frequently Asked Distinction)

| Parameter | Flow Control | Congestion Control |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | **End-to-end** (single sender–receiver pair) | **Network-wide** (all flows sharing a link) |
| Objective | Prevent receiver buffer overflow | Prevent router buffer / link overload |
| Signaled By | Receiver (ACK window) | Router (choke packet) or inferred by sender (loss) |
| Mechanism | Sliding window (e.g., 1 byte to 16 KB) | AIMD, slow start, RED, leaky/token bucket |
| OSI Layer | Data Link & Transport | Network & Transport |

## 2.4 Taxonomy of Congestion Control Mechanisms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 4 specifically asks for **Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop** and **Traffic Shaping vs Choke Packet** mechanisms. Master this classification.

### A. Open-Loop Congestion Control (Prevention)

Decisions are made **without considering the current state of the network**. Used to *prevent* congestion.

- **Leaky Bucket Algorithm**
- **Token Bucket Algorithm**
- **Admission Control / Traffic Policing**
- **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)**

### B. Closed-Loop Congestion Control (Reaction)

Feedback is used to dynamically adjust behavior.

- **Implicit Feedback** — Sender infers congestion from observed events (e.g., TCP timeout, duplicate ACK).
- **Explicit Feedback** — Router directly informs the sender (e.g., ICMP Source Quench, ECN — Explicit Congestion Notification, Frame Relay BECN/FECN).

### C. Backpressure Mechanism

A hop-by-hop signaling method: if a router's queue is full, it temporarily stops accepting packets from its upstream neighbor, propagating the pressure backward toward the source. Useful in **virtual-circuit networks** and **optical burst switching**.

## 2.5 The Leaky Bucket Algorithm

The classic traffic shaper that converts a **bursty input stream** into a **smooth, constant-rate output stream**.

**Operational Analogy** — A bucket with a small hole at the bottom. Water (packets) poured in irregularly leaks out at a constant rate. If the bucket overflows, water (packets) is **dropped**.

**Parameters:**
- Bucket capacity $= B$ packets.
- Output rate $= \rho$ packets/second.
- Arrival rate $= \lambda(t)$ variable.

**Output:** Always exactly $\rho$ packets per second (or empty intervals).

**Mathematical Behavior:** A continuous-time leaky bucket can be modeled as a **single-server queue with deterministic service time**. The output is a constant bit rate (CBR) stream regardless of input burstiness.

## 2.6 The Token Bucket Algorithm

Unlike the leaky bucket, the token bucket **allows controlled bursts** while still enforcing a long-term average rate.

**Operational Analogy** — Tokens (permission slips) are generated and dropped into a bucket at rate $r$. A packet can be transmitted **only if it consumes a token**. The bucket holds at most $b$ tokens. If the bucket is full, newly generated tokens are discarded.

**Parameters:**
- Token generation rate $= r$ tokens/second.
- Bucket capacity (burst tolerance) $= b$ tokens.
- Maximum burst size $= b$ packets (when bucket is full).
- Long-term average rate $\leq r$.

**Key Difference from Leaky Bucket:**

| Property | Leaky Bucket | Token Bucket |
|---|---|---|
| Output rate | Strictly constant | Constant average, allows bursts up to $b$ |
| Burst handling | Discards bursts | Permits bursts up to bucket size |
| Idle period behavior | Bucket still drains at $\rho$ | Bucket accumulates tokens (up to $b$) |
| Output packet size | Fixed | Can be variable |

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all equations** required to solve any KTU Module 4 Congestion Control numerical problem. Memorize and practice.

| # | Formula / Law | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} < 1$ | Queue stability condition (utilization) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $W = \frac{c}{s}$ | TCP bandwidth-delay product (BDP), $c$ = link capacity, $s$ = RTT | bits |
| 3 | $\text{Window}_{n+1} = \text{Window}_n + 1$ | TCP Slow Start per ACK (exponential growth) | MSS units |
| 4 | $\text{Window}_{n+1} = \text{Window}_n + \frac{1}{\text{Window}_n}$ | TCP Congestion Avoidance (linear / AIMD) | MSS units |
| 5 | $\text{Window}_{\text{new}} = \frac{\text{Window}_{\text{old}}}{2}$ | Multiplicative decrease on loss event | MSS units |
| 6 | $\text{Throughput} \approx \frac{1.22 \cdot \text{MSS}}{\text{RTT} \cdot \sqrt{p}}$ | **Mathis Formula** (TCP throughput, $p$ = loss probability) | bps |
| 7 | $T_{\text{max burst}} = \frac{b}{r - \lambda_{\text{avg}}}$ | Token bucket max burst duration | seconds |
| 8 | $D_{\text{total}} = D_{\text{prop}} + D_{\text{trans}} + D_{\text{queue}} + D_{\text{proc}}$ | End-to-end delay decomposition | seconds |
| 9 | $\eta = \frac{\text{Goodput}}{\text{Throughput}}$ | Protocol efficiency, $\eta \in [0,1]$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Fairness Index} = \frac{\left(\sum x_i\right)^2}{n \cdot \sum x_i^2}$ | **Jain's Fairness Index** ($x_i$ = throughput of flow $i$) | dimensionless |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Leaky Bucket** is implemented in ATM (Asynchronous Transfer Mode) networks as the **Generic Cell Rate Algorithm (GCRA)** — the foundation of CBR and VBR traffic contracts.
- **Token Bucket** is implemented in **Linux `tc qdisc`** (traffic control queuing discipline) and in **Cisco routers** as the basis for QoS policing.
- **TCP Congestion Control** is the reason the Internet does not collapse: every Web page load, video stream, and SSH session is governed by the AIMD principle.
- **AIMD (Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease)** is provably **convergence-fair** — a foundational result by **Chiu and Jain (1989)**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Queue Stability and the Birth-Death Condition

**Problem Setup:** A router's output link has service rate $\mu$ packets/sec (exponential service). Packets arrive at average rate $\lambda$ packets/sec (Poisson arrivals). Model the queue as an **M/M/1 system**.

**Step 1:** Define the system as a continuous-time Markov chain with states $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ representing the number of packets in the system.

**Step 2:** For a stable equilibrium, the **inflow to state $n$** must equal the **outflow from state $n$** (balance equation):

$$\lambda P_{n-1} + \mu P_{n+1} = (\lambda + \mu) P_n$$

**Step 3:** Solve this recurrence with the boundary condition $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} P_n = 1$. The steady-state probability is the **geometric distribution**:

$$P_n = (1 - \rho) \cdot \rho^n, \quad \text{where } \rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$$

**Step 4:** The mean number of packets in the system (Little's Law applied to M/M/1) is:

$$E[N] = \frac{\rho}{1 - \rho} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu - \lambda}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation:
- As $\rho \to 1^{-}$, $E[N] \to \infty$ — the queue length **diverges** (congestion).
- For the queue to remain bounded, **the arrival rate must be strictly less than the service rate**:

$$\lambda < \mu \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \rho < 1$$

**Step 6:** The average queuing delay (waiting time in queue) is:

$$E[W_q] = \frac{E[N]}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{\rho}{\mu(1-\rho)}$$

This derivation proves that **congestion is mathematically inevitable** if the utilization $\rho$ approaches 1. Congestion control mechanisms aim to keep $\rho$ well below unity (typically $\rho \leq 0.7$ in engineered networks).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — TCP Throughput (Mathis Formula)

**Problem Setup:** Estimate the steady-state throughput of a TCP Reno connection that experiences packet loss with probability $p$ per packet.

**Step 1:** In TCP Reno's congestion avoidance phase, the congestion window (cwnd) follows a **sawtooth pattern** (AIMD).

The cwnd grows by 1 MSS per RTT (additive increase) and is halved (multiplicative decrease) on each loss event.

**Step 2:** Let $W$ be the window size at which a loss occurs. The window then drops to $W/2$ and grows back to $W$ linearly over the next $\frac{W}{2}$ RTTs.

**Step 3:** The total data transmitted in one congestion epoch (one sawtooth cycle) is the area under the curve:

$$A_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{W}{2} + W \right) \cdot \frac{W}{2} = \frac{3 W^2}{8} \text{ MSS}$$

**Step 4:** The number of packets transmitted per cycle is approximately $\frac{3W^2}{8}$.

**Step 5:** Relate $W$ to the loss probability $p$. For a single loss event, roughly $\frac{1}{p}$ packets are transmitted per loss:

$$\frac{3 W^2}{8} \approx \frac{1}{p} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad W \approx \sqrt{\frac{8}{3p}}$$

**Step 6:** The throughput $B$ (in MSS per RTT) is approximately:

$$B \approx \frac{1}{\text{RTT}} \cdot \frac{W}{2} = \frac{W}{2 \cdot \text{RTT}}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $W$:

$$B \approx \frac{1}{2 \cdot \text{RTT}} \sqrt{\frac{8}{3p}} = \frac{1}{\text{RTT}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2}{3p}}$$

**Step 8:** Refining with empirical constant $1.22$ (Mathis et al., 1997) to account for TCP's ACK clocking and delayed-ACK effects:

$$\boxed{B \approx \frac{1.22 \cdot \text{MSS}}{\text{RTT} \cdot \sqrt{p}}}$$

**Interpretation:** TCP throughput is **inversely proportional to $\sqrt{p}$**. A tenfold reduction in packet loss (e.g., 1% → 0.1%) yields a $\sqrt{10} \approx 3.16 \times$ throughput increase.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Token Bucket Burst Tolerance

**Problem Setup:** A token bucket has rate $r$ tokens/sec, capacity $b$ tokens, and a stream of packets with average rate $\lambda_{\text{avg}}$.

**Step 1:** During an idle period of duration $T_{\text{idle}}$, the bucket accumulates tokens at rate $r$ until it is full (capacity $b$):

$$T_{\text{idle, max}} = \frac{b}{r}$$

**Step 2:** When the burst begins, the bucket can sustain the burst for an additional duration. The available tokens are $b$, and the net rate at which tokens are consumed during the burst is $(\lambda_{\text{burst}} - r)$ tokens/sec, assuming $\lambda_{\text{burst}} > r$.

**Step 3:** Solving for the maximum burst duration $T_{\text{max burst}}$:

$$b = (\lambda_{\text{burst}} - r) \cdot T_{\text{max burst}}$$

$$T_{\text{max burst}} = \frac{b}{\lambda_{\text{burst}} - r}$$

**Step 4:** If we express $\lambda_{\text{burst}}$ in terms of the **peak-to-average ratio**, we get:

$$\boxed{T_{\text{max burst}} = \frac{b}{r - \lambda_{\text{avg}}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A larger bucket capacity $b$ allows **larger tolerable bursts** at the cost of more variable traffic. This is the central trade-off in traffic shaping design.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of Congestion Algorithms

| Algorithm | Layer | Open/Closed Loop | Burst Handling | Complexity | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Leaky Bucket | Any | Open-loop | Discards bursts | Low (counter + timer) | ATM GCRA, classical textbooks |
| Token Bucket | Any | Open-loop | Allows bursts up to $b$ | Low (counter + timer) | Linux `tc`, Cisco QoS, policing |
| TCP Slow Start | Transport | Closed-loop (implicit) | Probes for bandwidth | Medium (cwnd state) | All TCP variants |
| TCP AIMD | Transport | Closed-loop (implicit) | Maintains equilibrium | Medium | Tahoe, Reno, BIC, CUBIC |
| RED (Random Early Detection) | Network | Closed-loop (explicit) | Probabilistic early drop | Medium (queue avg) | Router AQM |
| ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification) | Network | Closed-loop (explicit) | Marks instead of drops | Low (2-bit header) | Modern data center TCP (DCTCP) |
| Backpressure (Hop-by-Hop) | Data Link / Network | Closed-loop | Per-hop suspension | High (per-VC state) | Virtual-circuit networks, OBS |
| Choke Packet (ICMP Source Quench) | Network | Closed-loop (explicit) | Sender rate reduction | Low | Legacy, deprecated |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Question:** A router has an output link of capacity $C = 1$ Mbps. The average packet size is $L = 1000$ bytes. Packets arrive at rate $\lambda = 100$ packets/sec. Determine:
- (a) The service rate $\mu$ in packets/sec.
- (b) The utilization $\rho$.
- (c) Whether the queue is stable.
- (d) The average number of packets in the system.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Service time per packet:

$$\text{Service time} = \frac{L}{C} = \frac{1000 \text{ bytes}}{1 \text{ Mbps}} = \frac{8000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bits/sec}} = 8 \text{ ms}$$

Service rate:

$$\mu = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = 125 \text{ packets/sec}$$

**Part (b):** Utilization:

$$\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{100}{125} = 0.8$$

**Part (c):** Since $\rho = 0.8 < 1$, the queue is **stable** but heavily loaded (80% utilization — dangerously close to the cliff).

**Part (d):** Mean number of packets in the system:

$$E[N] = \frac{\rho}{1 - \rho} = \frac{0.8}{0.2} = 4 \text{ packets}$$

If $\lambda$ increased to 124 packets/sec, $\rho = 0.992$ and $E[N] = 124$ packets — congestion has set in.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Leaky Bucket Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Burst Arrival] --> B{Packet Count equals Bucket Capacity}
    B -- No --> C[Add Packet to Bucket Queue]
    B -- Yes --> D[Discard Incoming Packet]
    C --> E[Leak Output at Constant Rate rho packets per sec]
    D --> F[Increment Overflow Counter]
    E --> G[Output Stream is Smooth CBR]
    F --> H[Notify Source if Policing Enabled]
    G --> I[Forward to Output Link]
    H --> I
```

**Reading the diagram:** A burst of packets enters the leaky bucket. If the bucket has space, packets are queued. Regardless of input rate, the output link drains the bucket at a fixed rate $\rho$. Overflowing packets are dropped.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Token Bucket Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Token Generator at rate r tokens per sec] --> B{Token Bucket Full}
    B -- No --> C[Add Token to Bucket]
    B -- Yes --> D[Discard Newly Generated Token]
    C --> E{Token Available and Packet Waiting}
    E -- Yes --> F[Transmit Packet and Consume One Token]
    E -- No --> G[Packet Waits in Queue]
    F --> H[Output to Network]
    G --> E
    D --> A
    H --> I{More Packets Pending}
    I -- Yes --> E
    I -- No --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** Tokens are generated at rate $r$. A packet can only be transmitted if a token is available. The bucket can accumulate up to $b$ tokens, allowing controlled bursts when the source has been idle.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — TCP Congestion Control State Machine (Tahoe / Reno)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SlowStart
    SlowStart --> CongestionAvoidance: cwnd reaches ssthresh
    SlowStart --> SlowStart: Triple duplicate ACK and Reno Fast Recovery
    CongestionAvoidance --> CongestionAvoidance: ACK received window increases by 1 per window
    CongestionAvoidance --> SlowStart: Timeout ssthresh set to cwnd 2 cwnd set to 1 MSS
    CongestionAvoidance --> FastRecovery: Triple duplicate ACK
    FastRecovery --> CongestionAvoidance: ACK received after half window
    SlowStart --> FastRetransmit: Triple duplicate ACK
    FastRetransmit --> CongestionAvoidance: cwnd halved ssthresh updated
    note right of SlowStart
      Exponential growth
      cwnd doubles every RTT
    end note
    note right of CongestionAvoidance
      Linear growth
      cwnd increases by 1 MSS per RTT
    end note
    note right of FastRecovery
      Reno only
      Avoid full slow start
    end note
```

**Reading the diagram:** TCP begins in **Slow Start** with cwnd = 1 MSS and doubles the window every RTT (exponential growth). On reaching **ssthresh** (slow start threshold), it transitions to **Congestion Avoidance** (linear growth of +1 MSS per RTT). On detecting loss via **timeout**, it resets to Slow Start. On detecting loss via **triple duplicate ACK**, TCP Tahoe halves ssthresh and restarts Slow Start, while TCP Reno halves ssthresh, halves cwnd, and enters **Fast Recovery** without resetting the window to 1.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — End-to-End Congestion Control Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Source_End
        A1[Application Layer]
        A2[TCP Sender cwnd Manager]
    end
    subgraph Network
        B1[Router R1]
        B2[Router R2 with RED AQM]
        B3[Router R3]
    end
    subgraph Receiver_End
        C1[TCP Receiver ACK Generator]
    end
    A1 --> A2
    A2 -- Send Packet --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 -- Drop or Mark Packet --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 -- ACK --> B3
    B3 -- ACK --> B2
    B2 -- ACK --> B1
    B1 -- ACK --> A2
    A2 -- Reduce cwnd on Loss --> A1
    C1 -- Advertised Window --> A2
```

**Reading the diagram:** Data flows from the TCP sender through a chain of routers (one implementing RED) to the receiver. The receiver sends ACKs back through the same path. When the RED-enabled router detects incipient congestion (queue average exceeds a threshold), it either drops a packet probabilistically or marks it (if ECN is enabled). The sender infers congestion from the absence of ACKs or from ECN echo flags and reduces cwnd accordingly. The advertised window from the receiver implements flow control on top of this.

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — The Performance Knee and Cliff Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Load Region] -->|Steady Rise| B[Knee Point]
    B -->|Sharp Delay Increase| C[Cliff Point]
    C -->|Throughput Collapse| D[Congestion Collapse]
    A -- Throughput scales linearly --> A
    B -- Delay shoots up --> B
    D -- Recovery via Congestion Control --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** As offered load increases from zero, throughput rises linearly and delay remains low. At the **knee**, delay begins to grow rapidly. At the **cliff**, throughput collapses because the network spends all its time retransmitting dropped packets. The goal of congestion control is to operate the network at or just below the knee.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between flow control and congestion control. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Flow Control | Congestion Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | End-to-end (point-to-point) | Network-wide (multi-hop) |
| **Purpose** | Prevent the sender from overwhelming the receiver | Prevent the network from being overwhelmed |
| **Signaled by** | Receiver (via advertised window in ACK) | Sender infers from loss / RTT, or router signals |
| **Layer** | Data Link and Transport | Network and Transport |
| **Mechanism** | Sliding window | AIMD, slow start, RED, leaky/token bucket |
| **Example** | TCP receive window `rwnd` | TCP congestion window `cwnd`, RED |

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's tip: Drawing a clear table and giving one **specific example** of each mechanism guarantees the full 3 marks.

### Q2. List the two major categories of closed-loop congestion control techniques. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The two categories are:

1. **Implicit Feedback Congestion Control** — The sender infers the existence of congestion by observing network behavior such as **packet loss, timeout expiration, or increased RTT**. No explicit signal is sent by the router. **Example:** Traditional TCP (Tahoe, Reno) uses duplicate ACKs and timeouts as implicit signals.

2. **Explicit Feedback Congestion Control** — The router or receiver explicitly notifies the sender about congestion. **Example:** ICMP Source Quench packets, Frame Relay BECN/FECN, and **Explicit Congestion Notification (ECN)** in IP/TCP.

A third sub-classification often required by KTU is **backpressure**, a hop-by-hop explicit feedback used in virtual-circuit networks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Leaky Bucket algorithm** for traffic shaping with a neat diagram. How does it differ from the **Token Bucket algorithm**? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **Leaky Bucket algorithm** is an open-loop congestion control mechanism used to convert a bursty input stream of packets into a smooth, constant-rate output stream. It is analogous to a bucket with a small hole at the bottom — water (packets) poured in irregularly leaks out at a constant rate.

**Algorithm Description:**
- The algorithm maintains a **bucket (queue)** of finite capacity $B$ packets.
- Packets arrive at variable rate $\lambda(t)$ and are placed in the bucket.
- Packets are transmitted onto the output link at a **fixed rate $\rho$** packets/second.
- If the bucket is full when a new packet arrives, the packet is **dropped** (overflow).

**Key Properties:**
- Output rate is always **constant** regardless of input burstiness.
- Maximum queue length is bounded by $B$.
- Useful for **traffic policing** and enforcing a peak cell rate in ATM networks.

**Mathematical Model:**

$$\text{Output}(t) = \begin{cases} \rho & \text{if bucket non-empty} \\ 0 & \text{if bucket empty} \end{cases}$$

**Differences from Token Bucket:**

| Property | Leaky Bucket | Token Bucket |
|---|---|---|
| Burst handling | Discards excess | Allows bursts up to bucket size |
| Idle behavior | Continues draining | Accumulates tokens |
| Output rate | Strictly constant | Average constant, peak higher |
| Use case | Smoothing / policing | Shaping / metering |

**Q. (b)** A token bucket has a token generation rate of $r = 4$ tokens/sec and a bucket capacity of $b = 10$ tokens. If the source has been idle for 3 seconds and then sends a burst of packets:
- (i) How many tokens are available when the burst begins? (3 marks)
- (ii) What is the maximum burst duration if the burst arrival rate is $\lambda_b = 12$ packets/sec? (4 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i)** During 3 seconds of idle, tokens accumulate at rate $r = 4$ tokens/sec:

$$N_{\text{tokens}} = r \times t_{\text{idle}} = 4 \times 3 = 12 \text{ tokens}$$

But the bucket capacity is only $b = 10$, so the bucket is full:

$$\boxed{N_{\text{available}} = \min(12, 10) = 10 \text{ tokens}}$$

**(ii)** During the burst, tokens are consumed at the net rate:

$$r_{\text{net}} = \lambda_b - r = 12 - 4 = 8 \text{ tokens/sec consumed}$$

Maximum burst duration:

$$T_{\text{max burst}} = \frac{b}{r_{\text{net}}} = \frac{10}{8} = 1.25 \text{ seconds}$$

Maximum packets sent during burst:

$$N_{\text{burst}} = b + r \cdot T_{\text{max burst}} = 10 + 4 \times 1.25 = 15 \text{ packets}$$

Verification: $12 \times 1.25 = 15$ packets. ✓

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Describe the **TCP Congestion Control** mechanism. Explain **Slow Start** and **Congestion Avoidance** phases with a graph showing cwnd vs RTT. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

TCP Congestion Control is a **closed-loop, end-to-end, implicit-feedback** mechanism introduced by **Van Jacobson in 1988** (RFC 1122, 2581). Each TCP sender maintains a state variable called the **congestion window (cwnd)** that limits the number of unacknowledged bytes in flight.

The sender's effective window is:

$$\text{Effective Window} = \min(\text{cwnd}, \text{rwnd})$$

where `rwnd` is the receiver-advertised flow control window.

**Phase 1 — Slow Start:**
- Initial state: `cwnd = 1 MSS`, `ssthresh = 65535 bytes` (or a configured value).
- For **every ACK received**, cwnd increases by 1 MSS.
- Net effect: cwnd **doubles every RTT** (exponential growth).
- The phase ends when `cwnd` reaches `ssthresh`, or a loss is detected.

Mathematical growth rule:

$$c_{n+1} = c_n + 1 \text{ (per ACK)} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad c_{n+1} = 2 c_n \text{ (per RTT)}$$

**Phase 2 — Congestion Avoidance:**
- Entered when `cwnd ≥ ssthresh`.
- cwnd increases by approximately **1 MSS per RTT** (linear / additive increase).
- Per-ACK growth rule:

$$c_{n+1} = c_n + \frac{1}{c_n} \text{ (per ACK)}$$

- Loss detection:
  - **Timeout:** ssthresh = cwnd / 2; cwnd = 1 MSS; go to Slow Start.
  - **Triple Duplicate ACK (3 dup-ACKs):** ssthresh = cwnd / 2; cwnd = ssthresh (Tahoe) or cwnd = ssthresh + 3 (Reno with Fast Recovery).

**Graphical Behavior (Sawtooth Pattern):**

```
cwnd |
     |              /\        /\
     |             /  \      /  \
     |            /    \    /    \      Linear (Congestion Avoidance)
     |           /      \  /      \    /
     |          /        \/        \  /
     |         /                     \/
     |        /       Exponential (Slow Start)
     |       /
     |______/________________________________
       Time (RTTs) →
```

**Q. (b)** A TCP Reno connection has RTT = 100 ms and MSS = 1 KB. The connection is in congestion avoidance with cwnd = 10 MSS. It then experiences a triple duplicate ACK. (4 marks)
- (i) What is the new ssthresh?
- (ii) What is the new cwnd (assuming Fast Recovery)?
- (iii) How many RTTs are needed to reach cwnd = 20 MSS again?

**Model Answer:**

**(i)** On triple duplicate ACK, ssthresh is set to half the current cwnd:

$$\text{ssthresh}_{\text{new}} = \frac{\text{cwnd}_{\text{old}}}{2} = \frac{10 \text{ MSS}}{2} = 5 \text{ MSS}$$

**(ii)** In TCP Reno's Fast Recovery, cwnd is set to ssthresh + 3 MSS (for the 3 dup-ACKs that have left the pipe):

$$\text{cwnd}_{\text{new}} = 5 + 3 = 8 \text{ MSS}$$

Once a fresh ACK arrives, cwnd is set to ssthresh (deflating the inflation by 3). So steady-state cwnd after Fast Recovery = 5 MSS.

**(iii)** In Congestion Avoidance, cwnd grows by 1 MSS per RTT. To go from 5 MSS to 20 MSS:

$$\Delta c = 20 - 5 = 15 \text{ MSS} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 15 \text{ RTTs}$$

Time required:

$$T = 15 \times 100 \text{ ms} = 1.5 \text{ seconds}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing `cwnd` and `rwnd`:** cwnd is congestion control (sender-side, adaptive); rwnd is flow control (receiver-advertised, fixed by receiver buffer).
> 2. **Slow Start is NOT slow!** Despite the name, it grows **exponentially**. The "slow" refers to the initial start at 1 MSS (compared to sending the full receiver window at once).
> 3. **Tahoe vs Reno confusion:** TCP Tahoe always resets cwnd to 1 MSS on any loss. TCP Reno's Fast Recovery avoids this for triple duplicate ACKs.
> 4. **Token Bucket idle token capping:** Tokens beyond bucket capacity are discarded. Forgetting `min(N, b)` costs a full mark in numericals.
> 5. **Forgetting units:** Always state RTT in seconds and MSS in bytes/bits when plugging into Mathis' formula. Mixing units is the #1 cause of wrong final answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (must-memorize before exam)**

- [ ] **Congestion** = demand exceeds capacity. **Congestion Control** = prevention + mitigation + recovery.
- [ ] **Three Causes:** bursty input, insufficient router resources, speed mismatch ($\rho \geq 1$).
- [ ] **Three Effects:** increased delay, packet loss, throughput/goodput collapse.
- [ ] **Open-loop (prevention):** Leaky Bucket, Token Bucket, Admission Control.
- [ ] **Closed-loop (reaction):** Backpressure, Choke Packet, Implicit (TCP loss detection), Explicit (ECN, BECN/FECN).
- [ ] **Leaky Bucket** = constant output rate $\rho$, no bursts allowed.
- [ ] **Token Bucket** = rate $r$ tokens/sec, capacity $b$, allows bursts up to $b$ packets.
- [ ] **Token Bucket idle time** token accumulation: $N = \min(b, r \cdot t_{\text{idle}})$.
- [ ] **TCP Slow Start:** cwnd starts at 1 MSS, **doubles every RTT** (exponential).
- [ ] **TCP Congestion Avoidance:** cwnd grows **+1 MSS per RTT** (linear / AIMD increase).
- [ ] **On Timeout:** ssthresh = cwnd/2, cwnd resets to 1 MSS, back to Slow Start.
- [ ] **On Triple Dup-ACK:** ssthresh = cwnd/2, cwnd = ssthresh (Tahoe) or cwnd = ssthresh+3 (Reno Fast Recovery).
- [ ] **AIMD** = Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease. Provably fair and convergent (Chiu-Jain 1989).
- [ ] **Mathis Formula:** $B \approx \frac{1.22 \cdot \text{MSS}}{\text{RTT} \cdot \sqrt{p}}$.
- [ ] **RED (Random Early Detection):** Probabilistically drops packets **before** the queue is full, based on a moving average of queue length. Thresholds: $\text{min}_{th}$ and $\text{max}_{th}$.
- [ ] **ECN** = 2 bits in IP header; uses marking instead of dropping when both endpoints support it.
- [ ] **Jain's Fairness Index:** $F = \frac{(\sum x_i)^2}{n \cdot \sum x_i^2} \in [\frac{1}{n}, 1]$.
- [ ] **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP):** $W = C \cdot \text{RTT}$ = max unacknowledged bits in flight for full utilization.
- [ ] **Flow Control vs Congestion Control** — must be on your answer sheet as a comparison table; KTU frequently asks this as a 3-mark question.
